text:
I'm so sad how many people really don't like this place.  During the summers, our family goes here for brunch often on Sunday afternoons.  They make the best french toast I've had anywhere!

The place is family owned and run, and possibly overwhelmed with so much family to care for while running a restaurant, it's rather obvious I agree.  On Sunday afternoons their teenage daughter is often the waitress, she's sweet and shy, the atmosphere is pretty authentic and unique for a Cambridge restaurant.

Eating here is like eating at a friend's house, it's not for everyone, I have personally witnessed the service issues, and I can't disagree it could be better, it also depends on the day.  However, I'm also not usually in a rush when I come, it's a family meal, we come to relax and enjoy ourselves over a good meal.  

The food I've had here makes up for the service and has always been good.  It's probably also the cheapest eat in town, for a table of three we often only spend $10 before tipping (we tip well, it's a family owned business).

Summers are best here, we can enjoy the seating outside, enjoy the breeze on a sunny day, people watch, and be served outside.  I am all for supporting a local family owned and run business.

I ate here several times a few years ago because it was convenient. But the grime, the lackluster and possibly grimy food, and the sullen staff just turned me off. I went back again this week because my husband insisted on trying it. Our tap water came in styrofoam cups... mine was stained with dirt. The service was still sad and the space was still grimy. The falafel was fine but not made with love, and I'm actually just hoping it doesn't make me ill. I don't trust that place.

basically: don't bother, try someplace else
Went with four people and order the veg/meat combo that sells per person. The food was not that tasty, injera lacking flavor, and the price is exorbitant for the quality/quantity of food. I've had way better in Philly, tucsonArizona and other places in Boston. 
Won't come here again unless someone else is paying and I'm having a craving for bland ethiopian

I've lived within spitting distance of this place for ~3 years and just ventured in here for the first time about a month ago. What the hell was I waiting for?  This place has some delicious food. I've eaten here 4 times in the last month and have enjoyed everything I have tried. The Tom Yum soup is some of the best I've had, so spicy and delicious, and they don't skimp on the shrimp like they do in some places. Fresh rolls and veggie rolls both quite tasty, but my favorite app thus far is the stuffed wings. Who knew you could stuff a chicken wing with even more chicken and some shrimp and make something so delicious? Maybe it's my fascination with meat stuffed with meat that explains part of my love for this dish. I usually like my Thai noodley, and the noodley dishes I've had have been good: Pad Thai, Pad See Ew, and Rad-Na. Not quite as good as Similans, which is why they only get 4 stars. I also noticed on their menu that they charge extra for rice. I hate that...just charge me $1 more for my meal, give me the rice, and make me feel like I'm getting it for free. You know I want it, everyone wants rice, just don't make me feel like I'm getting ripped off for ordering it.

As for service, I've only had take out, but the staff has been quite awesome. Each time I've gone in, the place has been quite busy, but they still manage extreme politeness on the phone and in person. I look forward to actually eating in one day.

This place is really close to the Porter Square T stop, so I would recommend taking the subway instead of trying to park in the neighborhood.
Great service and wonderful Italian food.  My wife and I stop here for the bolognese and it never disappoints us.

I wouldn't say the food was amazing, but it wasn't terrible. I have been a few times now and ultimately have enjoyed the experience. The music is always good and I have always gotten Robert as my waiter and he is awesome. He remembers me and my friends, what I've liked and dislike, and goes out of his way to do anything he can to make our experience better.

Suan la Chow Show is what we came for. And it will always be one of our favorite dishes. We have tried (as have others), to reverse engineer the recipe. We can get close, but Mary is holding the recipe close!!! Delicious!!!

Noticed this awesome little joint on my bike commute to work. I planned a get together with some friends for a special occasion and our experience blew us out of the water. The people we so friendly, customer service was amazing, the food was incredible and the drink selection was top notch and perfect for a large group of people with differing tastes! I highly recommend their burgers! They were delicious! To top the evening off, they even brought us desserts on the house! I will definitely be making this a regular choice for dinner and drinks! Thank you Hops-n-scotch for making our evening so memorable!
Spicy miso ramen is yummy. Not so spicy, either (at least by my taste). This place has a nice vibe too... people just chilled and enjoying a good meal without any pretense.

I was reluctant to move away from Cambridge in part because of this restaurant! And in all fairness, Hingham (capital of multiculturalism and diversity as we know it) is not exactly teeming with ethnic food options. You cannot imagine my joy when by a quirk of fate I am now working back in my old Porter Square hood and can go to this restaurant without having to travel by spaceship over light year distances. 

Not that this restaurant isn't worth the drive. It is consistently great, the staff is consistently friendly, and the Thai Iced Tea is fantastic. They've got really nice healthy options on the menu including steamed veggies with brown rice and peanut sauce on the side. Their pot stickers are a dream. But what I always order here is the pad thai. For some reason, there's something about the way they make it that always induces me to eat the whole thing (against my better judgment). Just once, I'd like to order something other than the pad thai, but it's so good that the memory of other meals sucks me in and I always order the same thing. Friends have been very satisfied with their curries, though, so those seem to be winners as well. Also, it seems that they've recently taken to providing a free soup of the day with meal orders which is a really nice touch. The other day it was a chicken hot and sour soup that was absolutely delicious. 

You rarely have to wait here- it's big enough for families and they have a large amount of seating. Don't take the lack of wait as indicative of the food- during the day it tends to be quiet in Porter (with the exception of Boca Grande which is generally a zoo). They're big enough to accomodate and actually have enough wait staff to turn the tables over (I know, shock, right?) So go to Tamarind. You really won't regret it. I feel like this place is Spice Thai's sad sister that just wants some attention- everyone gives Spice such props, but honestly, I still think this place is better. At least for Pad Thai.

Decorated with a modern retro twist, Shabu Ya is certainly a good place to go when you are in need of a fix but it all comes at a price. While I think the quality of Shabu Ya is just as good as Shabu Zen, I think that the portions are slightly smaller and the price, quite a bit higher. 

We started off with some sushi and sashimi as appetizers- catepillar maki, 1 order of unagi sushi, and 3 orders of salmon sashimi. Overall the sushi was decent but I found that the cuts of salmon weren't as good or fresh as I had expected them to be. 

As for the hotpot portion of our meal, I ordered the vegetable delight and lamb combo. The vegetable delight could have been bigger and I feel like there should have been a larger assortment of items. It only consisted of three types of mushrooms, one type of tofu, broccoli, a piece of corn, carrots, and three types of greens. During the end of our meal, our waiter brought over a cart of vegetables (which I didn't know would happen) and asked if we had wanted more. I guess this must be the unlimited vegetables that everyone keeps talking about. I just wish that this had been brought to our attention sooner.

In regards to service, I think that the service here was the best that I have ever received at an Asian restaurant, much less a Shabu restaurant. Usually you are left to fend for yourself after the food as reached your table, but our server was attentive and not at all overbearing.

I have only gotten take-out or delivery from MuLan so can't comment about service or atmosphere. The fried rice is delicious and the other dishes I've ordered were very good. Dumplings look like they are made at the restaurant. The food in general seems less Americanized than at other Chinese restaurants. It definitely has better quality ingredients than some.

I have to say I'm surprised (1) this place has managed to stay open this long (2) they haven't made any changes to the food despite the many bad reviews. So I'll add mine to that list and say absolutely avoid this place if you're looking for dosa. You could instead buy the dosa batter from the store that's in front and DIY. Another option is Dosa and Curry in Somerville which is not fantastic but still much better. 
Dosa Factory's version of dosa is not what a dosa should taste like and really there is nothing good on that plate - the dosa, chutney and sambar are all wayyy subpar.

We sat at the bar for dinner and drinks.  An impressive beer menu and very nice bartenders. The food was pretty generic, so I will not be returning for that.  Even the waitstaff was eating pizza from All-star pizza bar next door.

Will the real Mary Chung please stand up?

The reason I ask...after reading many reviews that say the spicy dishes are extremely spicy,  we deliberated a while before deciding to take the plunge and order one mild and one spicy dish.

We started out with wonton soup and were quite impressed not only with the size of the wontons but with the quantity.  Not only  that but they were quite tasty, too.

For the mild main course, we chose Moo Shi Pork.  I was going to order Moo Shi Chicken but the waitress said it had all dark meat in it.  The Moo Shi was very flavorful and I was impressed with the amount of pancakes that come with the dish.  Most places only give about 4 pancakes which are never enough.  My only complaint was that the pancakes were a bit on the dry side.

Now...for the spicy main course.  We ordered Ma Po Tofu (I know it's spelled differently on the menu but forget exactly how).  When it came, we both tried it with some rice.  Yes, it was spicy---spicy enough to make our noses run but not so spicy that we were guzzling water trying to put out the fire!  It struck me as a truly authentic dish.  I have a Sichuan cookbook at home and make this dish sometimes and found that Mary Chung's version was almost exactly like what I make.  Mary Chung's had a good deal of Szechuan peppercorns in it which is quite impressive.  My only complaint was that the tofu was too soft.  I think that a firmer tofu works better in the dish.

So...what's going on here?  Did we go on an off-night, was that why the food wasn't burn-your-mouth spicy?  Or are other yelpers just not used to spicy Chinese food?  Or has Mary Chung's recently started toning down the spiciness?  Or are Mary Chung's spicy dishes more spicy than other local Chinese restaurants' spicy dishes?  So...which is the real Mary Chung?

We would certainly go again next time we're in town.  The food is priced right and  you certainly get your money's worth.  However, even though we went when the place wasn't too busy, waitress service was still on the slow side.  So I wouldn't recommend dining here if you are in a rush.  If you have lots of time to spare, then give it a try!

Friends of mine were having a BBQ around the corner, so after the party died down a bit, some of us relocated here for the after party. 

This place is right up my alley - not too dive-y, but not to SATC either. The bartender made my pain in the ass espresso martini with a smile and I ran into my colorist. Always a good sign.

The place was pretty full, but the six of us found a corner with stools near the DJ booth. Overall, a nice vibe, friendly service and the food they were taking to the diners looked extremely appetizing. A child of advertising, I buckled and got fries. Also very good.

I will def be back. This place would please a wide range of people, and if not, Newtowne and Temple Bar are nearby as plan B's.

The rosemary sauce is killer...The toppings are fresh and tasty...There is only one thing missing, a decent crust. The crust can be described as more of a cracker or pita consistency rather than a hand-tossed or even thin-crust dough. Change the crust and this place could easily go from OK to amazing.

The quality of the meats here are of particularly poor quality.  The two times I've gone I have been literally injured by my food (razor sharp chicken bones hidden in my supposedly boneless meat). The veggies have always seemed rather squishy in a way that doesn't make me too confident about their freshness.

When they mean spicy they REALLY mean spicy! For some dishes the spice is great, and as a spicy food lover I really enjoyed it. However, there are some dishes here that are frankly terrible because any flavor is completely offset by how spicy they make it. If you are sensitive to spicy food I suggest you go elsewhere almost everything here has a bit of bite to it.

Chinatown may be a bit of a hike but it is worth it to find much cheaper, fresher, and better balanced food.

best ramen ever!! so many choices, huge bowls and so cheap!

after you're done, walk down just a little to grab some mochi ice cream and then make another stop by the japanese food market to grab some pocky, and if you're still hungry- get some sushi all under one building.

I'll say it: it's not what it used to be.
New menus, new prices,... less exceptional food?

I would give this place a 3.5 stars(but no halvies on yelp!) .  The restaurant is located on the second floor above Wagamama in the Harvard Square area.  At first service was unimpressionable, but luckily we got a male waiter who was very attentive and quick.  The decor is not my favorite part of the place - brightly colored furniture of pinks, greens, blues, and orange.  It looks like a frozen yogurt shop, but I guess to each his own.  

Food - We started off with a salmon avocado roll.  Tasted fine, nothing to rave about.  Next, we shared Shabu shabu of the beef short ribs with regular beef broth and stone pot bibimbap with pork.  The sauce that comes with the bibimbap is spicy which we both liked.  The meat for the shabu tasted flavorful and fresh.  The broth is mildly favored(almost bordering bland) but it tastes better after you cook some pieces of meat in it.  The portions were enough for both to share and we were both stuffed.  

We used a Groupon which made the meal quite affordable.  The shabu shabu was $18 for the beef short ribs, which came with broth to cook in, noodles, and assorted vegetables.  It was $13 for the stone pot bibimibap pork.  

Overall we would come here again.  We saw that they have a buffet on the weekends.

I can't speak to friendliness/quality of waitstaff, as I've only done takeout here, but the food is very good.  It's pricey (18 bucks for a pizza is a lot, I feel), but you're getting a pizza in Cambridge, and I've not found this good for less.  The ingredients are fresh, which is huge when you're doing pizza IMO.  I've also had their BLT, which uses VERY good bacon and bread.  The artichoke/sweet potato pizza is a fave of mine, and the thin crust is something I'm a fan of.  I'd recommend trying this place out, but be warned that it's small and seating is intimate.

I didn't actually have shabu shabu, so I feel a bit disingenuous giving this spot 5 stars, but WE ATE IN A PRIVATE ROOM!  And it was private because it had hanging metal chains.  It doesn't get much more awesome.

What I did eat was the Red Sox maki and it was delish.  Seriously, seriously delish.

I ate here with someone who works in the building (and is therefore a frequent lunch patron) and his wife, and at one point, she made an observation that summed up pretty well what was putting me off from Tavern in the Square: "It's like a smaller Cheesecake Factory."

And that's the problem. It was that sui-generis feeling that makes The Factory feel so ridiculous, sterile, and put on (apologies to any Cheesecake Factory devotees). It's like the kid you meet your freshman year of college who has completely overhauled his wardrobe, and while everything he's added to it is technically good looking, everything matches just a little too well, it's all a little too composed, and he doesn't quite know how to wear it.

The menu is slightly too large, without clear focus (an entire section for burritos? really?), and featuring the same kind of post-ethnic dishes paired with down-home favorites that you might expect at any upper-end "American-food" restaurant. If someone told me that I was disappointed because I hadn't ordered the right thing (and sorry, but lactose intolerance means no truffle mac n' cheese for me), I would believe them, but without an organizational push to what's good, or at least to what kind of meal -- sandwich? entree with sides? Italian? Mexican? Asian? -- I'll remain a skeptic.

Now, to be fair, it isn't that bad. I had a burger which was entirely passable, the other dinners we had looked fine. The beer selection was actually surprisingly decent, and the seasonal flight (heffeweisens now) looked interesting. Service was prompt, even when it started filling up, although I hear that slips a bit when the bar is super crowded (later weekend nights, trivia night, big sports events). And maybe most importantly, it fills a niche in Porter that hadn't been served before. It's casual, but not somewhere to be embarrassed bringing a date. It's got a touch of class, but won't break your wallet. It's also fairly high capacity, which I'm sure is good for when it gets busier. All in all, I think I would usually choose nearby Christopher's over it, but that's me, and some people might dig the more put-together, more intentional, new-and-improved-freshman feel here.

Along with being a busy college football bar (along with random screams and cheers), this place actually has a fantastic craft beer list. 

Hopheads should get Wookey Jack. For more subtle hop flavors and Belgian drinkability, Lost Abbey's Devotion is fantastically refreshing.

The Monterey Chicken sandwich had well charred grilled chicken. The onion rings are light and fluffy.

its just too easy to walk there from 77 mass ave after a long hard day (of the usual dosage of neglect by your advisor) and order the sweet potato pizza with goat cheese, mozzarella, and a bunch of other stuff i cant remember right now. their carafe'd wines are cheap and good enough to pair with the pizza. my only wish is that they offered dessert, because there isn't a dessert place nearby. well you could walk to the nearby 7/11, but a house tiramisu would be so much better.

This is a hidden gem! It's located in a residential neighborhood, and we only found out about it while researching Italian places in Cambridge, having decided to venture out from the nearby Harvard Square establishments. Gran Gusto is a fairly large space, and there seemed to be multiple groups celebrating birthdays. I ordered the seafood stew, and it was so delicious that even though the portion was huge I finished every last bite! There was shellfish, shrimp, scallops, and a piece of salmon. The tomato broth was amazing. The dish also came with two pieces of toasted bread, brushed with olive oil. The pasta dishes also looked really good! I hope to come back!

This place is very, very tasty and they DO have a liquor license. In fact, their wine selection is pretty good and reasonably priced to boot! They make excellent soups, the spring rolls are fantastic, and the service is always very nice. Slightly more dependable than Sugar and Spice, though I gave them 4 stars as well. Really depends on what you're in the mood for.

After trying one of the Chinatown shabu shabu restaurants, I wanted to introduce more friends to this delicious, fun, and healthy style of cooking. After reading some reviews and having walked and driven by both of the Harvard Square establishments, I set my sights on Shabu-Ya. 

The decor is bright, modern, clean, and cheery. Often times with restaurants in the city you feel squished but this place is very open and spacious feeling. I love that the restaurant has different sections without actually feeling sectioned off. There's a bar and lounge area, a sushi bar, a round island for singles hot pot, and booths; but nothing actually seperating these areas. 

Our server was very friendly, and did an excellent job explaining how Japanese hot pot meals work, and also giving us an idea of how long each of the vegetables (which he identified for us in case we weren't quite sure) should be cooked. I appreciated this very much as my last shabu experience did not have the same type of explaination.

I ordered the kimchee broth and my dining partner ordered the tom yum. The kimchee was very good, just spicy enough, and just an overall great flavor. My friend was also very pleased with her choice. 

The meat and vegetables were all very fresh. 

I didn't try the sushi but they had a great menu. I'll definitly be back for more shabu and to try the sushi.

My wife and I have gone to Dhosa Factory in the past and been satisfied with the dhosas (hence the two stars and not one). But recently we had friends visiting and ordered full entres instead for takeout. Not only was the food very mediocre (a trend for many Indian restaurants in the area), but it made us all sick. I will not be eating there again, not even for the dhosas.

Went here for dinner last night with my boyfriend. He had dined here earlier last week with a friend and has not stopped talking about their Falafel. (we met at a falafel hut)...anyways, we walk in and we are the only people there other then the family who I guess runs it? We ordered hummus, babganoush, falafel, a lentil soup, a chicken kebab entree, a tabbouleh wrap and 2 bottled waters...AMAZING!!!!!!!!!!!! Best part--$28!!!!!!! I understand peoples issues with the menu's, mismatching plates, and the decor but I thought it made it all the more authentic. Not sure about having to leave the place to use the bathroom (it is around back and through a gray door? I decided to hold it...) 
I would consider myself a Middle Eastern foodie/snob.  Being a vegetarian, it is very easy to eat.  I usually go for Steve's Greek but I think I have found a new contender.  Another perk?? Toscanni's is a short walk! :)

Grabbed lunch here after a doctors appointment, and were sad to note that the food didn't taste like it's typical delicious self! We ordered general tso's chicken, pan fried dumplings, and egg rolls. The dumplings themselves and egg rolls were fantastic, but the sauce that came with tasted really odd. I would say it tasted smokey, when usually it's a soy/vinegar/garlic/red pepper concoction.

The general tso's was very different. Usually one bite sendings me running for the water (which my boyfriend enjoys...?) but the spice wasn't there at all. It's main taste was "sweet", and didn't hold the spicy depth it usually does.

I'm not sure if it was a different chef, new owners, or just an off day, but we're loyal customer's so we'll be back at some point. Hopefully we get the same delicious quality as usual!

I went here this past weekend for my birthday dinner. I've been wanting to try Ethiopian for awhile, but, this being my first time, I have absolutely no point of comparison, unlike some of the other reviewers.

The five of us split a bottle of wine. I'm surprised that a previous poster said they charged everyone by the glass--they were out of our original choice, so the server suggested a South African red and brought the bottle out to us.

Only one of us had ever eaten there before, and he suggested we just get whatever combination the server offered. We went for the recommended combination, with a slight variation; since we had three vegetarians out of five diners, the server offered to reverse the ratio of meat to veggie dishes. The food arrived on a giant platter, piled in sections on top of a spongy bread. We also were given a plate with individual pieces of this bread piled on it to tear apart and scoop our food up with.

Everything was delicious. A few things, like the spinach, were pretty much spice-free, but they were great mixed with a little of the spicy sauce or another dish.

This definitely would not be for the less adventurous or more germ-phobic eater (although they do give you hot towels to clean your hands prior to bringing out your dinner, and wetnaps with the check).

I really loved the food here! Best Thai food in the area (and as other yelpers pointed out, this is a less distinguishing title than you'd think)

This became a place where I frequented with my ex-gf because it was cheap, and delicious every time. 

For me, no dish surpassed either the Panang Curry or the Pad See Ewe... v. friendly service, and no need to make reservations in advance.

Immediately regretted eating at this place after sitting down. The place is out of date and unsanitary. Once the food came out, we noticed a cockroach on the table. The chicken tikka dosa had little to no flavor. The customer service was not the best either. Overall, a bad dosa experience and bad quality Indian food for the price it cost. Stay away.

If you know what to order, the food is great, otherwise it can be pretty hit or miss.

Our favorites?     House special fish: It's this delicious white fish in this really great special spicy sauce.  Yummmm.  (we're actually going again tonight to have it!)

I would also recommend the green beans and the eggplant dish.  

The last time we were there I tried ordering an egg soup and she said it didn't have pork in it, but.... my husband who doesn't eat pork couldn't go near it.  It didn't taste that great either.  So... now I'm kinda hesitant about ordering other things off the menu.

But it won't stop us from going there to order our favorite dishes!

Great idea.  People love dosas, and most Indian restaurants don't offer South Indian food.  However, these were about as bad as any restaurant dosas I've had.  Dosa Factory is not really a restaurant -- just an addition to the take-out department of an Indian grocery and specialty store. 

The dosa itself was soggy and the filling was skimpy and indifferently prepared.  The service was unfriendly as well.  There are some good Indian restaurants in Cambridge.  Shalimar across the street is better than average, Kebab Factory is superb, so don't bother with Dosa factory.

FIrst of all i am Asian. Secondly i used to live in LA where there are tons of good Thai comfort food. Tamarind would, at best, be a Panda Express back in CA. Typical AMERICANIZED thai food. Everything is freaking unbelievably sweetened to accommodate american taste bud. The only reason it did not receive 1 star is because we ordered delivery, and the whole process was smooth and prompt.
Pretty legit suan la dumplings. The sauce it comes with is what makes it though. It's not spicy at first but after two dumplings you begin to feel the heat and sheepishly sneak a sip of water.

Decent food but consistently unpleasant experience with owners and staff.

I live in the neighborhood, come in here very infrequently because the experience is so irritating, smarmy MIT types not even included.  And they are always there.

Cash only--that's the first tip off.  

One time I bought a large order--$85 and change.  Digging for the change, I figured they'd wave me off and just say thank you.  No, they were going to get that $.31 come hell or high water.

Twice now I've gone in for lunch.  The place is literally 25% full--effectively empty. I order a simple lunch, and before I'm anywhere near done, the wretched waitress is pushing me out of the booth.  "You done? you DONE?"

Lady, there's nobody waiting, and I'm barely halfway done.  As you can plainly see.  Please bugger off and let me finish my damned lunch, will you?  "YOU DONE?"

There.  I feel better now.

Went there with a bit of skepticism after reading some reviews here, but was pleasantly surprised from the start, once I found it.  The waitstaff was very pleasant and friendly.  The price for lunch buffet was under ten bucks, rolls and salads, sushi and bulgogi were all fresh and savory, miso soup was fantastic.

Second time around I took along my daughter, and a friend.  We all loved it, this time ordering shabu shabu and a vegetable sushi (for vegans among us), kim-chi and other salads came free with it.  The friendliness was all around us as we toasted with some French people next to us, and some Korean patrons the table over.  Waiters were always checking on us.

Planning to go again, for lunch, next week.  Thank You, Shabu-Ya.

Food is not bad! Not sure if its authentic or not, but, its tasty.. Its not very spicy, which is what I like, but, my friends always ask for their hot sauce to add to their foods. 

I usually get one or more of their Chicken Satay, Red Curry, Tom Yum soup, Pad Thai, Pad Kee Mao

Enjoyed a very memorable and delicious evening at Hops Test Kitchen and Raw Bar. This was our first visit but won't be our last. The atmosphere was lively, upscale casual and perfect for a relaxed dinner exploring a creative and unique menu with service that is second to none. 

Our server was extremely attentive and knowledgeable about the menu. She made great recommendations from start to finish as this was our first time dining. We enjoyed a great selection of fresh oysters followed by the Chef's tasting menu which was a delight. Each course was unique and delicious. 

We look forward to our next visit and may try the HTK Grand Seafood Tower which also looked amazing!

This place was soooo empty last night when we walked inside, which made me think, "Uh oh, we chose the wrong Thai restaurant on Mass Ave." But by the time we placed our order, all of the booths and most of the tables were full of friendly happy people making Tamarind House a better place to be. 

I ordered the pad thai, which was not spicy at all but extremely sweet. Usually, I can eat an entire serving of this stuff at other restaurants, but at Tamarind House I was only able to eat one tennis ball sized portion due to the sweetness. The vegetarian egg rolls were tasty, and they didn't squirt hot streams of oil out when we bit into them. Hubby got the chicken in red curry sauce, and liked everything expect the two strange vegetables we weren't able to identify. Were they eggplant and potato? I'll never know. 

Service was excellent, prompt, and cheerful. Tamarind House is a nice, solid Thai restaurant with good prices and quick service.

Omg this place is so good.

So here's a story with a happy ending. Me and my boyfriend were like "WE NEED FLATBREAD PIZZA." No normal regular pizza would do. So we went to flatbread pizza co. in davis square (attached to the sacco bowling alley.....blah blah blah). Place has over an HOUR wait at 6:15 on saturday. FUUUUUCK that place.

So we go all the way to kendall (a long way when you live in davis square....much effort) to go to Emma's Pizza.  Can I just say that this place was SOOOO so so worth it. We say down right away (although when we left at 8:30 there was a little wait). There were like 35 pizzas to choose from, all of which I wanted to try. And my pizza.....think #10....chicken, cherry tomatoes, goat cheese, and CAPERS. Omg so good. Bf got the artichoke, red pepper, ricotta and motz...... in conclusion really really good.

There is only bottled beers (I think)....which is fine. But I would say expand the beer selection PLEASE. And get rid of he coors light! I like a wide variety of fancy beers while eating my fancy pizza. 


WORTH finding it in kendall

Mediocre.  Granted, it was Valentine's Day, so that might account for something.  The food was hit or miss.  I had lamb shank that was quite good, my wife ordered Filet Mignon.  It was not a Filet, and it was not cooked how she ordered it.  The service was poor.  No one took our drink order, I had to ask for a drink menu, I had to ask for silverware, and our food took an hour after ordering to arrive.  

Decor was cute, but they had 2-top tables jammed in together.  All night I  had servers bumping me, and it was difficult to even have a conversation.

And then since it was a "special" night, they tacked on an automatic gratuity, a practice I really dislike.  I don't think we'll be back.  Far better Italian fare in the area.
I had the Korean bulgogi here, and it's by far my fave korean dish.  It's marinated beef served in a hot skillet.  I highly recommend it!

I've only been here once, but I was very pleased!  The falafel sandwich wrap was $3 and was a tasty and satisfying lunch.  Inside the little cafe it was warm, homey, and welcoming with prime seating along the windows for great people watching.  
They could definitely charge more for the food, but I'm sure the students appreciate a good deal.

I honestly cannot believe all of the praise Gran Gusto's pizza is receiving. We had their pizza with broccoli rabe, sausage, and mozarella. So bland that we asked for salt and pepper which only made marginal improvements. the risotto with asparagus and gorgonzola was also bland and kind of gluey. one of the earlier reviews made mention of too much salt. in our experience everything needed more seasoning. our waiter was gregarious, but did spill a bit of wine on the table while decanting. the maitre de was also very friendly and threw a bit of italian into his greeting. nice guy. Overall, the atmosphere was decent/unremarkable except for the new age/techno songs with what we thought were whale calls run through a delay pedal. seriously the most high-pitched sound ever heard in a rustic italian setting. just weird. fortunately, we were settling up and didn't have to endure the entire humpback whale mating song. So...as far as pizza goes I'm sticking with Emma's in Kendall Sq., or Cambridge 1 in Harvard Sq.

Do not let there mismatched goodwill mugs, and strange 7-11 big gulp look a like cups throw you off, that is probably the best part of this place.

Be sure you still have 1/2 a cup of coffee or water left before you ask for your refill, because by the time you get it, your cup will probably have been empty for a while already.   

This place is not for a big group to attend for breakfast, and it is defiantly not some where you want to be if it is busy, they already have a hard enough time getting you your food before a  whole hour has elapsed even if you are the only customers they have.

Very close to a 5-star review, but I feel like I need to try something other than the Chicken Pag (which is the bomb dot com) before I can do that. It's not you, it's me, Emma!

They offer quirky pizzas and an equally-quirky beer selection, which also gets them serious points.

One (nit-picking) qualm is that my sandwich takes about 20 minutes to come out sometimes, and I would expect it to take slightly less time. It's so good, I can definitely overlook my heightened anticipation.

We visited this place the other day.  We had heard good things about it and parking was easy.

Service could be better as my chair actually got bumped by a rushing waiter.  It took awhile for them to refill our empty teapot.

Overall, the food itself was decent.  I really liked the beef scallion appetizer.  Unfortunately, one of the dishes listed as using satay sauce turned out to be quite different than expected.

I went here for lunch with some work colleagues and I definitely enjoyed the food.  They have a huge menu but it is easy to read and they've highlighted some of their specialties to guide you.  At lunch, you also receive complimentary soup and of course, rice.

PRO TIP: If you have a large party for lunch, make a reservation for 11:45AM.  If you don't make a reservation, come BEFORE noon.  It is a TINY restaurant and it gets packed easily.

Of everything that we ordered, my favorites were the Sauteed Chinese Watercress w/ Garlic and Smoked Lamb & Leek.

On a scale of 1 to 5 of authenticity of Chinese food (1 being Panda Express), this place is probably a 3 or 4.  Which means, YOU CAN'T EXPECT WESTERN STYLE SERVICE.  Chinese service is prided on efficiency, that means, getting you seated in a timely manner, serving you food, and getting your bill.  So just because you don't get waited on hand and foot like you would at most North American restaurants, doesn't mean the service is bad, you just have to understand cultural context.

PRO TIP:  To get a new tea pot or have you tea pot refilled, take the top and turn it upside-down or sideways on top of the pot.  This signals to the staff that your tea pot is empty and that you need a new one.

Overall, good things.  I would definitely come again.

My wife and I have taken family and friends here for brunch twice, and while the food is tasty, the service is hilariously bad. Granted, they all seem to be brand new to the job, but the wait staff does some weird stuff...

For example, on both visits, it took 10+ minutes just to be brought the first glasses of water, because the waitress was busy chatting with a bartender. And on our last visit, two odd things...1) The hostess insulted the beloved Forest Cafe, which had been in that space for generations, by saying, "We replaced the Forest Cafe--except we're all organic and much more yummy." And 2) our waitress took our coffee mugs away to refill them (as opposed to bringing the coffee pot to the table), only to bring the same mugs back and place them unwashed in front of different people.

Speaking of coffee, they also don't provide spoons to stir your coffee with and when you ask for milk, they bring the tiniest cup of milk, enough for just one person.

The food, though, is great and almost meets the high standards set by its beloved predecessor, the Forest Cafe. So all-in-all, go for the delicious food but expect the service to remain quite bad for a while longer.
Great spot for Ethiopian/Eritrean cuisine. I would highly recommend his place for vegans/vegetarians as they have a lot of yummy options!

Decent sushi and hot pot. Very casual environment if you're looking for a quick bite with friends. They are especially a good place if you have big groups of people. Plus, they let you do as many sake-bombs as you want.

Just came from a wonderful meal at the Hops N Scotch Gastropub that just opened in Cambridge.  We started of with a few appetizers which included Scotch Eggs, Fried Pickles, Chicken Strips, Pesto Fritters and Pecan stuffed dates wrapped with bacon.  All were delicious especially the stuffed dates which was our favorite.  I then ordered the Ahi Tuna Salad as my entree which was very fresh and loved the mango sesame dressing. The staff was very friendly and attentive especially for a Friday night.  Welcome Hops N Scotch to Inman Square.  Look forward to coming back and sampling more great food, beers and scotch !!

Easily the best chinese offering in Central Sq, Mary Chung's does a lot right, but let me get my woes out of the way:
- Cash only.
- No delivery.
- Closed Tuesdays.

But the reason to eat here, obviously, is the food and it is stellar. The portions are rich and flavorful and there's plenty of choice. They also have a limited weekend dim sum menu with a few pearls on there, like the spicy dun dun noodles, and the addictive steamed pork dumplings. The steamed chicken and vegetables are also excellent, and come with a really great, spicy garlic soy sauce.

Mary Chung's definitely delivers to foodies in search of good Chinese but diners looking for ambiance are left out in the cold. Despite this, there are times when I crave this place and nothing else will do.

Stopped in this evening for dinner and decided to go for the chef's tasting menu after several other happy meals that we had enjoyed here in the past. We also asked for a pairing of drinks. The food that came our way was amazing. Starting with three fish appetizers, chef Brian then served a risotto and gnocchi dish which we thought would be the main. Instead, a beautiful steak dish followed and the miso jus with broccoli satisfied my craving for something substantial. To finish, we were offered a cheese board and a chocolate moose dessert. Very satisfied with the quality of dishes! The service was not what I would consider the highest of standards but was friendly and certainly well intentioned. Chef Brian really does know his flavors though and you won't be disappointed if you let him take the lead for the evening!

I don't know much about Eritrean or Ethiopian food, but I had a pretty good experience here. The bread was really great, and the curried lentils were fun to mop up with the spongy bread. It wasn't mindblowing, but it was good. The service was great, they were very friendly and attentive. 

This is another spot I would encourage to look into the green restaurant association 'green restaurant certification'. Kind of selfishly, because I would love to come back here, but I want to give my business to restaurants who i know take special steps to be as sustainable as possible. 

Regardless.. Asmara is a must try for those who have never had Eritrean/Ethiopian food.

I wish I read the reviews on this place before even considering it. I was looking for a place on New Years day for a late breakfast. I wanted to go to the Breakfast Club but the line was out the door. We decided to go to Cambridge cause I remembered I wanted to try Sunny's or there would be more options. So we see that Sunny's was closed and across the street we see a banner that reads "breakfast all day". We knew that was it after a couple hungry strikes

We enter a tiny little run down shop and there is no one around. Then a woman comes out with no smile and I asked for breakfast and she goes ok, sit wherever you want. The whole vibe was shady but we were starving and thought ok lets just do this. 

I decided to with the eggs, homefries, and toast. My bf gets the spinach and feta omlette. 

We were waiting to hear the breakfast being made, and its silent for like 10 minutes, then we notice she is back and an egg cracks. Okkkay. We sit sipping bad coffee and dusty hookahs sit in front of us. 

The food finally comes out. It was so thoughtlessly prepared, but I wasn't surprised. My "scrambled" eggs came out more like a crepe, flat and crispy.  Toast obviously burnt and homefries came with 5 potato pieces tasting like fish. Bf's omlette was tiny and just blah. Bacon was undercooked and we sent it back.

When we went to pay at the register (prices were only a couple bucks and it was useless to complain since we would never return) we noticed the back was filthy. Just don't bother!

This was my 4th visit to Shabu Ya. If this restaurant was not directly in my neighborhood I doubt I would get suckered into visiting it that many times. Now, I think I can permanently cross it off the list. I was lured in by Groupon offering me a sizeable discount on the food. We wandered in toward the early side of dinner.

We were seated immediately in an empty restaurant at a shitty table. Now having worked in a restaurant, I understand that there are server rotations and that it is not necessarily a slight against us to get stuck at an inopportune table. However, the place was empty so we asked to be seated at a better table. They grudgingly obliged. From that point on (I don't know if this was related or not) the service seemed to be stifled.

We ordered the gyoza to start, a large sake, some sushi and I of course ordered the bulgolgi which I noticed they spell bulgolki here. Quite honestly the only thing that was 100% appealing was the gyoza which is a fairly difficult dish to mess with. Even so, the gyoza app was brought out at the same time that our food came out (which was surprisingly fast.. even for an empty Asian restaurant). It seemed like they just wanted us to leave.

The bulgolgi was a soggy, fatty and generally bland version. It was literaly swimming in fatty water. It came with most of the standard accompanying side dishes which didn't do a whole lot to amp up the appeal. 

My friend said one of his rolls was good and interesting, but that the eel roll was completely inedible. He literally said he considered spitting it out it was that bad.

I've never come to this place and been particularly wowed with either the service or the food. I think I'm all set with Shabu Ya. Although, can't speak on the namesake of the restaurant (the shabu shabu) but I'm pretty confident it is subpar at best.

Consistently good, cheap Thai food.  I at at the restaurant for the first time yesterday.  I love the decor and the coziness of it.  My friend and I got a seat right at the window, and it was fun to look out onto Mass Ave at the foot traffic.

I ordered the Pork Garlic.  The sauce was perfect.  Garlicky, but not overwhelming.  My garlic burps after weren't even that bad!!

I've had take out from them several times, usually when my parents come up to visit.  We've tried several of the dishes on the menu, and all of them were really good.  The dumplings and curry dishes are to die for.

The food is good enough, but I will never go back to a restaurant that doesn't have a handicap accessible restroom (the women's room has two very tiny stalls rather than one that could be used by someone disabled; this is a small restaurant, and doesn't need a two-stall restroom).

This is my ramen home. The portions are amazing for the money and you can taste the time that went into the broth. Be prepared to wait 45 min to an hour during any days dinner rush, even on weekdays. You also should be friendly and open to sharing a table with people or else the wait can get longer. It is CASH ONLY but there's a Bank of America ATM near by. The house ramen is great and so is the spicy pork. I can't recommend this place enough!! It's good old traditional ramen!

Here's the skinny..

Food basically sucks, appetizers inedible (though salads are sizable and not all that bad).
All sandwiches, burgers, other fried foods seem like freezer to table as with any chain, so avoid them. Portions punishingly huge to make up for lack of quality. Expensive relative to what you get.

Beer/wine selection isn't bad, but avoid the microbrews as they get premium pricing for these options. Best to stick with domestic light beers (bottle or tap is fine). Staff is very cliquey with regulars, indifferent to transients and occasional patrons.

For games, it can be loud (if that's what you like) with a carnival atmosphere, great for big games like the Superbowl, horse races, college gridiron, etc. Lots of televisions (some work, some don't, so depends on where you stand as to what quality view you'll get). I have been in this bar when the satellite feed went down during an NFL playoff game which was disheartening. Overall, if you want to watch a game and scream and shout with cheap light beer, its not a bad experience.

NOTE: There are regulars at the Porter location. Be warned: most are either local drunks, boring sports fanatics, malcontents, and psychologically damaged and antisocial individuals. These folks usually congregate around the window side of the bar. Many bring iPads and other technology to play with, ignoring others in the establishment. Someone could do an HBO series on these characters if they really wanted to...

I was pleasantly surprised by Tamarind House- I had decided that no Thai food in my neighborhood was going to stack up to Dok Bua, but I finally found another place that serves Pad Ki Mao!  Totally delicious, and enough for lunch leftovers the next day.  I'll definitely be back.

Walking in, I felt like I was on vacation, at a theme park. Very space age, retro ikea. Space age-retro? I know, right?

It's just plain old nice to have shabu in the area, but I felt like they were a little on the pricey side for the amount of shabu items I received vs. competitors in the general Boston area. They also took way too long to give us uncooked food maybe they were washing it really well or something...and did that general somewhat annoying but humorous thing where an older woman came by to chastize me for not using my broth with my vermacelli...chill lady. Only Korean moms get to talk to me like that. 

It seemed like a fun venue for a party though, they had these little nooks for private parties that were sectioned off by their metal stuff. Eh. Give it a try if you're in the area...

Good pizza, but nothing to rave about.  But then again, I'm not a huge pizza fan, especially thin crust pizza.  I'd probably go back if someone wanted to go, but I it wouldn't pop up in my head as someplace that I'd crave.

Came here Saturday evening w/my boyfriend and we were promptly seated by a very friendly host. Love the atmosphere here - we got a table by the open window which looked out onto the street. We didn't order that much, but the dishes we had were solid & left us wanting to order more the next visit. We had - the shrimp toast, caesar salad, a selection of the raw oysters, and two pasta dishes. I had the spaghetti & he had the sweet potato & bacon stuffed cannelloni. They had an impressive selection of beers & seemed to have cool events I want to check out - like Trivia Tuesdays & oyster happy hour where the oysters are half off.

One of the shining points at this resto is the service! Very attentive & prompt.

So at first glance, this restaraunt looked good: middle eastern food, complete with my beloved falafel wraps and arabic coffee. And, the prices were great: all entrees and sandwiches were around $5.

However, the food nor the service was worth $5.

I'm really not sure why the waitress expected a good tip, nay, a tip at all, if she expected us to walk up and get our own drinks from the cooler. We had to remind her about  three times to get our coffee, and getting the check was nearly impossible as she was hiding in the kitchen. A baby cried from the back room and kitchen area the entire time.

The food was barely worth five dollars. My falafel wrap was a few peices of falafel, a pathetic little peiece of lettuce and a slim, slimy slice of tomato. Ick. 

If you want decent middle eastern food in central, just walk to Moody's Falafel Palace or the Middle East/Zuzu. Moody's is quick and cheap, and Middle East/Zuzu has better service and quality for a sit-down restaurant.

We had a big, hung over group after a wedding and we needed breakfast. Stat. A slightly confusing seating situation but we finally sorted it all out and got our orders in. Now, the service, is non existent. Our waitress was also probably about 13 years old, so I do have to cut her some slack. But food came out at strange pauses, so half our table of 6 had eaten most of their food before the rest of us got ours.

Now here's the deal though...it's really cheap, and the food is pretty darn good. I enjoyed the babaghanoush appetizer like woah! And my omelette wasn't half bad either. So, it is what it is. You want some good cheap eats? Cool. Just deal with the strange seating, mismatched dishes, and bad service. You want fancy and nice? Go somewhere else.

Not bad not bad. We came as a group of 12, and there was another group of about 15 who made reservations as well. Sadly, there was only enough space for one group to sit as one long extended table, whereas the other group of 12 were separated into 3 booths. It's a small restaurant and could not really handle the size of the two groups. They focused on serving the other group before coming to provide us with water or take our orders. :( 

Other than the wait time, the restaurant was great! The shrimp chips were nice and crunchy, with a little spice in it. Fried dumplings (9) and shrimp satays (6)  were great choices for appetizers. 

For entree I had pad thai, which comes with shrimp and chicken. It was delicious, though I wasn't used to how it wasn't spicy at all. 

So food is almost a 4, but because of the slow service, closer to a 3 for now. But I'd recommend, just not in large groups.

THEY DON'T TAKE CREDIT/DEBIT CARD! Whaaaat?! Luckily, I just had enough cash on me, but for a place where I am going to have dinner, it's kind of obnoxious. I just had take-out, but the service I had for that was excellent. The food is alright, nothing to really write home about. I had the pork wonton soup which I really enjoyed. They gave me lots of wontons which was nice. I had the shrimp lo mein, but it was mediocre. I did like that there where lots of shrimp, but they only added carrots and mushrooms. I would have liked more stuff in it like water chestnuts, sprouts, and such. I probably won't be going back here because I think I can find something better. The price was a bit steep for what I got too.

First time to this restaurant. Pleasant surprise. Went early on Saturday so lots of room, made an online reservation with OT but didn't need to. Nice atmosphere, waiter, waitress, owner all very present and helpful. Good wine by glass list, a Chianti Reserva and a Montalcino. Grilled calamari worth the trip, such nice fresh healthy alternative to the usual deep fried. 
Fettucine Bolognese was real deal. my wife got homemade pasta special with scallops and shrimp. The waiter mentioned andouille  which she didn't see but on asking,  owner explained was a sausage pate that is melted into the sauce- very informative. Torta ricotta cake to finish - delish. We live 35 miles away and just happened to be in town but we would definitely stop in again if in Boston area

There is every reason to imagine that this place is going to be a disappointment - it's nestled in a hastily-converted corner of an office park, with windowpanes painted on the plate glass to give it ambiance, and barely enough signage on the street to let people know it's not an insurance office.

But damn, they do a pretty good job. I've only done lunch there (my office is just down the street), but it has exceeded my expectations. Tasty food (I had the speck and prosciutto piadine), pleasant surroundings, and never crowded. I would definitely recommend it, and it's on my list of places to explore when I want a decent dinner.

One suggestion guys - get a fountain soda machine. I drink Diet Coke with my lunch, but something about getting a can on my linen-tablecloth makes me think less of a place that isn't a takeout joint.

I don't have too much to compare this too, but I think the food is pretty decent and definitely worth a try.  The injera bread, though a bit sour was tasty!! The prices are very very reasonable, so it makes for an interesting change in pace.

Cute ambience and environment.  The complimentary bread was just okay (not much real flavor in the bread itself) but the olive oil was pretty good.  The tables didn't have any salt or pepper so I couldn't season the olive oil before dunking the bread in it.  

Ordered the margherita as that's my standard whenever hitting up a pizza restaurant for the first time.  It arrived at my table looking beautiful, but alas the taste could not match up to the beauty.  Dont get me wrong, the pizza was fine, but I didn't moan nor swoon.  

I wouldn't come back but I wouldn't be averse to eating here again either if you know what I mean.

Hurray! Finally a restaurant where I can order the chicken without worrying about the hair I may grow on my chest from all the hormone additives. Rafiki's menu is impressive in its attention to organic and local meats and veggies, as well as its inclusion of real vegetarian entrees (no vegetarian will be stuck with bread and a side salad here). My chicken was nicely cooked, served with mashed potatoes and broccoli. Basic but very satisfying. My husband had the mushroom ravioli which was fabulous, and will be my choice on my next visit. The cheese plate appetizer was to die for. They also offer some very creative mixed drinks. I had something with organic vodka and rooibos tea which was different and very refreshing. For dessert I splurged with the gluten free chocolate cake- dense chocolaty goodness. We had no trouble with the service. Hopefully that's because they worked out their newby kinks. I definitely plan to return, probably for breakfast....thank gods someone is finally serving that in Porter Square!

My boyfriend and I came here with a groupon, which is usually the kiss of the death, but just may have saved my experience. 

The deal was for two appetizers and two entrees. The appetizers were amazing!! We got the eggplant and the mussels and they were both really really good. The eggplant was HUGE and covered in tomato sauce and cheese. There was also a good amount of mussels. Definitely would get either of those again. We probably wouldn't have gotten any appetizers if we didn't have the groupon, so super glad that we did.

I'm really glad we had to get two appetizers, because the entrees were both pretty weak. I got a steak, and ordered it rare, but it came out medium well. Not ok. I had them take it back and re-make it, but it came out medium well AGAIN. At this point, I let it go because my boyfriend was nearly finished with his duck. The problem was that the steak was SO thin that keeping on the stove or grill for more than two minutes completely cooked it all the way through. Not ok when it's $25. 

My boyfriend didn't LOVE the duck, he said it was a little chewy, but it wasn't BAD at all. 

The drinks were REALLY good. We ended up getting two drinks, and yeah, it was a little pricey, but it's Boston, so spending $10 on a cocktail doesn't faze me anymore. I had the rafiki 75, which is one of my favorite kinds of drinks (75s). My boyfriend got something with whiskey, and he also enjoyed it.

good food, good wait staff. 

the only problem is the price, its kinda...no, its really expensive for what it is...but whatever. i like it, anything to avoid the nasty dining hall food while im here at harvard -___-

I actually like this place a lot more than I would generally have expected. it's clean, staff is pretty nice, food is pretty good, and they serve huge beers. They also have a pretty ok selection of beers. The problem is that it's a huge personality-less chain....but, that is also why it's an ideal ace for big groups to watch sports games. Game sound is guaranteed to be on, they will be staffed appropriately, they have huge TVs and their bar food isn't bad. it's a little more expensive than id like to pay for such events, but I don't really mind it, if our big group of friends can all hang comfortably.

Worst place ever. The service was crappy. The food was mediocre at best. We asked if we could have the Bellini that the other table had and were told no that it was a test run and only the 3 single ladies could have them. 20 mins later someone else orders the Bellini and our waiter came over and said oh I guess you can have them and they got most of our tables order wrong. They also only gave water to half our table when all of us were waiting for a refill.I will not be going back here ever!

Dined here again the other night. Local and organic goods speak volumes to me. I have a profound respect for restaurants that offer quality dishes while trying to reduce their eco-footprint. Will be back soon!

This place was good!
Food has flavor... It was tasty. The waitress was very courteous.
Too bad I live 40 minutes away (mainly due to traffic)
If you like anything asian try this place.
I look down and see the buttons on my shirt pulling on the sides.
I'm very satisfied. The pork buns were awesome too!

If you like ramen, add this place to your bucket list, come in eat some ramen and cross this place off the list, throw the list away and then come back for more.

I had some really good mac n' cheese here, but I wish it would have come with something else (a side salad, a cup of soup, even just some onion strings) because it didn't feel like a very well-balanced meal, yet it was more than enough for one person to eat for dinner. If i came again, I'd get it to share with someone and share a salad or soup with the other person too.

It's very noisy and boisterous here...if that's your scene, it's cool, but if you want a nice relaxing dinner and good conversation, you'll be itching to leave so you can actually hear each other. 

Our waiter was very good and the hostesses were quite nice too. This is a very friendly place.

I had walked by here many times and finally grabbed some breakfast before work.  
Pros: affordable & fast
Cons: food & environment
My food was less than satisfying-I've had a better breakfast at home and the space was dirty and felt neglected.
I would recommend other breakfast places in the area.

Really, people? Awfully weak slices even if they did toss 4 special slices at us for the price of 3 since we had to wait for 'em to go. They really weren't that generous since the nearly $10 was for about $6 worth of quality, so no favors were performed.

The worst place I've eaten at in Boston. The food is horrible to say the least. There were cockroaches running around the place and our table and the tables and chairs were dirty as shit! Don't ever go to this place.

So, I've been meaning to get here forever as it is a short walk from mi casa. I'll come right out and say it; the food was 5 star worthy. The pizza was insane. Thin. tasty sauce. Real buffalo mozzarella used sparingly. Amongst the best I've ever had. My companion's butternut squash risotto was to die for, and the mozzarella and speck appetizer was divine. So why not 5-star review? I felt it was pricey for what it was. We made a cardinal error in falling in love with the description of the appetizer and never asked the price. At the end of the night we spent $80 including tip for 2 people. Appetizer, pizza, risotto, 2 beer and a glass of Cabernet for $80. Again, for the pizza alone I'd go back but it seemed a bit much for a casual meal. Service was a strange mix of what seemed like very friendly locals mixed with native Italians (or that was my guess). Still, all and all, that pizza.....

This place has the best ramen in Boston. I've had every single ramen bowl and they are all amazing. Their roast pork buns are also a must get when you come here. 

There has ALWAYS been a huge line whenever I go so in order to avoid waiting, get there prior before they open or you're almost guaranteed to have to wait for a table. 

I took a star off because the service is a bit slow at times. Last time I went, I was seated and waited 30 minutes before my pork buns arrived. They basically only have 1 server so when it gets busy, the wait could be longer than expected. And they only accept cash which is a bit of an inconvenience too, but the ramen is just so good it is worth every minute of waiting!

We arrived for an early dinner - so the place wasn't busy. Not sure if it ever gets busy as it wasn't that good. Service was slow! We waited are least 15 minutes for two beers and waited forever for our food to come. Not ideal with two kids under 3 at the table with us. Management did take our entrees off the bill - which was much appreciated - so I'm hopeful that they will work the kinks out of their system.

Gran Gusto had been on my "list" after I saw it on boston.com's list of best pizza places. My husband and I were in the area and up for an early Saturday night dinner when we decided to go here. First, the biggest plus, was the parking lot in the back. There are plenty of spaces and even though it says to park there after 6pm, it was fine to park there at 5pm on a Saturday. The restaurant itself is inside an office building, but they look like they have great outdoor seating in the summer. 

From the moment we stepped into the restaurant we were impressed. The owner and hostess greeted us and took our jackets. Throughout the entire meal, our waiter Anthony was fantastic. He explained the menu, answered any questions, and was all-around pleasant. Our water glasses were never empty (LOVE that) and the homemade bread was delicious. We started with the stracciatelle and I felt like I was back in Sorrento. We found out later that the owner's brother who is a chef was actually the chef at the hotel we stayed at in Italy last year! For entrees we had the artichoke ravioli and a pizza. The sage butter sauce the raviolis came in was amazing and the pizza was delicious. For dessert, tiramisu and chocolate almond cake. My husband who never eats dessert was scraping the bottom of the cup with his tiramisu in it. 

I left wondering if the next time we went if Gran Gusto would continue to impress us or if it would be a one-time fluke. I'm happy to report that the next time we went, it was equally impressive. The wild boar sausage was delicious but I was really blow away by the homemade gnocchi, made just how it should be. We had a different waiter, who was just as attentive and friendly as the first. 

Bottom line: everything is homemade and delicious, the service is friendly and attentive, and there's parking.

I ordered a large order for my office. The order short. No problem these things happen. But when you call and say the order is messed up, you get told that's impossible. Take it from the guy that order the only sandwich that was stand alone to the others. You did make a mistake. 

Over your arrogance/ignorance you lost a customer that used to come in and buy slices 2-3 times a week. Plus the majority of my office.

I love peaaannutttttssssss (warning.. don't yell that out loud.. people have dirty minds). So of course I'm gonna order fresh rolls with peanut sauce to start AND the Kai Yang barbecue for an entree (the sauce has a peanutty taste). I wasn't really digging the stuffed tofu my companion got, but she ate the whole plate so I guess to each their own. The fresh rolls were.. ya know.. fresh, but nothing outstanding. The BBQ however.. SO delicious.. ooh. The chicken was a tidbittidbit on the tough side, but the seasoning and the sauce and the veggies with the brown rice made my soul. The only annoying part was that they charged us extra for her rice (white) and even more for my rice (brown). Meh, I'm willing to look over that fact for now. 

Service was wonderful. Prompt, courteous, and properly inquisitive. There was even a woman in the back to pointing to the bathroom (I guess I was headed for the kitchen).

Just a warning.. sitting by the window may annoy your lunch/dinnermate because you get all ADD in conversation when you think you know the person standing outside. Slash, there's a bit of an echo in that section (I think patrons hated my bulletproof laugh.. whatever haters).

I decided to try Dosa Factory after walking past it a ton of times. I love Indian food, but this really disappointed. I got the saag paneer which was really oily, so I could only stomach a few bites. I also got garlic naan which was just ok. Sorry, but if I don't enjoy my favorites, I will not be back.

I love West African/Ethiopian cuisine. It's one of my favorite things to eat. That said, this place sucks. Their dishes were downright bland compared with what I've come to expect from this style of food. I see other reviews mentioning how their injera is sour - injera is supposed to be a bit sour, and the bread here didn't bother me. But the service (abrupt and rude) and the food did. I had a beef dish, for one, that was tough and didn't taste like anything, really. Second, one of my vegetarian companions, out of pure curiosity, ordered the "salmon curry". First of all - salmon? Do they have lots of salmon off the Ethiopian coast? That right there should have answered all the questions we had about this dish. The fish was way overcooked and the curry was shit flavored, I believe. Man, that sucked. The other dishes besides the fish and beef were unremarkable, dull affairs. To top it all off, all this stuff is way overpriced. This place SUCKS. There's much better Ethiopian to be had, in Boston and elsewhere.

Amazing!  Pizza Crust is thin and crunchy.  Had the Hasty Pudding Pie which had the rosemary sauce, carmelized onion, sweet potato, spinach & goat cheese.  Services was very friendly.  The place is small & quaint.  I definitely recommend it.  I will come back for sure!

Went back again as soon as I saw that they brought back the Soy Sauce ramen. It wasn't as good as it used to be, but it certainly hit the spot. The noodles seem to be less thick and the broth was off just a little. Other than that, perfection. I'll be coming back again and again and again. I just wish they served edamame as well.

Perhaps those who are new to Shabu are excited about this place, but to be honest, I think it's only fair to rate this restaurant in comparison to other places in town, such as Shabu Zen, given that the concept and even their menu pretty much mimics Shabu Zen. 

Some issues we had with the place:
1) They are inconsistent with the amount of meat and veggies they give you. For example, I ordered the same meat dish as my friend and got a few pieces less than they did. The server was not at all apologetic about the oversight. This inconsistency happened both times I have gone there. 
2) They tout that they provide unlimited veggies which we were really excited about seeing that this feature was something that other shabu places did not have. However, when asked whether you could have four more pieces of tofu, the server replied, "hm...well, I can't really give you that many...I'll give you this much" (drops three pieces in - NOTE: this was after I didn't have the correct amount of tofu pieces in the initial veggie plate ). It made for a really awkward situation because we didn't want to be demanding, yet they had just told us that it was unlimited :( I think management needs to be more consistent with what they tell their servers to say and do.
3) Then the server asked us how we liked their service. Even more awkward. 
4) The broth was not flavorful enough :(

I really wanted this place to do well, and really do hope they change for the better. If not, I'm not confident it will survive. As for now, me and my friends and family will continue to go to Shabu Zen.
Get the micro green salad with warmed blue cheese. We had a delicious burger and pizza. Our server was excellent - kind and attentive. We'll be coming back!

My husband and I went here for the first time last night after living about a five minute walk away for over a year. 

We made reservations for 8, but it seems like we didn't need them. While there were people there, it wasn't crowded at all. They wasted no time in telling us the specials and asking us what we wanted to drink. We didn't have any wine but they have an extensive wine list. 

We got a big basket of bread, which we couldn't stop gobbling. 

My husband had the special appetizer of soft buffalo mozzarella with speck. We didn't know what speck was so we asked. The waiter was very helpful and explained it to us without being demeaning, which was appreciated. My husband loved it and finished all of it. I had the mista salad (mixed green salad), which was simple but delicious. 

For his entree, my husband had the rigatoni with sausage, mushroom and garlic. He loved that it wasn't too saucy and had a lot of sausage and mushroom. I had the handmade pasta with shrimp and raddiccio. It had a cherry tomato sauce that was not too heavy and I was very happy with the amount of shrimp in the dish. 

It took a little time to get our entrees, which was okay because we weren't in a hurry. It was a tiny bit annoying that at one point they told us it would be five more minutes and then it took another fifteen. But, I do understand that they actually make the food instead of just reheating so I guess it is worth it. 

Many people there were ordering pizzas and they looked great as well.

After walking by Asmara for months I was excited to give it a try but I left pretty disappointed.  The prices were high and the food was quite bland.  When we got our bill we noticed that each entree was $2 more than stated on the menu.  We pointed this out to the waitress (owner?) and she refused to believe it.  She tried to convince us that we had read the menu wrong and to prove her point brought different menus for us to look at than the ones we had ordered from.  It turns out she had mistakenly given us lunch menus instead of dinner menus.  When she realized her mistake she still refused to let us pay the prices on the menus we'd ordered from.  The interaction was unpleasant and left a bitter taste in our mouths.  I won't be going back to Asmara.

Very authentic (apparently, though I've never been to Taiwan).  Bamboo shoot pork and sautéed green beans are amazing.  Not the most gluten-free friendly (soy sauce in everything) and it's unclear if the request for no soy sauce is even an option.

I really wanted to like this place; a new independent gastropub with a great selection of craft beers and multiple vegetarian options sounded right up my alley. I came with two friends for dinner, and ultimately we were disappointed.

First, service was not fantastic. We sat for about 30 minutes before our server first approached us. Granted, we could have tried to get his attention in some way other than awkwardly staring at him every time he walked by, but when a half an hour passes and the only staff member who interacts with you is the hostess asking if she can take your extra chair, it tends to leave a negative impression. 

Second, the food was not great for the price. I ordered the roasted eggplant tahini, and I was really excited about it, but it was nothing particularly special. I could have made that myself, for much less than what I paid here.

Overall, a disappointing experience and I will not be returning, at least any time soon.

Sapporo Ramen had been a destination at the Porter Exchange Building (Porter Square, Cambridge) for over 20 years. Although their portions have been reduced, their assortment of soup choices has increased. Sapporo Ramen is still one of the best places to get a good Ramen in Beantown.

As a visitor from out of town this place was a good find. Mostly because as someone with a gluten sensitivity this place looked promising. It didn't disappoint. 

The fried pickles were amazing and huge! The pizza was ok and according to my boyfriend the mac and cheese was good. Service was good and we enjoyed playing trivia the night we were there. 

The only semi-downside is the portions are huge. The mac and cheese could serve 3 people easily. Just beware of the gigantic portions.

I'd come back for those damn pickles. Yum!

I think the food is actually hit or miss. NYC chinatown is way better but that is to be expected. Food Boston chinese food, its good. Some dishes are really great but others are only okay... I love the suan la chow sho of course. Its amazing. My boyfriend loves the mooshoo pork. I like that its no frills. Prices are good. The specials stay the same-- ie. grandmas pie. Cash only.

Great dosas with sambar, but the rest of the menu could use some work. I recommend either making the menu smaller and simpler or improving on everything that doesn't come in dosa form. I will come back again for sure, but might be hesitant to order outside their specialty.

Good, but I can't give it four stars after having lived in Tokyo for four months and going to Ippudo once a week.  That is truly the ramen of the gods.  

Sapporo was good.  I got the miso, which was ok.  I agree with some other posters that it was pretty bland.  However, I really went there for the noodles and they did not disappoint.  There were a few slices of pork as well as some ground pork.  Not exactly authentic, but it was good.  The noodles were chewy but firm, the perfect texture.  They gyoza were also excellent.  

I think the size of the serving was actually larger than what I got served in Tokyo, which is really saying something.  

On my latest trip I tried their Hokkaido-style ramen.  It's not named as such, but it's the one with corn and a pat of butter.  I liked it much better than the miso ramen. 

I still believe that if someone could pull off an accurate copy of a Japanese ramen-ya it would do gang-buster business.  Hopefully it will happen soon.

- Great BLT
- Long wait for a sandwich.  Not good for a quick lunch break.
- Extremely unfriendly waitress (there was a male waiter who seemed much nicer, but we got the grumpy lady)

I'm about as Chinese as the pope is Jewish, so I have no idea if this is truly authentic or not.  What I do know is that the menu lacks general gau's (which is a good thing in my book) and I *love* the food.

I've had some amusing times here thanks to a language barrier,  but the service has always been first rate.   Once or twice, I've been so stuffed that I couldn't finish and Mary came over to ask if everything was alright.  She was worried when she saw I wasn't a member of the clean plate club.

Now.  . . how many times do you see that these days?  Seriously.  

Mary,  as long as your food and service stay top notch, keep on being closed tuesdays and only accepting cash.   I really don't mind!

I wish I could give this place ZERO stars. The service at this place was probably the worst thing I have ever encountered at a restaurant. I hope they were not biased against non-italians or non-regulars. You would get the feeling that they did not want us there and we were troubling them by eating there.

Quick summary: 
service:  0 out of 5
atmosphere:  2 out of 5
food: 3 

I drove all the way from east cambridge with my guests from NYC on a Sunday night in May 2013. We made and online reservation on our way there. I decided to take my friends based on my Italian friends telling me this is one of the two really authentic Italian places in Boston (the other one being Pasta Beach on the waterfront.)

We got there with high hopes and great expectations. We told the guy (owner/manager) that we just made an online reservation 15 minutes ago, which made him grin. I wish i had not told him. I guess he hated us from the very beginning. 

He sat us at a side bar by the door while waiting for a table to open up. The place was packed with the graduation weekend crowd and really loud.

After 10-2 minutes sitting at the side table with no water, menu, etc. he took us to a small table packed behing a large family. Dropped us a menu and left. while we were thinking he came back and asked us what we want. We were thinking what we should order, he got impatient and left with no explanation. 

Someone brought a little bread but we had to grab olive oil and pepper from another table ourselves. 

we finally ordered but he did not have patience for us trying to decide on wine. We gave up wine and decided to just eat and leave.

From the two appetizers we ordered one was ignored and the other (half decent)  came 1 minute before our pizzas.

The pizzas were REALLY good but we were so mad with the treatment we did not enjoy it at all.

another half an hour or so waiting to pay our bill....

heading out with regret, anger, and empty pockets.

I agree with everyone else, don't go here!!
