we tried the food for brunch and the fact that no one else was in the joint should have been a sign, but it was pretty early on sunday and none of the other places we wanted to go to were open yet so we thought we'd give it a chance. big mistake!!

greasy food and there was that burnt pan stuff on my omelet. the only edible thing was the toast...

I love injera, the smushy bread that comes with Ethiopian cuisine. I mean I love injera so much that I will regularly hold it softly against my cheek and sigh wistfully before consuming it. The bread at Asmara is fluffy, soft, and just a bit sour from the fermented teff used to make it. It's excellent, I mean really really excellent. Sadly, the food seems to err on the side of bland. My collard greens were well cooked but tasted mainly one-note and the yellow lentils had barely any flavor beyond "This is a lentil." My companion and I wanted to share their vegetarian sampler and were instructed by the waitress that it "served one." Served one person for $16? Well alright. Once ordered, it was obvious that one would have been sufficient when paired with an appetizer, and we felt we had been swindled a bit into over ordering. 

Why three stars? The food here is authentic and good, but the prices are just a bit crazy. I've been to other Ethiopian restaurants (Addis comes to mind) where the portions were even more plentiful, and more importantly-- flavorful and prices cheaper, and the food quality was just as good if not better. Is their priciness due to location? I couldn't say. But for ho-hum service and not  particularly impressive food, I have to say I'd rather go elsewhere for Ethiopian, even if their injera is fabulous. I just wish the rest of their food was as tasty.

After deciding to throw over another gym workout in exchange for the massive consumption of carbohydrates, I grabbed Peter F. and a Groupon and stopped by Tavern in the Square.

To be honest, it's not really my kind of place - the layout feels disjointed and there are sooo many tvs, not to mention several projectors. That would've been fine if I wanted to watch a game, but I really would rather enjoy my fried pickles without a side of informercial.

On the bright side, the food was delish - fried pickles had a good level of spice to them, the black n bleu burger with sweet potato fries was satisfying, and the waiter didn't even blink an eye when I asked for a shirley temple. Peter F loved his customized burger, but then he hasn't eaten red meat in 7 months.

I saw several plates of nachos being served to other tables, and GAWD they're huge.

Really tasty Thai food, but delivery has been extremely problematic of late.  Two times in a row, the food has arrived 40 minutes late because of miscommunication or confusion between the dispatcher and driver about my location.  They were apologetic, but it's really annoying.

I've had Emma's amazing pizza a few times before, and it is absolutely delicious.  Why, then, the harsh 1 star review?  I am so shocked, upset, and appalled at an 'incident' that occured last night.  I arrived at Emma's at 9:20 pm (40 minutes before their 10 pm closing time) with a party of 2.  There were 4 other parties sitting and eating and enjoying their pizzas.  A woman emerged as we walked in and stated, "Oh, sorry, we closed our kitchen because we're having a slow night."  

What? My companion and I stood there, our mouths agape.  For a Tuesday night, at 9:20, it actually looked like they were doing pretty well!  

We had driven 20 minutes to eat here, after double-checking that they would be open when we arrived.  We did not come in 5 or 10 minutes before closing...

I was so disappointed not only that we didn't get to eat their pizza, but in the uncaring way we were dismissed from the restaurant without even a real apology!  

I will be calling Emma's today because I am so upset with the poor customer service.

Absolutely delicious. Really considerate waitstaff, large portions, and a homey ambiance make this place a real gem in Central Square. For three people, we shared 3 separate entrees and chose to have them served traditional style, and we were stuffed by the end of the meal!

This is an amazing restaurant.  We have been coming for years because the food is so fresh and delicious.  The Shuan Le Chow Show -- shrimp version -- is our absolute favorite.  It is very spicy -- if you want a milder version, ask for it in the wonton soup., or get the sauce on the side.  The beef with bean curd is outstanding, as is the stir-fried baby bok choi and the mustard greens.  It is fun to see the staff making the dumplings from  scratch at a big table in the back.  This is head and shoulders above any Chinese restaurant in the area.

We only went here because the bars at Temple and West Side were super crowded, and we just wanted to sit at the bar and have a pre-dinner cocktail (reservations elsewhere). 

I liked the vibe and thought the bartenders were really nice. My blood orange margarita was scrumptious, and the taste I had of my husband's sangria was also enjoyable. However, we made the mistake of splitting an appetizer to stave off our hunger before we went to dinner. The codcakes were atrocious. I basically just ate them because I was getting wasted off of the margarita.

I used to live on Harvard St. right behind this restaurant and would always go there for their lunch specials. I'd say they are really good and authentic. I eat here pretty often when I'm just too lazy to cook. They also have a free appetitizer (sour radish) that I love.

Service is above average for a chinese restaurant. Once I had a dish that was too salty and they took it off the bill. 

As a Taiwanese restaurant, they also have some very exotic Taiwanese food which I haven't been able to find in other restaurants.

I have been to this place a couple of times for different reasons - healthy food, Saketini, ice cream.

I have tried the Combo Platter. The food was great, though you end up with way too many vegetables, be careful while ordering. Saketinis are great if you like sweet colorful but still strong cocktails. I tasted the green tea ice cream. A little bit expensive but tasty.

The best thing about my experiences was the service. Twice I had the same server, once when I was there once with a large group. I didn't catch her name but I remember her telling us she was from Hawaii. My roommate's mom, who couldn't speak much English, was with us at one dinner, and she even managed to get her talking. Everything was on time, her drink recommendations (after she saw that we had no idea about Saketinis, she decided to order for us) were perfect.

Why not five stars? because I believe Shabu express went out of business because of Shabu-Ya. And you cannot have the same tuna rolls at Shabu-Ya!

Overall: Good food, decent service, basic interior

Food:  I love this Taiwanese place- great food, huge menu- tons of options

Interior: Matches expectations

Service: decent

I recommend this place if you're looking for Chinese around Kendall/Central- best in that part of town.

More like 3.5. So in town for the weekend and decided to give this eco-chic place a try vs. getting my cambridge common burger with sweet potato fries.

It is chic and not crowded for a friday night- must be the weather.

1)Service: Attentive and we had (others) come over and ask about our meal 2 minutes from the time our waitress did/and again. This was almost over doing it at times. I think they read the Yelp reviews about service and are trying to up their game- but let's not over kill it. 
2) Good Bread/Oil dip
3) Maple Turkey Burger with sweet potato fries. This was good, but the mixed greens on my lettuce were really wilted-dislike. But the fries weren't greasy-like. FYI: I like honey mustard with my fries- but they have no condiments besides ketchup.

It's def. more money than cambridge commons, but the quality isn't better. However, if you are looking for a nice place to chat/not a large bar scene/no wait. This is a great place to grab a casual dinner with friends. Plus- I love it that they do support the local farms- so I'll def. pay the difference.

It was pouring rain and we popped in here. Even though it was 4 pm, they said we can order food from a limited menu.
We both had the grass fed burger - an awesome beefy delight and might be my new favorite neighborhood burger (sorry Christopher's). 

The place looked really nice. One can linger at the bar or have a cup of coffee or tea from their full service coffee station. 

I will try it again.

While the ambiance isn't anything special, this is a favorite neighborhood haunt of ours. We've never had to wait for a table, the food is quite good (We start every meal with the vegetarian spring rolls -- so yummy!) and the service is always friendly and attentive.

Broth is good, though it was only lukewarm and the meat was cold.. Not sure what happened, but probably because orders are rushed. And the egg is just hard boiled instead of soft boiled like it should. Broth is very thick though, and flavorful. The ramen here is on the heavier side. The broth is too thick for my taste.

Wanted to try their dumpling on Yelp. It was actually pretty good. I also ordered a hot and sour soup, although it wasn't spicy, but it wasn't bad, and the quantity was large for both of them. However, I do have to comment on their service: Employees were sleeping, which I understand is actually common for Chinese people (to take naps during the day, although I'm not sure what the working protocol is. Then a lady who looked like the owner turned on a Chinese drama or show on her computer and played very loudly, as if it would be nice background music for the restaurant. I had to ask for some basics, such as water, sauce, and the check, while my waitress hit in back. The service was not great, but the food isn't bad.

Every dish we got was delicious.

We started with a fried octopus, which was perfectly crispy on the outside and tenderly chewy on the inside. The creamy sauce nicely complemented the octopus, chips, and mushrooms.

Girlfriend got some oysters, which were good, but the winter points for $5/ea didn't seem to be worth the premium.

For entrees, we got the truffle risotto and the oxtail pappardelle. Both were great, but I'll focus on the pappardelle since that was my main dish. The oxtail appeared to have been braised and was very tender and flavorful. Perhaps even better were the turnips, which provided a crunch and some acidic flavors to balance out the dish. The pasta tasted very fresh. Only complaint is a few pieces of pasta were stuck together, and there may have been a little more oil at the bottom than I would have liked. I got the large size, and it was right about what you would expect for a pasta entree--i.e., if you get the smaller portion, should probably add on something else.

Will definitely be back to try more!

Sunday brunch: Butterfinger pancakes, tomato/spinach eggs, shrimp, desserts & mimosas. That description barely scrapes the surface of the delicious array of food. 

Everything was scrumptious with plenty of vegetarian options. Our waitress was pleasant and had a really cool tattoo. Gotta love that.

I can only speak for the brunch buffet-- thank freaking GOD for that!
Tavern's all you can eat Sunday offering didn't show up on any searches I did as I sought out a spot to nosh on unlimited bacon...but a coworker referred me here, and I'm glad she did. For $16.99, here's what we could have:
-coffee, tea, soda
-omelets made to order and brought to the table
-eggs benedict
-bacon
-sausage
-fresh fruit
-plantains (!!)
-crepes
-pancakes
-french toast
-hash browns
(keep going? okay)
-home fries
-cereal
-yogurt
-make-your-own sundaes
-eclairs, petit-fours, mini cheesecakes 

The food wasn't 5-star hotel quality, but for the chance to fill my plate(s) with as much as I wanted, I wasn't complaining.

I've been looking forward to trying this place because it is super eco-chic, and everything from the beef to the placemats seems to have been made of grass.  It was friendly and had a nice atmosphere, although the seats in the sexy window booths were strangely shallow and high -- I'm six feet tall and my feet were dangling uncomfortably.  But I generally liked the decor. 

 For food we ordered the mixed green salad, the vegan spinach and sweet potato ravioli, and the special chicken and broccoli. 

I wasn't impressed, particularly for the prices ($8 salad, approx. $20 entrée -- for lunch).  The salad was nicely dressed, and beautifully plated, sort of wrapped in long narrow slices of English cucumber garnished with tomato.  The problem was that the garnish vegetables all appeared to have been cut a day or two before, and were dry and somewhat withered.  Not appetizing to eat.  Also, there were some rotten bits in the mixed greens -- which are hard to pick out I know, but at a restaurant like that they should be able to do better. 

The entrees were good but not at all amazing.  The ravioli were tasty, topped with some sauteed swiss chard and a surprisingly good crunchy mixture of dried carrot and onion.  However, the sauce they were swimming in was mediocre and kind of ruined it.  I think it was some kind of fancy carrot and tomato puree, but the flavor and effect was basically like Campbell's soup.  I really think they should do a lot better for $18.95.  

Similarly with the other dish.  The chicken was well cooked, and the meat was very flavorful.  It was accompanied by some okay, but unremarkable and undersalted mashed potatoes, and a decent light gravy.  There were more of those crunchy carrot things.  The broccoli was just plain steamed, in large chunks, no dressing of any kind.  I know it's healthy and all, but this dish was pretty dull. 

I like the idea of Rafiki but I wouldn't eat here again for my dollar.  Maybe if it cost half as much.  

I would consider trying the bar for a cocktail, but it annoys me that the cocktail menu has not a single price on it.  That's just pretentious and makes you feel like a cheapskate for asking how much the drinks cost.  And I think that making the customer feel cheap is rude.  Just saying...

Definitely hit or miss. Some dishes are really good, while others are mediocre, lackluster, and bland. Lunch specials are not that great. 

Looks fancier than the standard Chinese place and is slightly more expensive. Some dishes are worth it...others less so! Service is functional, which is good enough for me

I've been to a couple of shabu places and a friend of mine wanted to check this place out with me. Both of us were huge fans of hot pot...so we were excited to check this place out. 

The place is pretty spacious and I like the bright environment of the place compared to other shabu places. We were seated at the booth and there was more than enough space for two. 

Service was pretty good. The host and waiter were polite...which was surprising because Asian restaurants with Asian staff usually have one of the most terrible customer services. This place was good compared to a lot of other Asian restaurants that I've been to. 

The food was a bit pricy but I thought the food was good. We got beef and pork with miso and kimchee broths. The kimchee broth was really good...very refreshing and had the right spice. The meat was good and a much better quality compared to the one in hot pot buffet. Its funny cause when we asked for more condiments, they gave us a good amount. Like I said, the service was good.  

Haven't used the groupon coupon yet...so I'll definitely be coming back to use it.

Excellent food.  Tip: order it spicy.  Very pleasant and laid back atmosphere. Ashley happy. They willingly sell their injera (the awesome special bread you'll associate with Ethiopian food) separately.

Note to Yelp:  How about an Eritrean category?

Oh, yuuuck!!!
I wish I had checked Yelp before coming here. Wow.
I came in here for lunch after work today. I was hungry and cold, and it was right around the corner from my work, so I thought, "Why not." I saw that they had falafel, and figured it would be impossible to fuck up falafel. Boy, was I wrong.
I was the only person in the restaurant, yet I still had to wait 20 minutes for my food. When my falafel wrap arrived, it was terrible. The falafel itself was squishy and falling apart, the lettuce was wilted, and the hummus just tasted weird. This, coupled with the sound of the owner's screaming baby in the back room, was enough to make me never come back here again. PLEASE, STAY AWAY.

Sports bar?? Monday night football. Despite numerous request from patrons to replace the music soundtrack with the sound from the game, no go because of store policy.  Go somewhere else for Monday Night football.

My go-to thai restaurant.  It's perfect - delicious, reasonably priced, and just across the street.  Everything you need in a go-to restaurant.

I always order the Pad See Ew with chicken, extra spicy.  My boyfriend has the Pad Thai with Tofu.  We order Crab Rangoon (yes, at a thai place, and I love it, so what?).  Everything is delicious, and if picking it up it's only $27-ish.  Yummy.

I had never truly had authentic ramen before, and after coming up here with a friend; someone who *always* goes out of their way to visit Sapporo when they're in the area, I think I'm going to have to do the same.

It was just...wow. Great. I'm not the biggest fan of tofu, or even hot and sour soup for that matter, but the fact that the pork was just so tender and warm, and the tofu just absorbed all of the spices and flavor that the spicy broth gave it. The scallions were the perfect size, and the table had lots of fixins, if that's your thing (two different types of pepper, spicy sauce, salt, ketchup, strangely enough.)

The place itself is kind of hard to find; you have to go into this tiny college bookstore building, and it's down the back hall where all of the other Japanese style restaurants are located. They only have seating for a couple of tables; maybe 15 people total, so a line quickly forms around the key dining points of the day (noon, 5, 7, etc). They also hold weird hours, and have no qualms with just closing in the middle of the day with no ETA on when they'll reopen. There's plenty to do in the surrounding Harvard Square area, at least, so enjoy the day!

I give this place 4 stars for a couple reasons. The food was delicious, and cheap too, but it was lacking a certain soul that you get at more intimate dining places. The portions were gigantic, and for only 2 or 3 dollars more, you can get double, or triple the portions (I went with the 2$ add-on, and definitely don't regret it.) If you're shopping at the plaza, and not a student, you can get your parking validated for a small fee; take the T at all possible (red line to Harvard Square drops you off about 1/2 a mile away, I believe).

This place is awesome. I love the person who works there, she always has a smile on her face & is very warm & friendly. I love the food, as a vegetarian I have to be able to have options which they have plenty of here. It's really kickass food that fills up up rather quickly due to the injera's absorbing qualities. I would definitely get their juices since they are freshly made but pass on desserts which are not authentic & just frozen.

I'd like to echo Jillian Y's review.  In fact, her comments very much reflect my take on this place.  My girlfriend and I live in the neighborhood and have really wanted to give this place a chance on a number of occasions as the area lacks something in between Cambridge Common's pub fare and West Side Lounge/Temple Bar's more expensive dining.  We like the decor, the music, the concept but that is about all at this point.

Today, we went for brunch and had a pretty awful experience.  My girlfriend ordered pancakes and I ordered a Mediterranean omelet.  My food was fine if nothing to write home about.  My girlfriend's pancakes were, as she described them, more like "biscuits."  They had a hard outer shell and were barely warmer than room temperature, despite our fairly simple food taking nearly 30 minutes to come out to us.  It then took another 10-15 minutes for our server to ask us how we were and to notice that the pancakes weren't being eaten.  In our waiter's defense, she had just started and really tried to make things right when she found out.  At that point, my girlfriend changed her order to an omelet.  This was fine, although accompanied by home fries that tasted no better than Shaw's frozen home fries.  Perhaps the coup de grace, though, was when we got our bill and we had been charged more for the omelet rather than just the price of the abandoned, original pancakes.

I don't want to sound nit-picky.  My larger point is this: this place is, while not extremely expensive, certainly overpriced for consistently mediocre to poor service and consistently sub-par food.  Searching through the Crate and Barrel catalog for modern lighting and a love for organic eggs do not a good restaurant make when everything else is lacking.

The only times I've been here have been with my 60+ cousin and his friends, and they usually do the ordering and I've just focused on the grazing.  While I can't cite the headlines of many specific items we had, I'll say that everything I've had here has been good, and I'm not usually super into Chinese food unless we're talkin dim sum.

The mustard greens were great, we had a delicious noodle dish where the noodles were girthy and crispy but topped with chicken and bamboo shoots and peapods and mushrooms in a gravy that made the noodles soften but not mushy.  I'm going to have to do some investigation on what that one was - it was my favorite.

The Mongolian beef with some kind of green was just ok for me, and then the tofu in a peanut-y sauce (I think it was in dun dun sauce?) was pretty good too, especially if you're really into peanut butter.  I wasn't starving so didn't try the sweet and sour chicken and came too late to get in on the scallion pancake action, but both plates were demolished so it appears they went over well for the table.  My cousin always gets the egg drop soup, the bowl for which was also polished cleanly.

The tab came to $80 after tax and tip for 6 of us, which was pretty darn cheap considering we were all full and got in a variety of options.  Leaving the restaurant and looking at the food on other tables - everything looked delicious.  I need to start to make this more of a central square staple - especially if I'm heading in with a group.

This is the better of the two Thai options in the Porter Square area.  Some of their more authentic dishes are excellent, though I've been disappointed in their pad woon sen.  Seating is usually quick, and they won't kick you out at the end of the night.

The food here is much better than Sugar & Spice, and the atmosphere is pleasant as well.

It is nothing short of a miracle if this place is open.

My girlfriend loved the lentil soup she once had at this place. 

So we tried to visit this place at various times, and irrespective of when we went (believe me, we tried going at all kinds of times) - it used to be closed.

Maybe they were renovating or something then - I dont know, whatever it was, it did not make great business sense.

Anyway this once when we managed to find it open (we were really excited to go in, I remember), we had the lentil soup again (this time it was ok, clearly there is not much consistency in the cook's recipes). 

But should say that the food was not too bad. I think we had some veggie soup as well, and that was also not bad.

5/10 for the food, 0/10 for their hours.

I used to live near here and went in once every few weeks. Now I've moved away and I have got to say that the food here is still exceptionally good. We usually get the suan la chow show, spicy green beans, and dry cooked beef. The service is friendly, and Alice is a very nice waitress.  The food is delicious, and miles above the regular Chinese takeout in quality! They don't take credit cards, and are closed Tuesdays.

Several Sundays ago I went to a local foods event at Rafiki Bistro. So glad I did. Not only do they locally source their food as much as feasible, they know how to serve it up with style and great taste. The beef sliders served us Sunday were delicious, the ambiance was warm and welcoming, and the service friendly and efficient. So of course I had to go back the next night where a friend and I had the  Monday night special, coq au vin. Both it and arugula salad with grilled green beans and gruyere cheese more than met our expectations. Robert's service was topnotch, too. We opted to take our dessert order to Berry Line for their delicious  yogurt as Rafiki's dessert offerings that night were slim. OK, now to write a review of Berry Line!

Seeing as how this place is almost always ridiculously busy, I had high hopes that the food would be ridiculously yum for the price. The verdict? Not so much.

For less than $10, you get a heaping bowl of steaming ramen that can probably feed a family of 4... or maybe 2 families of 4. It's seriously a gigantor-sized serving... so you do get a lot of food for your buck.

Taste-wise, it's really nothing to get too excited about. It's not bad, but it's not great, either. It's just okay. Not the most flavorful bowl of noodles I've ever had, but I wouldn't mind returning if I was near Porter Exchange and happened to be craving a big bowl of noodle soup.

If you're stuck between which of their 3 main soup broth flavors to choose (soy, miso, clear), I'd suggest going for the miso -- though it doesn't really taste like your typical miso soup broth, it's still the best-tasting one out of the 3, in my opinion.

The bottom line: We have been coming back here for three years. We like the simple and authentic ambience but we keep coming back for the food.

The pro's: The food is really tasty and authentic. There is a huge variety but the plates still come out quickly. We have had multiple dishes over the years, and we liked all of them, so get whatever you want and it will most likely be to your liking. The portions are very generous.

The con's: The service is somewhere between indifferent and unpleasant. It is authentic, but does not really belong. The prices went up a bit lately and it is no longer "that cheap place you go to whenever".

Holy portion size Batman!  I went here for the first time with friends a few months ago.  Ordered the unexciting combo of Chicken Fried Rice and Chicken Teriaki.  Little did I know that would consist of about 1/2 gallon of rice and 8 skewers of chicken amounting to about 1/4 lb of meat!  It was quite good and enough for two meals!  The service was fine and the price was very reasonable.

Emma's is one of those guilty pleasures I rarely afford because:

1. They don't deliver. 
2. They also don't serve pizza by the slice.
3. I live 30 minutes away.

That being said I've never had the pleasure of dining in. Which is probably to my fortune from some of the reviews of their customer service. Takeout though is pretty decent. I like that I can order half and half because their gourmet pizzas are delicious. Especially the sausage and caramelized onions. But as much as I enjoy sticking to the sweet flavorful sausage and onions I'd like to think variety is the spice of life and sometimes order the grilled chicken with gorgonzola. The guy behind the take out window even offers to heat it up for me when I arrive so it's not cold. I've had nothing but pleasant experience with their takeout and they do have some inventive flavors going on there.

I've never written a review before, but I felt the need after this experience.  Two words: food poisoning.

A few more words:
Everything that I ate here was bizarrely sweet.  I've never had tom yum soup that tasted like it had vast amounts of sugar in it and I've NEVER had green curry that was sweet either.  In addition, what they claimed was "spicy hot" barely registered as warm.

I've had maybe one or two deliveries from here. The food there is okay, nothing remarkable.   However, this is more for their MIT Truck, as that has given me a food option that allows a quick and easy way to buy some cheap food.

For $4, you can get a falafel, or a kebab, or something else with a pita, and a sodie pop.  Think about this.  A wrap filled with decent ingredients plus a soda for about half of what you will pay elsewhere in the Kendall area (and, with about a quarter of the line that Gooseberry's has, so when you need to get food quickly, it's usually quick and easy). 

Again, the food isn't the greatest, but it's definitely serviceable, and easy to get, in the Kendall/MIT area, and a much better deal than most.

Used to really like the food here, but the prices just went up (by about $2 on most Dosas and dishes) and the quality has seemed to go down.  The last time I went my rice was stale, and the naan, frankly, has never been very good.  Even the service - and I'm talking about taking-my-order-at-the-counter service - has gotten a little weird.  I find myself standing there forever until they decide to take my order, and when they finally get around to it, they're pretty miserable.

They're right around the corner and I'm sure I'll give them another chance, but my hopes aren't high.

Fabulous dinner! Ambiance was beautiful, the staff were attentive, friendly and extremely dedicated to their work. The Arugula Pizza was absolutely delicious! I spent my birthday here today and it was great!! Can't wait to come back soon! Reminded me of a little piece of Italy tucked away in Cambridge with an adjacent parking spot!!

While walking by in Central Square, we stumbled upon Asmara were able to look in through the windows to see what it was like.  We were intrigued and decided to come back for lunch one day.

During this lunch time, the place wasn't very packed, maybe two or three other parties seated total.  We were seated immediately and the place seemed to be run by one lady (probably the owner).  We were new to Ethiopian food and asked for suggestions.  She stated that a combination of some sort was the way to go, so we opted for the meat combination called the Sega Bebaynetu - $17.95 (per person).  The meat combination came with boneless chicken, spicy lamb, sautéed beef, and mild vegetable stew, mild lentils and a house special salad.  Everything is served is served on a platter and placed in the center of the basket in front of you.  

Traditionally, Ethiopians eat with their hands.  Injera, or their form of bread, serves as a helper to eat the piles of food with.  I found the injera itself crepe like in texture, and very different - slightly sour almost.  By itself it was a little off putting, but with the meat choices it was easily overlooked.  The flavors of all the meats in the combination were similar.  I found the "spicy" options not very spicy and better tasting than the non spicy options.  

There was more than enough of the meat combination for two of us and we opted to take the rest of it to go. I can't say I'm an Ethiopian food connoisseur, nor do I have anything else to really compare it to, but the food was just Ok in my opinion.  I wasn't wowed, but more so impressed by the overall cultural experience.

Had dinner here for first time. Grilled calamari was delicious, arugula salad with goat cheese and grapes was light and the flavors mixed well. Main course was pizza and both were great: chewy crust and the amount of toppings just right. Cappuccino was wonderful as was the chianti recommended by our waitress. Service was attentive and not rushed. Definitely will go back.

I went here with a friend on a Groupon.  I'd always wanted to go in here after Shilla closed down but was hesitant... I totally should have gone in before!  The food was freaking amazing, the staff were super nice & the atmosphere was really chill.  We're definitely going back!!  There was only 3 other couples in the restaurant (we went around 8pm) but I'd rather have that then Wagamama downstairs which ALWAYS looks packed and uncomfortable.
Yes, bakery supplies, and beyond! The cafe serves top notch Americanos and delicious baked goods from a North Shore supplier.

this is my first yelp review and basically i've never been so disappointed to see a restaurant so empty, so maybe this will help.  i got lunch take-out for my officemates at gran gusto yesterday, which entailed a random stroll through the back alleys of north cambridge's most exclusive office park parking lots, a nice change of pace for my mid-day break.  because there was no one else in the restaurant the owner(?)/manager/waiter/maitre d' antonio was more than attentive, eagerly making substitutions to appease my coworkers, and charmingly slipping into italian as he presented me with various creations straight out of their brick oven during my kind-of-long wait.

the food was more-than-delicious, i had trofie with zucchini and clams-- it was unique, light, perfect.  i liked it so much, that i took a friend back the same night, where we were met with the same hospitality and the tenderest of carpaccios and the most flavorful of risottos. 

the wait is a little long for take out--things move a bit slower at gran gusto, like i'd imagine they do in the tuscan countryside, or a neapolitan seaside town.  but i can't imagine a more gracious host, a better setting, or more delicious accompaniments with which to sit down, split a bottle of chianti and have some real chat.

unremarkable in every way. except for the service, which was remarkable in that it was almost comically bad. 

the turkey burger, i give a three-star "a-ok". the rest? meh.
Cold day + Slight hangover + Ramen at this place = All is well. Make sure to throw a little red chili paste in there to supply your hungry belly with a coat of delicious noodle-y fire.
The fresh rolls are amazing and grandmas's shrimp are mouth-watering. Green/red curry dishes on the dry side but VERY quick and friendly service. Recommended.

I think i've hit the jackpot! Gourmet pizza, killer service and great people watching. For dinner, there's a fabulous little dining room, for lunch, good luck with that unless you arrive early or have reservations with a party. For lunch I nab a pizza by the slice and eat outside on the bench or sometimes just take it back to the office.

It's a dosa place in the back of an Indian grocery store.  They play Bollywood music videos on a flat screen TV next to the refrigerator where you get your own drinks.  This place screams Central Square, and it is awesome.

The dosas are good - comparable to the old Rongoli out on Brighton Avenue, and a lot cheaper.  Sure, everything comes on an orange tray and in styrofoam, but I didn't come to a restaurant, again in the back of a grocery store, so that I could experience four-star china service.  I wanted cheap rice, some naan, and a good dosa, and these were all things I got, plus the unusual atmosphere of the place.  

If you're looking for more sophisticated dining experiences, this probably isn't the place to go.  If you want an interesting atmosphere, and cheap, solid food, this is a great location.  Plus, it's a little hidden, so you can get some hipster-cred points if you know about it and your friends don't.

Love dosas. Moved to Boston after spending some time in Nepal where I would eat dosas very often, I was so happy to get the dosas here! The aloo inside the masala dosa could be cooked better, but the dosa itself is delicious. Sambar can be very hit or miss and the achar/chutney aren't that great. But, the dosas themselves are great, as are the veggie pakotas, momos and vadas.

When I first arrived it was almost empty, and I was honestly worried that I had picked a terrible place. I was quite happily wrong! Dinner here with friends was a lovely experience. The gent behind the bar gave great advice about the wine, our table was ready when promised, our server was well-versed in the (admittedly somewhat short) menu, and the food was delicious. Between us we had excellent French Onion soup, petite cassoulet, chicken paillard, duck confit, and steak. The food was well presented, perfectly cooked, and delicious. The flavors on each plate were well combined. The atmosphere was light and welcoming. Highly reccomend.

Second visit to confirm my opinion/suspicions.

First of all I saw the Kalbi lunchbox and thought, "too good to be true"! Ahahah $10.95, too cheap... although the price was eye catching, the Kalbi itself was way above the monetary value. 

Absolutely delicious!! The sauce wasn't over powering and really let the high quality rib meat do the talking. A huge surprise because the decor leads you to think that the cuisine is as serious as the bubble gum pop chairs and kindergarten classroom walls. Like a bad Korean music video (they are all bad actually). The Kalbi had beautiful grill marks and the slightly charred flavor that accompanies something off a real grill. Very delicate and bold, flavors of the sauce and meat bouncing around your palate like a light switch being flicked on and off. 

Quiet down class room. 

The sushi on the other hand is a big negative. The maki that came in my lunchbox was a sloppy sad little thing, falling apart before I even touched it. 

On my first trip I got Sashimi for an appetizer and even then the knife work wasn't all that. The second trip I got the fried "jumbo" squid legs, which while very tasty, not quit jumbo, more like "not-a-baby-squid" leg, not quite adult. I would order it every time I go there though because they were some banging lil' squid legs!

The sides for the lunchbox were the same both times, two shumai, one gyoza... lame. Not exciting, nothing special, they tasted like pre-made frozen dumplings. 

The salad was also not all that, I mean the flavor was there but the lettuce ranged from tiny to huge. Sloppy, not rustic. The cucumber was cut on the bias and extra big for a piece of cucumber, also made it hard to eat, especially when using chopsticks. 

Now, I realize that they specialize in Shabu hot pot so... should I be disapointed? I have to go back for hot pot and judge that as well. 

Service: C+, clearly no front of house manager. One person took my order, one delivered food, one refilled my drink, one asked how every thing was literally less than one minute after the other did and there was only 3 waitstaff on so... I really wasn't sure who I was tipping. 

Bottom line: wouldn't recommend to friends, I would go for lunch by myself, like fast food, you don't really tell people to go to McD's just cause you eat there for lunch twice a week (now don't get it twisted, I haven't had fast food in years, efhghgfh ), don't think I would bring friends or guests.  Don't get the sushi. Dinner time might make me upset spending money here I think. This is a secret to keep to yourself for a great lunchbox deal in Harvard Square.

Yummmmm pizza.  love the combos.  good service.  you can even order by the slice.  super casual.  good beer.  good place to bring kids.  right around the corner from our apartment so can't go wrong with the location.    Some may say they don't like the consistency of the pizza, I happen to enjoy it, it's different.  Why would I go out for pizza if I wanted it to taste like pizza I could make at home? right?

They'll even let you do half and half pies, which is awesome. They just charge you for the more expensive pie, I think that's fair.
Adorable and unique baking supplies. And the best chocolate chip cookie I have ever had.

So, I am a huge worldcup Soccer fan.  Decided to go there for the Mexico vs. Argentina game.  Advertised in front of the bar they call themselves THE place for the Cup, so we decided to try it out.  We were 3 people and wanted to get a table at the bar next to the screen.  We were told from the beginning that sitting at a table in a bar was not possible if EVERYONE was not going to get a meal, we would have to sit at the bar if we wanted only drinks....one person wanted to order an entree but the rest of us were planning on having a pitcher of sangria ($25 a pop) and maybe some wings and got serious attitude from the hostesses and a few servers.  So, we could not sit in the "Tavern", at a bar table because they were an "upscale restaurant."  After talking to the manager we were finally seated at a table.  They lost a huge amount in what we would've ordered if (a) they would've been nicer (b) not given us so much attitude.    

We ordered salads, they were ok.....nothing special, huge portion.

I will NOT recommend this place and it is NOT the best place for soccer.

P.S. We chose to stay because the game was starting and really wanted to watch it....we would've missed a lot had we gone somewhere else.

Every time I go to Tavern in the Square in Porter, I get nothing but the best service! The service is always so friendly!  They greet me at the door and make sure my experience with them is always spot on!

Mary Chung is a Boston (er, well, Cambridge) institution of spicy szechuan food. The food is always delicious, and Mary herself is usually there to greet customers and oversee quality. However, despite the restaurant's ongoing popularity, the fact that it's usually full, and the sheer number of people who pass through, it's usually possible to get that one available table either immediately or almost immediately, and Mary will quickly come to recognize your face if you become a repeat customer.

The real treat of Mary Chung is the spicy dishes. Traditional chinese dishes like General Gau's Chicken have some actual kick to them, and traditionally hot dishes (marked on the menu) can, on a good night, actually be hot enough that people unaccustomed to spicy food might even feel a little pain... but I assure you, that having been through this "baptism by fire" my tears didn't stop me from recognizing the astonishing flavor and being eager to return. (Let's just say that after a hot night at Mary's, one may be grateful that Toscanini's Ice Cream is more or less across the street.)

Fans of the restaurant know that Mary's signature dish is Suan La Chow Show - just ask for a bowl of "swans". It is a dish of pork dumplings wrapped in noodles, served over a bed of bean sprouts, with a very spicy soy based broth. Spoon the sauce over the dumplings, take a good bite of one, and nab some sprouts with your chopsticks... it's heaven in a bowl, although a rather firey heaven at that. (A local artist designed a t-shirt gently lampooning the cover of "Make way for ducklings", showing Mary crossing the street followed by a line of children, all carring bowls of the dish... "Make way for dumplings".) Other house specialties are listed on the front of the menu.

One small word of advice, Mary does not accept credit cards.

For outstanding flavor and authentic spicyness, I highly recommend Mary Chung Restaurant. All of my friends have loved it for years, and now I take my family there when they visit, too. It's always a hit.

went there when it was limited kitchen menu...

was hungry...so we ordered nachos...  CRAP !!  

huge pile of crappy chips with cheese and sour cream on top...had to ask for salsa to make it better...( to no avail )

cocktails are ..eh~

pros :  beer, spirits, atmosphere   ...that's about it

this place is bigger than the central square one

meter street parking ( very limited parking lot )

only happening place in porter sq at nite ...but then porter is dead  no?

Came on a Tuesday night, and boy oh boy was that a big mistake! Do not get me wrong, the experience here was awesome, I just wished it were a Wednesday night instead. Upon arrival, I immediately noticed their whiskey selection, and I must say, I was pretty amazed. Now here's the part I wish I knew about. Our bartender, Kenny, had me sold when he told us they had an entire day dedicated to whiskey. Not only is there a free whiskey tasting, but also they have free appetizers with a purchase of whiskey. Now that sounds like a solid Wednesday.  

Back to my Tuesday night, I thought the food was delightful, but what I appreciated more was the atmosphere. My date and I had a great time. The room wasn't too loud to the point of screaming at each other, and when there was quite time (eating , I got to jam out to their music. A play list I would not expect this restaurant of this caliber to be playing, in a good way. I'd recommend this to place to anyone, as long as they are ready to have a good time and leave with a full stomach. 

PS: GET THE TRUFFLE FRIES, it's the key to success

Great experience at brunch. Great coffee. Great home fries. Good fresh egg scrambler. 

Small portion - but after eating, it felt right. Didn't feel stuffed like I usually do after a big Sun brunch. That was nice for a change. 

I gave 4 stars instead of 5 because my husband thought the food was good, but not excellent. 

Excellent service. LOVE the concept of natural food. 

I wish more people came here so there would be a vibrant feel. It was really (!!) empty which makes me feel sad for them!

I come here with my roommates every Tuesday night for 25 cent wings.  They come in a few different varieties: plain, buffalo, bbq, and teryaki sesame.  Having tried all of them, my favorites are the buffalo and the teryaki sesame.  In order to get the 25 cent deal, you have to order at least 10 at a time.  We usually 1 plate each, and sometimes 1 extrra to share.  One downside is that the deal only applies to bone-in chicken.  Sometimes I just don't want to deal with eating my chicken off of a bone, okay?

They also have an excellent selection of beers and mixed drinks.  My favorites so far have been the blackberry lemonade and the pomegranate beer.

I'm also a fan of the buffalo chicken nachos and the sweet potato fries with a maple dipping sauce.  They give you the option of adding truffle oil to your fries, and to quote one of the roomies: "everything is better with truffle oil!" 

Also, I have to mention the fried snickers dessert.  It was a bit too chewy, but since it combines fried food and a snickers bar (2 of my favorite things), I'll let it slide.  I've also heard good things about the chocolate lava cake.   I'll have to try that sometime.  (Maybe even tomorrow, after the wings!)

Went here on a Friday night with the wife and the atmosphere was really cool, but way too dark.  Wine list was pretty good too.

So, nice atmosphere, nice wine - unfortunately terrible servie and terrible food. 

We sat down pretty early and it wasn't too busy.  Still took 10 minutes just to get a waiter over to our table.

I've heard the gnocchi mentioned in other reviews and have to say it might be the worst thing I've ever eaten at a restaurant in a very long time.  Nothing like traditional gnocchi, it looked and tasted like microwaved mozzarella sticks from Shaws.

LOVE LOVE LOVE EMMA'S!

My husband and I have our favorite Emma's pizza (half #4 for him, half #18 for me) at least once every 2 weeks.   We ordered it for take-out again last night, which reminded me that I needed to leave a review.

The crust is super thin and crunchy  and delicious around the edges, but substantial enough under the rest of the pie that it isn't soggy.  I notice in a bunch of reviews people say things like "I don't like crispy crust and I went to Emma's and I didn't like their pizza."   Well, duh.  Their crust is crispy.  If you don't like crispy crusts, you're not going to like Emma's.  Don't go - problem solved.

The rosemary sauce is so delish, with a bit of a bite that really enhances my half of the pizza (#18 - baby spinach, artichoke hearts, sweet potato!).

Pedro's guacamole - who would think that a pizza place would have good guac?  But they do and it's drool-worthy, especially with the crispy yet chewy pita/pizza crust chips they serve it with.

The only two minor drawbacks (which are not enough to knock off a star - maybe a 1/4 star) are:
1) the dining room is so tiny that if you want to eat in on a weekend date night, you're guaranteed to have a wait - but they totally redeem themselves by serving you drinks in the waiting area!  Wine while I wait?  Yes, please!
2) the servers on occasion (maybe 2 out of every 5 times we dine in) sometimes forget about you - long waits for the check or for drink refills.  The odd thing about this one is that it tends to be when the dining room is actually less busy, like early Sunday evenings.

Speaking of Sunday evenings - thank you thank you thank you Emma's for opening up on Sundays now!  I don't know how many times in the past that we have been lazing around on a Sunday and were suddenly motivated to get out of our PJs to get Emma's, only to remember, "Darn it, they're not open."  Well, no more!  Thank you thank you thank (I can't say it enough!!!).

Emma, you'll always be one of my favorite Cambridge ladies!  (The other two are Christina [home made ice cream] and Anna [Taqueria]).

Just imagine it... vegetables made out of real styrofoam, served to you after an authentic long wait.  This place scrapes the very bottom of how bad a restaurant can be before I won't even bother to write a review of it.  Their marketing slogan should be, "Hey, at least it won't kill you."

Pretty darn good meal

Went with a gilt voucher    this meal was almost free. This meal would have  been excellent value at full price 

Great food great drinks  

Grilled Octopus
Mixed green salad
Parpaprdelle 
Sirloin
Burger

Returned here for dinner few weeks ago with not such a good experience. They seem to slipping in both areas; food and service. Served us cold raviolis. The sauce was lukewarm, and the ravioli center was ice cold. This raises suspicion whether these were fresh raviolis as touted by the staff and owner. When the time came for the bill, we used a gift card to supplement part of the bill. My partner purchased the gift card about two years ago, however the owner declined, he reported this only valid for one year, he went on saying that they updated their computer system and therefore "the system" won't accept the gift card. We were quite displeased, to avoid making a scene my partner stated that law in the state of MA - you as a business are required to accept the gift card as it has no expiration date on the actual card, and state law is written that if the gift card was purchased within 7 years you have to accept it. This experience was more that upsetting, as we were regulars at this establishment. However, the owner did not seem a bit moved. Needless to say they have lost loyal customers and our referrals. Farewell. Farewell.

Great place to come with friends and enjoy shabu shabu or some sushi.  The sushi's not as good as Genki Ya, but it's decent and there's plenty on the menu for your non-sushi-loving friends.  If you're not in the mood for sake, the lychee martinis are amazing!

Be warned that if you come here on a weekend night, you'll probably run into some pretty loud groups sake bombing.

From the cucumber cocktail to the gnocchi with rabbit to the succulent pork belly to the melt in your mouth halibut, we were wowed by each and every dish presented to us at Hops and Scotch Test Kitchen. Thank you so much Esok, can't wait to come back!!

I really enjoyed brunch here! They were knowledgeable about gluten free, and let me edit the dish I wanted in order to accommodate my allergy. The place was surprisingly not very busy for brunch, which made for a more relaxing morning. We emailed for a reservation and they got back to us the next day. The food was quite tasty! Looking forward to going back sometime.
Also, the open windows are quite pleasant on a warm night. The scotch flight is a great go-to.

Came here for dinner with my husband and ordered a noodle dish (a tad bland), pork rice cakes (a tad salty, but yummy) and green onion pancake with beef rolls. The food was ok, but I just moved here from Vancouver where the Chinese food is amazing so I might just be spoiled. For a place that says Taiwanese cuisine, I was surprised to see a lot of the typical dishes (eg. beef noodle soup) excluded from the menu. Service was prompt and friendly enough.
i really enjoyed the calamari, pad thai (more like vietnamese bun xiao) and rice.  lovely staff. cant wait to try more

Disclaimer: I didn't try out Sapporo Ramen until after I came back from LA where I had the best ramen I've had in my entire life so... that being said Sapporo was just not on par and far below what I would have liked from a Ramen shop in a Japanese food court at Lesley. Cafe Mami is probably my favorite, and while I had huge hope for Sapporo, I was also told by a fellow food lover that without Ken's Ramen at Super 88 in Allston, Sapporo was the best thing left in Boston. Sad, but true. I may return if I'm desparate for a restaurant quality ramen, but I will more likely be waiting for a seat at Cafe Mami.

Loved the place! Coffee is good, pastries are amazing and the place improved a lot since the shop changed name. A lot more happier, the owner is a lovely young lady that is eager to help you with anything you need. The place has 5 tables, if you can catch one of those enjoy! It's a nice place to catch up with a friend or study or work. I torre like me you're gonna love every detail of this shop. I totally recommend this place. They're open until 6 pm almost every day !
Amazing Taiwanese foods. My favorites are the salt and pepper pork chops, lamb with hot chilis, Mu Lan fish, pea pod stems with garlic, and string beans.
YUM.  Good stuff.  The bowls are smaller than most other ramen places I've been to, but still tasty.  Kimchi ramen there is good.

When I first posted a review for TITS I complained that it was super loud on a Saturday night with people watching college football. Clearly I didn't watch or enjoy football at the time.....

Now, however, I am obsessed with football and watch as many games as possible. This means that TITS is my go to place to watch all of the games and I love it. I often come with a large group of people and we get pitchers of bud light, nachos, and fried pickles. Their nachos are amazing and I'm obsessed with them. TITS is an awesome place to watch sports and has a great atmosphere--they have numerous TVS and they turn the sound up for important games. I watched the game here when Brady (aka the GOAT) returned from his four game suspension and had an amazing time.

Also, the 3 pepper mary is the best Bloody Mary I have ever had. My friends and I come here on Sunday mornings just to get 2-3 Bloody Marys to finish the weekend strong. Would highly recommend if you're a bloody fan.

If you're looking for some great bar food or a place to watch the game, definitely come to TITS.

The ambiance is great...the food is amazing.   I had the Truffle Oil Pizza and it was delicious.  There pizza is authentic italian pizza.  No other restaurant comes close.  NO comparison to any other.  The pasta is perfection as well.  Would come here again...PERFECT.

I used to go to previous restaurants in this location, some Taiwanese, and they were never good enough to justify dealing with the parking hassle.

The other day, I was driving past at lunchtime and noticed that there was actually a space in the ice-clogged lot, so I slammed on my brakes, missing disaster by inches, and pulled in.  The relatively unattractive restaurant was mobbed with Kendall Square engineers, many of them apparently Chinese/Taiwanese.  

With some trepidation, I ordered a lunch special, and it was amazingly delicious!!  The food was fresh and carefully prepared; they served a number of unanticipated tasty side dishes; and the portions are quite generous.  I actually couldn't finish my meal, and that never happens!  The tastes were original, and plate was attractively assembled.

If you can find a place to park, just go.  You will enjoy your meal.

Worst food and milkshake I ever had. I will never come back to this place. Dosa, uttapam, falooda and milkshake.... We tried everything and it was worst ever.
I won't even give a single star. Sambhar was only coloured water.

It's a good place to go if you're craving ramen. I've had better ramen. But this place is decently good but nothing to write home about. The Shoyu Ramen is a bit too salty for my taste. I usually go for the vegetable ramen now even though I'm not a vegetarian. And it's pretty cheap, which is always a bonus. 

One time I went here, it was crowded so we were seated with at a fairly large table with other people. Our poor neighbors had to endure my talking about male breastfeeding during lunch. So I guess overhearing unwanted conversations could be a downside.

I love the Dosa Factory! Great, yummy and warm Indian food with a nice atmosphere. Plus, it's connected to Shalimar Spice Shop, which is great because I can do my food shopping and enjoy a dinner out all in one. After spending some years in London, this is a great place to remind me of all the Indian shops and chat places in the UK.

If I had to pick my favorite dish at Dosa Factory, I would say it's the thali plate with the chicken masala and naan. This food totally hits the spot, especially after a tough yoga class at Prana Yoga. The portions are huge, and I always have leftovers for lunch/dinner the next day. Bobby Singh, the manager, is super nice, too.

tl;dr pretty good option near the Kendall for Taiwanese good, and has cheap+fast delivery.

I've been here several times with various friends and have also ordered their food for catering and basic delivery. I love the dishes I have ordered from them, specifically the eggplant with garlic, mooshu pork, Chinese squash. One of my friends really likes their Spicy Fish dish. I didn't like their fried chicken pieces. I was expecting popcorn chicken but the pieces were too small and thin and were more breading than chicken.

Their restaurant is decently sized, with tables of different sizes. I don't recall ever having to wait to sit the couple times I went.
Delivery wise, they're pretty awesome. The food always comes very fast. The online website order form is a bit annoying to use, but not horrible. 

When I catered from them, I also had a great experience. Only minor issue would be that their English isn't great, so if they get lost and you don't speak Chinese, you might be in trouble.

The quality of the food is good enough. 
But the issue I have is they are over priced for the quality and quantity you get. I ordered through the yelp app and they missed 2 of my ordered items. Grub hub refunded only for the price of the food. That was annoying in itself but then the portion of rice that I had to pay extra for wasn't even enough rice for the curry dish I ordered. I feel totally ripped off.
We had short rib khao soi and the street noodles.  So delicious and so authentically Thai.  Wonderful couple in the kitchen and out front.  Certainly you should go to see for yourself.

This place was pretty good! I got the spicy miso ramen it had a lot of bean sprout and a lot of noodle, but not that much meat. I went here because there was about a 90 minute wait at yume and there was no wait here. The broth was not that salty but it tasted weird after a white maybe it was the thickness of the soup. The meat tasted like meat from mala tofu. Overall this place was not bad.

Mae was my favorite restaurant not because of the quality of their foods but due to the warm & welcoming atmosphere that the servers provided every time I come in. Although the food price here is very high, I kept come back several times because their customer service really impresses me. However, the last time I was there, the server brought out the bill when I & my friend were not finished with out foods ( I also did not ask for the bill). To me, bringing the check out before it was asked for is quite rude and ,as I assume, this is a a sign that they would like us to leave (even when the restaurant was not crowded at all).

Love this place! Quick service, good prices on tea, and friendly staff. Came in to work on a paper, and I liked the jazz they were playing so much that I didn't even listen to my own music. 
They have a great variety of baking supplies, I'll for sure come back to check that out more.

Delicious fresh rolls, served with a savory sauce with crushed peanuts. Khao soi is huge, with ribs. I'd love a bit more broth and fewer crunchy and soft noodles, but that's just me. 24 hour marinated steak salad was tasty, just know that it is steak, a few herbs, cucumber slices and sticky rice and not a large pile of greens and herbs like I mistakenly thought. Loved the sauce!

I've read most of the reviews, and most complain about the cleanliness of the place... I mean, what do you expect? You're walking into this super tiny store that is an Indian supermarket, an Asian bao place, and a dosa place. I would be surprised if I walked in to see this clean and nice restaurant, so to start off, it really isn't that bad.

Probably one of the better dosas I've had in the states. The crepe itself tastes a bit more buttery (or maybe some other type of oil?). I got the lamb dosa and there were 5 tiny piece of lamb (which was very VERY delicious). For what I paid for, i definitely think it can be cheaper, but nonetheless not a bad place, but not super exceptional either.

I've tried 3 places in the Boston area for Ethiopian - Lucy's, Addis Red Sea, and Asmara. Asmara wins on flavor, portion, menu variety. Their combos are delicious. If you can, get the meat combo and the veggie combo to share between people. Also try the kitfo. Sometimes I've left here feeling like I've had too much salt but that's really the only thing I can fault Asmara on. Excellent service! They also take reservations. I have eaten here with a party of 2 and a party of 12 and everything inbetween - great every time :)
I tried the honey wine once and didn't like it that much though.
Asmara is one of my favorite restaurants in town.

Went there for Chinese new year. The place was bustling!!! The service despite how busy it was was fast and effective. The food was delicious and definitely one of my favorite Taiwanese spots I Boston. We got the dumplings which were perfect, the Mulan fish was spicy and flavorful, and the Dou Miao
Wow! Baking supplies that cannot be found anywhere else combined with delicious baked treats. Super unpretentious nice people with a warm welcoming environment to work and relax in. Love this place.

Maybe its because I'm chinese but this is really not the place if you want authentic good Chinese food,  any place in Chinatown would have been better than this.  Everything was bland and very greasy, in one of the dishes we ordered  (Which i cant remember the name right now) the pork was still cold in the inside which means it was probably reheated,  the dumplings we ordered was so bland and the filling inside was dry somehow. I mean the service was ok but i will never be returning considering all other good Chinese restaurants in that area.

the elmendorf team and energy is the warmest. their products are exceptional. the cafe's drinks and sandwiches are stellar. the service is aces. unbelievable selection of flours. the breakfast sandwich is divine. awesome place. awesome humans.

My boyfriend and I were heading out of town after a very exhausting visit with my family. I had always claimed that the East Coast has better pizza than what we were used to back in LA, so I wanted to make sure that we ate something delicious before leaving.

Upon the recommendation of my brother, he told us to go to Gran Gusto (citing their high ratings on Yelp) and it's ease of parking (since we had a rental car). We arrived around 2:30, right as the restaurant was closing to prepare for dinner service. I won't lie...I whined a little bit, and told him we had come out of the way to come here (which wasn't a lie). 

He checked in the back kitchen, and brought us to a table, which I profusely thanked him for. We were the only ones in the entire restaurant. 

We ordered the Rughetta salad, and their special for the day: a prosciutto pizza with buffalo mozzarella and truffle oil. Everything we had was DELICIOUS! The salad was perfect, the pizza was so tasty and FOR ONCE this is a place that knows how to use truffle oil properly instead of over-using it in a dish. Even the foccia bread at the table was fantastic!

And for such a nice meal and the fantastic service, it was around $40. Amazing.

So yeah, I have no idea when I'll be back here if ever but that should not stop anyone that wants delcious gourmet pizza by people that actually know what they're doing!

I love this place, and this review is long overdue. I am in the store at least once a week for either supplies or snacks and to bother the owners, Teddy and Alyssa, for tips and recommendations. Anything as a baker that you could possibly need, they will have it. Citric acid? Check. Gelatin sheets? Of course. Malted milk powder? Duh. You need einkorn, they have einkorn. Spelt, they've got it. And about a dozen other heritage corns, oats, grains, and wheat. They even have Carolina Gold rice. Getting into sourdough? Teddy has a beautiful starter and you can pick up your banneton, lame, and a copy of "Flour, Salt, Water, Yeast" or "Tartine Bread" here. You can also pick up fresh yeast, high quality chocolate, apricot seeds, candied and dried fruits, food grade lavender, and just about any niche baking ingredient that you could possibly imagine. 

And baking utensils? They've got you covered. Digital scales, Nordic Ware pans, Ateco pastry tips, assorted cookie cutters, wooden mooncake molds, tortilla presses, etc. And if you're a fresh milled wheat fanatic, you can even buy your own Mockmill here and take your grain to mill at home - but if you're local, they'll mill any of their grains on demand to whatever level of fineness you need. Round out your shopping with adorable linens, aprons, and oven mitts that make for great gifts. And their prices are insanely good, so there's no need to order online when you can pick it up down the street. 

This place is heaven for any baker, and that's before you even get to the cafe part. They serve Barrington coffee (and you can grab a bag to go), and seasonally you'll find treats like apple cider and pinole. The baked goods are fantastic: gooey soft chocolate chip cookies, flavorful scones, and whatever Alyssa feels like baking up that day (the ginger and pear muffins are my daughter's favorite). AJ King pastries round out the breakfast fare, and you'll find delicious sandwiches made with their own bread. This is truly one of my favorite places in Cambridge.

Great baked goods, great coffee -- a terrific little coffee shop that just happens to have cool and useful baking tools and equipment and entertaining cookbooks to browse.  It's a great mash-up.

We tried the gingerbread tower cake, the Kouign Amann, and the sticky bun on various occasions.  All impressive in their own ways (apparently from AJ King Bakeries in Salem).  The espresso is smooth and seriously good, and the service  super friendly to the point that you'll feel like a regular in no time.  At the time we visited the couple who own the place were also manning the counter.  They seem passionate about their endeavor, capable and knowledgeable, and like just really likable people you'd want to hang out with.

Definitely worth a visit if you're in the neighborhood, and probably a trip to East Cambridge if you're not.
The best place to buy freshly milled flours for baking.  Bought many bread making supplies here during the covid outbreak.

I wasn't sure which kind of cuisine to order here- Vietnamese? Thai? Chinese? After hearing the waitresses speak Thai to each other, I decided to order Thai because I think they would be better at cooking Thai food. 
This place is small and does not take reservations. They don't serve alcohol either. We had to wait about 15 min for a table but it was worth the wait. 
We started off with the golden bag for our appetizer, which resembled small samosas filled with potatoes and peas. It was a bit weird that it came with sweet chili sauce, I decided it tasted better without the chili sauce. 
For my main course, I had the original street noodles. The noodles come with soup on the side. Do not pour the soup into the noodles, you eat it separately. The noodles have chili spice on the side, the waitress told me to mix in a little of the chili powder, and adjust the spiciness level accordingly. Being over confident of my capabilities to handle spiciness, I mixed in all the chili powder... and man it was too spicy for me. I managed to get through the bowl of noodles, which was delicious by the way, but kept thinking of going to 7 11 across the street to get milk!
Luckily after 10 min of torture, the heat went away and I did not need to buy milk.
There are 4 level of spicy for all the foods, mild, medium, hot and Thai spicy. Be careful of Thai spicy, it is super spicy!
I look forward to coming back so that I can try all the other dishes.

Loved this family owned restaurant!! The boot noodle soup was so fresh and the service was great! It is getting chillier and restaurant didn't turn on the heat yet, so it was bit colder. The food was so delicious and worth wearing my jacket throughout dinner. I didn't love the Thai bubble tea. The tapioca balls weren't great. But overall, clean food!

Lived in Thailand for almost 2 years with my wife. We both agree Mae is hands down the most authentic Thai restaurant in the greater Boston area. It's better than some restaurants in Thailand. Kua Kling is a dish you'd be hard pressed to even find at most Thai restaurants in Boston. Eating their Kua Kling instantly gave me flashbacks of sitting in a southern Thai restaurant. It should also be noted that the service also friendly and excellent.

I can't help to love this place for its amazing Thai food and even more impressive service (they practically refill my water as I'm drinking it).  

This is a small and understated restaurant where the focus is more on food and less on classy customers and sexy decor.  Order food to the spiciness of your liking and pick any dish.  You can't go wrong.  I'd recommend the chicken satay and salad rolls as appetizers and the Pad See-eu (if you only like Pad Thai, go just up Mass Ave  to Sugar and Spice, their Pad Thai is better).

This is such a hidden gem in breat boston area! After I took a bite of the pizza I felt so lucky I was  that I found this restaurant hidden in a residential area in somerville. Very authentic Italian food; much better than most of them in the north end. My roommate and I were the only Asians in the restaurant that day: that was how we know whether an Italian restaurant is good lol

My roommate and I had a reservation at 5:15 pm. When we arrived we were seated immediately. The restaurant wasn't filled when we arrived, but it was quickly filled with people during our meal. The server was very nice and welcoming, with an italian accent English and sometimes with random Italian words coming out, another aspect to show how authentic the food is here:) 

My roommate and I shared a compo salad, the o re pizza and the pacheri pasta.  For the salad and the pasta, when we told the waiter we were going to share, they just served it in separate plates, each with half a portion. Everything was so delicious! My favorite was the O re pizza, that was much much better than the American style pizza and I suddenly understand why those Italian grandma's reacted so furiously when they tried the american pizza or pasta in those tasty videos lol The pizza dough was so soft and airy and the mozerella cheese on top was the best I have ever had. I didn't even realize that it was mozzarella until my roommate told me it was. It was very creamy but not heavy at all. The star of the pizza was the truffle slices and truffle oil drizzled on it. Even before I put the pizza in my mouth I could already smell the truffle flavor, tat umami bomb. Usually I can only finish one or two slices of pizza. That day I had three slices, along with a salad and pasta, and I did not feel bloated at all :) 

The pacheri pasta was also very delicious and the presentation of the dish was also on point. They definitely made fresh pasta in house and the ragu sauce was very flavorful. The short rib was also cooked to perfection: very tender and well seasoned. The fresh rosemary not only served as a decoration, its flavor also blended very well with the short ribs.

Highly recommend this restaruant and will definitely come back and try their other dishes!

Brought my family here after graduation but honestly delicious every time we go! We got the thai iced tea, fritters, sirloin, pad krapow gai, mango curry and the ribs bowl. The overwhelming favorite was definitely the pad krapow gai which was both spicy but we couldn't stop eating! Also, be sure to save room for dessert--the portions do tend to run large so entrees are perfect for sharing.

I LOVE Mae! It's very much a mom-and-pop restuarant and the owners are very nice. My boyfriend was craving Asian food one night and we came here. We've been a lot of times since as the food is really good and there's a wide selection--soups, noodle dishes, curries, thai dishes, but it's not an overwhelming number of options. My favorite dish is the "fat kid at heart " which has Chinese broccoli and crispy pork belly. My boyfriend's favorite is pad kra pow gai. It's easily our favourite place in Cambridge. I also like to enjoy a Thai iced tea while here.

Soooo good and affordable. A hidden gem! I LOVE the spicy miso ramen, it's my favorite. Place can get busy and depending on the time there may be a wait but TOTALLY worth the wait! HIGHLY recommended!

Wonderful Ethiopian food, I come here every time I am in Cambridge. Some of the selections can be a bit spicy though if you're spice sensitive so make sure you ask the waitress about mild choices before your boyfriend complains about how he can't eat the chicken. Lol. Always a cool crowd that eats here though and order the honeywine! It's a delicious and authentic accompaniment to the injera dishes.

I just went to this cute little baking shop and bakery for the first time, and I'm in love. The staff was very knowledgeable and were able to tell me about different yeasts for different purposes. They sell both cute and practical supplies, and they have great pastries (many from AJ Flour). I tried the goat cheese and olive scone which was different and tasty with a little kick and the Kouign Aman, which was pretty good (Flour's is still my favorite). My husband thought the chocolate chip cookie was exceptional too!

So we been trying different ramens since that's what I been craving lately during my pregnancy... sad to say I was a little disappointed we didn't finish either bowls. My spicy one was super oily. The gyoza was really good though. We sat outside and I wouldn't recommend anyone to do that because you are sitting in a parking lot next to where they put the trash. They sat us down and said call if you need help like no I don't want to have to get on my phone for you to check on me your customer

5/8/2011 month's day went this place in Walltham for dinner. The manager/boss just like to keep more customs than they can handle. The service is not good, the dishes are not good as described (spent $110 for three adults and two kids). overall, this is about average restrauant. I will neve come back again.
-George

Great service, and a great Asian option in the MIT area (dare I say the best option?) The prices are reasonable, and the portions relatively hearty. We ordered the crab rangoon (more cream cheese than crab, but very good), the calamari (very bad....almost inedible, sadly. I was comparing this to the calamari at Beantown Pho, and it was sorely disappointing. The calamari was too salty, the breading fell off immediately, and the jalapenos were not fried); the pad thai, and the small tom yum soup. My husband really liked the pad thai, and it came with large fried chunks of tofu. The main noodle dishes seem to be the highlight here, so next time we come here we will go straight for the entrees.

I'll preface by saying I've only ever dined-in at this restaurant once (when they first opened in January) and that this review pertains primarily to my subsequent delivery/take-out experiences (which have steadily declined in the past 10 months since I've been ordering from them).

My partner is Singaporean so we were very excited when we first learned about the opening of a new Southeast Asian restaurant in Cambridge. We stopped by for lunch right after the New Year and were not disappointed by either the food (it was during this first visit that I fell in love with their Indonesian Fried Rice and we were recommended to try the S.P. Calamari, both of which are frequent repeat orders of ours) or the hospitality. The restaurant is owned by a friendly wife-husband duo--Yuri (wife) is the main chef and Anil (husband) can be found behind the host/hostess counter when he's not travelling for other work.

When we first began adding the calamari to our delivery/take-out orders, while the portion size was on the smaller size, we still felt that the quality justified the cost (the calamari itself is crispy on the outside, seasoned to perfection, and served with a subtle spicy mayo sauce). However, with every order, the portion size only kept getting smaller and smaller until tonight when the take-out container was barely half-filled, and that's including 4 thick slices of jalapeno that were bigger than any piece of calamari we received. Mind you, the calamari does not come in a large take-out container to begin with.

What's more, my partner ordered the beef satay (+$1 for beef) tonight as he was craving satay. It was our first time ordering this small plate, and I don't think I've ever seen him so disappointed over the portion size of a dish. The order consisted of 4 tiny skewers of thinly sliced beef. Being perfectly honest, even I felt a bit cheated for $10.45.

I wish my only gripe was with the portion sizes of the small plates (the portion size of the other entrees are quite generous) and that I could say the flavors are on-point, but tonight the kitchen somehow managed to mess up our Indonesian fried rice. The fried rice tasted almost saccharine and conflicted with the flavors of everything else in the dish--not at all what I've come to expect from my most frequent repeat order. My partner and I ended up picking out the crispy chicken (which tasted slightly off but still palatable) and fried egg before we gave up on the rest of the dish entirely.

We've had good experiences ordering the following dishes in the past in addition to the calamari and Indonesian fried rice: A Fat Kid at Heart, Pineapple Fried Rice, Short Ribs Khao Soi, and Original Street Noodles. Of note, my partner brought his parents here while they were visiting over the summer and the taste of the food was subpar. He thinks it might have been because Yuri was taking a break from the kitchen and so the food was cooked by someone else, but suffice to say, I don't think we'll be ordering from here again in the foreseeable future.

Overall, the flavors here are fairly authentic (though not always consistent). I feel that you definitely get your money's worth with the larger entrees but stay away from the small plates if you don't want to feel like you're being robbed blind.

Loved this place! Ordered the Fat Kid At Heart and the Short Rib Khao Soi. Both dishes were lovely and I honestly hated that I was so full I couldn't finish the dish. Service was great! Our waitress was super attentive and diligent about keep our water refilled.
