I think I'd give Small Plates 3.5 stars. Last night we sort of wound up there and were pleasantly surprised by how charming and inviting the setting was. We stayed. The three of us ordered 5 tapas to share, each had a drink (beer and sangria) and then 2 desserts. 

The salads were okay, nothing spectacular. I did like the grilled peach that came with the baby greens salad. The lemon thyme ricotta crepe was delicious, but could've easily been a dessert. The summer squash fettucine was good, especially the  sauce. The wild mushrooms were sort of mediocre, missing some essential seasonings; they were the low point. The desserts, Ginger peach bread pudding and coconut rice pudding with a shot of mexican hot chocolate, were both good; the bread pudding came out as the highlight of the whole dining experience I think. 

Overall, the service was very good, the ambience lovely, the presentation was beautiful, the ingredients were creative and tasty, but I did feel a bit unsatisfied by the time we were finished. I don't usually feel hungry after eating tapas, so it surprised me. 

Oh! the bread and olive oil before dinner were amazing, maybe the best thing of the evening actually, but we weren't offered a refill, which was a downer. We could've used it to eat with all the sauces that came with the tapas. Next time, maybe. I'd go back to try some other things on the menu that interested me, but maybe not for a full-on dinner. It was a little pricey to wind up feeling a little hungry on the way out.

I'm not sure it even deserves one star. I've eaten there a few times and every time the food has been really bland. The rice was really dry, the veggies were undercooked, and the salsa was so-so. Personally I like when the cheese is melted on a burrito and here they sprinkle on shredded cheese. 
I like their naked burrito concept, it would work at another restaurant.
They do have outdoor seating at this location but you might be sharing a table with the neighborhood bum.

I would have to agree that this place really isn't that bad for a chain. They also offer the "naked" burrito (in a bowl instead of all wrapped up), which is great on so many levels. Less messy and no torilla to deal with.

Take this place at face-value. It's a national chain that makes good but over-priced Tex-Mex food.
Great food, friendly service, good prices. My favorite place to grab dinner near MIT.

I love this place. I would give it 5 stars but you come out smelling as though you've been doing all the frying yourself (read: the ventilation isn't that great, so don't wear anything here that needs to be dry-cleaned and don't go here before going to the movies or somewhere else). Awesome food, okay service, nice place to relax.

Honestly, in my past experiences with saloniki, I've really liked the food, which is why I ended up there today. However, I had a horrible experience. The girl behind the counter, first off, was extremely rude. She barked something about if I wanted feta and olives on my salad at me before I had even ordered a salad, and then proceeded to give me very annoyed huffed responses when I asked a question about one of the salads and was even more huffed when I decided to order a pita. She then proceeded to throw the food in my pita and gave me have the eggplant I normally get. The pita itself was burnt and cracked, proceeding to break immediately when I picked up my sandwich to eat. This was especially disappointing because saloniki's pita is my favorite part of their food, but this pita was basically inedible. I was just here two weeks ago, but apparently, their service and quality has drastically changed or is just highly inconsistent.

I had high hopes for this place, as it sports a menu that doesn't fall into the 'same old, same old' category as so many other places in town do.  Kudos for that for sure.  I also applaud them for taking the 'small portion' route, as that can be trickier than just decreasing the portions of a particular plate - to pull it off, they must contend with the fact that a small plate (from amuse bouche to true tapas) requires pacing of delivery and a balancing of flavors so as to not come across as just skimping on a cheaper, smaller entree.

(heck I think even entrees should have the same attention to balance, but that's for another place and review)

So our table of 5 ordered a sampling of the menu so that we could all enjoy.

and then we sat and waited.  and waited some more.  and then waited a bit more before getting an amuse bouche followed by a hummus and fruit/cheese plate.  the amuse...didn't - it was unbalanced and terribly salty, and frankly a mess to try to get all the flavors on the fork we were left with using to scoop it up.  the hummus plate had some highlights, but nothing remarkable.  to be honest I think the highlight was that there was food on the table at this point.

and then we sat and waited some more.  granted, we were lucky to be a solid group of friends with great conversations over an increasing amount of wine, but after the third "I just saw your food, it's coming out in a minute", the conversation starts turning to 'where the eff is the food the watiress keeps saying she just saw??'

eventually it all arrived, but at a pacing that we were close to 86ing the last two plates not so much because we were filling up, but more because we just were tired of waiting-then-eating as we approached hour 3 at the table.

for the plates themselves, some shined (bistro steak, killer B's, mushrooms) and some really fell flat (paella, cod). the rest were so middle of the road that I am having a hard time remembering what else arrived.

the service itself was sweet, if peppered with empty promises of arriving food, but I understand that that could be mostly a reflection of the kitchen and not our waitress.  And I do understand that sometimes the best meals in the world are those that buck against the typical American "eat fast and move on" approach to meals, where food is properly paced so as to prolong a wonderful evening of food, drink, conversation - but part of that perfect pacing is that you don't notice it, and we all did.

So I finish this review, and reflect on the experience and can honestly say, yeah it wasn't terrible.  Heck every restaurant has offerings that I would migrate towards or away from - not everything is going to satisfy everyone - so there isn't really something I can point out as being bad or negative - it felt like it stumbled a bit.
Weird setup for a cafe. Yummy and decent prices. Comparable to a Panera Bread.

We absolutely LOVVVE the City Girl Cafe!! At least one day per weekend, I wake up  thinking about City Girl, and drag my boyfriend and sister out the door at 9:50 just in time to miss the inevitable line that builds up before the doors open. On the menu, the Verde is to die for. I will never order anything else. The red bliss potatoes make my life. I make sure to take everyone of my visiting guests to City Girl for a memorable meal. I love the staff, the service, the atmosphere, obviously the food, and in particular the unique energy buzzing in the cafe. I recommend City Girl to anyone and everyone looking for a quality, dank and one of a kind breakfast experience.

I recently had Sarah's Market & Cafe cater a breakfast event for my organization. I worked with Raj on the order, and he was more than cordial and professional during each of our conversations. I placed a large order (that later had to be adjusted) for breakfast sandwiches that were vegetarian and non-vegetarian friendly. The sandwiches arrived on time and individually wrapped, as requested. Everyone commented on how tasty the sandwiches were and inquired as to where they were from.

Another thing to note: my order was supposed to include paper products (napkins & plates), but when it arrived, plates were not included. Raj apologized, and I assured him it was not a big deal. About 10 minutes after the delivery had arrived, I got a call--Raj had returned with plates. Now THAT is customer service.

I have never dined at Sarah's but based on my experience with their catering service, I will certainly be enjoying a meal there soon.

Where to start? The bagels are perfect, and handmade, and from a sourdough starter from like a billion years ago I think, and delicious, AND I live in New York. And YES: the line is long! Because people want the bagels! Sure, if you don't have the time to wait, I'd suggest not waiting in a really long line. It's a little crowded in there, but since MOST people know what they've gotten themselves into (deliciousness in exchange for a little closeness-to-your-neighbors), everybody I've ever stood next to or accidentally bumped is very friendly, and it didn't take too long to get a seat. 

There's been a lot of commenting on prices. First of all, relatively speaking, it's not going to break the bank to go here every so often. But yes: the bagels and their various toppings and combos are more expensive than stopping at D&D or something like that. For me personally, I prefer to NOT spend my money on those cheaper, wayyyy less delicious options in order to spend my money on something that's worth it: (the something that's worth it? You guessed it! Anything from Bagelsaurus!) But again, that's me! If it's not your thing, then yeah, don't expect something super fast to hurriedly eat and consequently not even taste on your way to the train! But if you do subscribe to "slow food" and really savoring something special, dig in. Cause Bagelsaurus is really incredible. And amazing. And did I say DELICIOUS??

Mary and her team are an incredibly sweet bunch who are dedicated to good food and the care of making things from scratch, with both creative and tradition rolled in.

I live in the 'hood and come here often. The burger is the equivalent of a gourmet whopper and it's OUTSTANDING. Every time! Also, the ribs are fantastic- really tasty. I barely even use the sauce, they're so good on their own!
The drinks are superb- Rob the bartender is the man. He should always be the one making your drinks.
Love the staff, but my only caveat is when they remove some of my faves from the menu, and the occasional slow response from staff. Otherwise, GO. And, I'll be honest- stick with the burger, drinks, certain salads, and ribs.

I love the  vibe here.  It's pretty much always crowded when you go (for "crowded" being "good luck getting a seat at the bar").  Drinks are solid, food is a little pricey for bar food, but tasty.  This isn't a place I'd come every week but I'll pop in for a drink when the mood strikes.
Had a solid meal. Excellent cocktails and good service. Charred octopus and the salmon were the stars of the food. We will be back.

The place is nice. The oysters are fresh. I ordered the rabbit but it was rather bland. My husband had the linguini with clams and that seemed to be very tasteful. Our waitress was dull and it was noticeable so we didn't want to bother her asking for dessert and went across the street.  This place is probably better for after work drinks and small plates.
i will admit that i didn't give it much a chance because i found it to be hobbit-y. I will try again one day.

i asked for a butternut reduction when ordering a chalupa.   

they obviously got the order right.

mmmmm

When the signage on your establishment proudly boasts "Lavazza; Chai and - most importantly- Free WIFI", you have guaranteed my first visit. And ABC has succeeded in bringing me back.

Though as I settled in with my au lait this morning the internet was being fussy, the kind gentleman next to me at the long, sunny bar area (equipped with plenty of plugs- bonus points!) alerted me as soon as it was back up and running. 

The place is sparse but clean and at post-brunch, peak time on a Sunday in Central, it is full but doesnt feel overrun. Ella Fitzgerald- esque music on the stereo is calming and invites laptop- totters looking for workable ambiance. 

Most importantly, coffee hits the spot and comes in generous- sized mugs. The muffins are amazing. I have been dreaming of the bran muffin (yes, one can dream of bran) since I had it a few months back. As soon as I finish this review I am getting up for one (though the pumpkin is tempting too). I have enviously eyed the cheese plate, which comes with those butterfly crackers- amazing! I haven't justified buying a $7.50 cheese plate while studying but I'm slowly getting there... I'll save it for the next time I am back.

I live in the neighborhood, have tried everything on the menu, and it's all great. I don't know what's up with the mixed reviews because i've taken many guests to Kika and everyone has always loved it.  Some of my favorites: sauteed artichokes, octopus in olive oil, lamb balls in truffle sauce, goat cheese in tomato sauce, steamed mussels, beef tenderloin with spicy herb sauce, pork loin with blue cheese and mushrooms. The service is inconsistent, sometimes excellent, sometimes far from it.

Awesome sandwiches.  Worth a trip to Harvard Square just to have the "Mr. and Mrs. Snob" turkey and asparagus sandwich or great baked goods, best eaten on the outdoor patio on a sunny day with fresh lemonade.  True, prices are high but you get what you pay for.

River Gods is a Celtic-themed pub that serves pretty delicious vegetarian tacos. The vegetarian tacos had some kind of mush in the middle.  

Came here on a Wednesday. It was not too crowded, which is great because a crowded room is the worst. The music was not too-too loud and I appreciated this. It took me awhile to figure out the theme because i came anticipating a tiki bar. After that confusion was sorted out, I had an okay time. I will probably be back.

What a great sushi place.  The wait staff pays great attention, and the sushi is fresh and nicely prepared.  Best of all - they have a card for regulars that gets you 10% off (though I am always a bit confused about how it is supposed to work).  

Great neighborhood place, and great people.

I'd like to see them perhaps offer some more of the special, creative rolls, but I really can't ding them for that, being that their main offerings are so great.
Cozy little joint with great beers. I love that they go all-out decorating for Halloween.

I frequently visit this location and was a customer since 1995, my recent visits were less than pleasant, the new floor ay out was confusing, service was slow, glass doors were smudgy and dirty, floors were a mess and bathroom was less than sanitary. This is not what a food establishment should look like.
However the baked goods were fresh and delicious, I cannot say the same about the coffee, it tasted like warm milk.

Sara H sums it up better than I can or will.

Clearly, it is an Au Bon Pain and I never go to another one, but this one my fiance go to most weeks after Mass.  Oddly, there is a good atmosphere.  Old folks going there as a regular thing, a few troubled people enjoying a non-judgemental atmosphere, a few scholars and a rather snooty Brit when I am there.

Somehow, it is rather nice.

There is absolutely nothing redeeming about this Qdoba. NOTHING. Not the location, not the service, not the food. If I could give it zero stars, I would. Everything I've ever ordered there is bland and overpriced. I paid six bucks for one slice of cheese melted in a tortilla, which in Qdoba-ese is apparently a quesadilla. Every time I've been in there, I have to wait at least five minutes to place an order, even if I'm the only person in line, because the employees  are always chatting with each other and barely focusing on what's going on. One time they tried to make me pay twice for my food, something that would have been avoided if they had been paying attention. I can't even praise the location because Anna's is right down the street, and they do the same thing better for cheaper (and I'm not even a big fan of Anna's either). Stay away. Far away.
My students earned a pizza party recently.  Pronto is close to us so I figured I'd give them a try.  They were so kind and the pizza was great!  I definitely would order from here again.

I love tacos!

I do not love these tacos!

I made a big mistake by going here AFTER working out and thinking I'd treat myself.  I treated myself to disappointment.

Does Qdoba mean misfortune?

I do not like!
Qdoba is great.  My suggestion, try the naked burrito with a side of chips.  Then stir the burrito in to a delicious soup and dip your chips in it.  Eat once a week for strong bones and a big smile.

This is a great spot for a cafe workday. It's a quiet and roomy cafe with lots of small tables and a few extra room. There are plentiful outlets and the wifi is very fast, an order of magnitude faster than the Harvard wifi. It took me 3 minutes to download a file that was going to take hours on Harvard networks. The staff are friendly and there is almost always space to work.

I am not a frequent coffee drinker, so I cannot really comment on the drinks. They seem typical of any chain coffee shop like starbucks or peets. Not mind-blowing but certainly good and with plenty of options and typical prices.

Bottom line: Reasonable prices, fresh ingredients, 2 blocks from my house.  Ok I'm a fan of all of that.

Got take out from here the other day with one of my housemates and was very happy with it.  Granted their menu is a little hard to figure out if you're not all "up" in the sushi lingo, but none-the-less I managed to find things that I liked.  Had some maki rolls and my friend had some as well.  We didn't get any miso soup (though you guys rave about it) because to be honest we had enough food as it was.  Between the two of us it ran $25.  Not too shabby.

Decor in the restaurant is a little.. uh.. meh.  But I don't think it matters cause i'm walking out of the joint anyway.  Service was friendly and prompt.

Will go back whenever i'm in home and craving sushi.  Beats out snappy sushi.

I have to admit, I have only been here 3 times but that is in the past week of discovering this little gem.  I have purchased the same thing all 3 times as well: toast with jam and butter to go.  Oh man, the toast is delicious, fresh, and you get 3 slices - plenty of bread for one person!  Every time I have received a different jam and all were tasty, though slightly over sweet.  
I haven't tried any of the pastries but the display is cute and tempting.  I am apprehensive about that fifth star only because the prices of everything seem fairly steep.

Hey heard about this place online and went with my girlfriend to check it out. The locations is great, it's only a couple of blocks from the central square stop which is convenient and it's in a very inconspicuous residential area. 

Inside is crazy looking and super cramped but that's sort of the uniqueness to it. Service was great; nothing extravagant or anything but was friendly. The food was pretty good, I'd say about $10-15 for an entree. I'd have to go again to say anything conclusive about the food. Also they apparently have local DJ's spin at 8 PM.

This is my family's go-to sushi restaurant. We've had many wonderful meals here, and love it. We primarily eat sashimi and maki and find the fish to be reliably superior and often exceptional. My youngest does not like the idea of raw fish, but loves their Beef Teriyaki and Tempura. It's a fun and relaxed place to take friends.

We also like the staff and owners -- two brothers. They're warm and friendly, and many have worked there for years. Also they are always welcoming, even when the restuarant is busy and you show up with a few extra friends.

Finally a word about some of the negative reviews. We've eaten at Hana for over ten years, and never have we had any disastrous meals or bad experiences. The only thing can agree with is the pace of service. I see it as relaxed -- It's a sushi restaurant and not and Olive Garden after all. But on busy nights it can be a little slow.

The food was lackluster in our experience. The service was good and the staff was nice. The seating area in the courtyard in the back was pleasant for an early dinner. The drink selection was nice and original. However, I was quite disappointed with all the food we ordered, which included the warm appetizers platter, southern fried catfish and paella.

Three stars for the amount of food I got for the price I paid. $20 for a rice bowl and two shishito orders. However, as pretty as it looked, I found my bowl to very bland and flavorless. 

I got a bowl with half jasmine rice and half arugula topped with shaved Brussels, kimchi, bulgogi beets, smashed cucumber and Japanese eggplant. I topped it with corn nuts and spicy gochujang. 

The rice was so over cooked it was mushy and all my vegetables just tasted raw - the beets were just crunchy small cubes with no seasoning, the kimchi was slightly marinated cabbage. What I had hoped to customize was an "Asian" flavored bowl and the only items with flavor were the eggplant pieces that were roasted down and the gochujang. 

The shishito peppers were very tasty and had plenty of seasoning but that was the highlight of the meal. 

I'm glad I came and tried it as this place is hyped on instagram, but I don't think I'd come back.

The people:  very nice
The pizza:  mediocre at best
The rotis:  awful bordering on gross

Does anyone actually go here?  I think that ABP in general has zero value proposition.  The coffee is inferior to Starbucks and Dunkin.  There's nothing particularly interesting about the food or pastries.  Not a fan of ABP, though this particular location is no worse than others.

I had a great experience at this place.  Got the artichoke pate, spinach sandwich on focaccia bread, hold the feta or whatever furry cheese it was.  It was AMAZING!  Got a half order of mac and cheese for my 2 year old.  We went during a really slow time and had no problem getting a table or with the service.  Wait staff was super nice and friendly.  Prices seemed average tome and well worth it for that sandwich.  I will go again and get the same thing but there were lots of other good vegetarian items on the menu.

Ordered delivery tonight as we are staying in Boston and didn't want to go out after a long day. We waited an hour which I figured is reasonable for a Saturday night. Food was okay, French fries were hard by the time we got them and we didn't receive any utensils, salad dressing or napkins. Made it pretty difficult to eat a salad in a hotel room.... likely wouldn't order from here again.

The place was disappointing in every way. 3 out of the 7 people at our table had to wait for literally an hour and a half to get their entrees and when they finally arrived the food was extremely mediocre. The "tapas" were fair to middling, the gravlax was way too fishy and thickly cut. Everything was poorly seasoned. The wait staff ranged from flustered to nasty. I will never go to SP again.

With an extensive menu and complex flavor, Taco Bell of Cambirdgeside goes beyond your typical free sample encrusted mall fare. Service here is above average and everyone seems very friendly! 

ONLY DOWNSIDE=NO BREAKFAST- really that's it
Very interesting decor and very different. Tight seating but all in all a good experience. Would recommend for local dining.

I would give 10 stars if it was an option!  Great food, very friendly staff.  We had the Herc  So fresh and delicious.  The sauce and herbs on this pita, not to mention the pita itself, are just so good!  Wish they had something this good in Jersey!  I would drive an hour for this food!

It's Taco Bell. It's fast food. It's great if you find a store that cares about that bag of pseudo-Mexican delights vended.

This is one of those places.

Crunchy tacos w/ too much hot sauce = yum.

I went back for a second round a couple of weeks later. They must have magical ingredient matching fairies at HQ. How bout that Quesarito? Just when you thought it impossible to mix and match 7 ingredients a different way.

So, I originally ordered from pronto with the hopes of getting a chicken po boy with asian slaw. I love Asian slaw and it made my day. I also ordered corned beef hash well done. Well after waiting my normal hour for delivery I received an order that was sub-par. First, my po boy had bacon and teriyaki sauce on it. Neither of which I eat, nor was it described in the menu. Second, even tho I understand that it was delivered. My hash was in no way well done. Upon calling about this situation, the manager told me the next time I ordered I would receive 30% off my total meal. Not caring about the 30% off my order, I have continued to try and order from this establishment with no luck. After multiple days of repeatedly trying to order on their website, I finally called. They told me they no longer delivering to my area. How are you going to mess up an order, guarantee a discount, and then denie delivery to a place you have delivered to before. I work in the industry and have been a manager for almost 20 years. But to guarantee something, and not follow thru , isn't right. This place is a joke. Just acknowledge you messed up, and deliver my food correctly, or refund my original order. If I managed like this, I wouldn't be in the industry any longer. Follow thru when you say you are going to do something. 
Best,
JR
I had very very very VERY bad diharea, I would recommend this place to a constipated person

This place is pretty delicious.  It's a deliciousness that you certainly pay a premium for, but that doesn't make it any less delicious.

The bagel here is a bit more like a California bagel than a real New York bagel, but I have zero issues with that at all.

The quality of the spreads and toppings is first rate, and their bagel is definitely better than most.

The food is great, the atmosphere is nice, the restaurant has  a nice bar, but its not really worth the $$. Thats the reason for my  three point rating. You need at least two to three dishes if you are hungry which sums up to a bill of around 80 dollars for a dinner for two. I love the idea and the concept,  but either the prices have to drop a little per dish or you just need to get more "tapas" for the same price, just as if you would eat Tapas in Spain.  Also, i did not like the way of our waitress too much, she really managed to interrupt our conversation 3 times, and she was trying to be beyond friendly in a very artificial way (probably for some extra tip) which just did not feel honest.

ABSOLUTELY worth the wait. I have waited in line for up to 30 minutes for one of their bagel sandwiches and I have never once regretted it.

Weekday: less likely to have a line. 
Weekend: there will be a line. Pick up coffee from somewhere on your walk to Bagelsaurus and you will have a much more pleasant wait. 

Favorite: Eggspanola sandwich (I usually choose this on an olive bagel). Over easy egg, Maplebrook feta, pimenton aioli, and parsley gremolata. It's the perfect savory sandwich that is tangy and creamy. I love it so much I rarely have the guts to try something new. However, their Hot Smoked salmon sandwich is equally good, but twice as expensive $11 so I get it once in awhile. They are also well known for their T-Rex (almond butter, banana, honey and bacon) which I have never tried but have heard plenty of good reviews.

Will need to revisit but my first experience was pleasant. Half the food was very good, but whatever had to do with seafood was bland or bad (the crab/mango stuffed avocado and the fried calamari). Go for the pork loin and the lamb meatballs which saved the day. The service was spotty (the waitress was completely confused at times), but on opening week that can be explained away. Funky bar area and overall nice vibe. Look forward to coming back soon.

Came in here today for the first time and had a similar experience to what another reviewer said on here. There was one man working and he abruptly asks his customers "What do you need?" God forbid I ordered something they offer on their menu, a strawberry smoothie, and was met with a grimace and moan and he says "It will take a while" as he walks away. He obviously was mad that I ordered a smoothie and not a coffee but if you offer something on your menu, you should be prepared to make it. And you should never make customers feel like they are in the wrong for simply ordering something that is on your menu!! I understand being understaffed (not sure why you don't have more than one person working during the morning rush hour) but that is no excuse to make a customer feel like they are doing something wrong for just giving you business.

I was excited to find a café like this close to where I work and would have come back for lunch today to try one of your salads, but now I will not. Thanks

Pizza is really awesome!! Quick delivery also! They didn't have chocolate ice cream BUT! They called me to let me know and I completely appreciate the call. Everything was so delicious. I'm absolutely ordering from them again!

The seafood pizza is pretty good, and the MIT special was pretty good.  I tried a pasta that was fairly mediocre (but I don't remember which one).  The side of vegetables was really expensive for some reason.  Food overall is fairly reasonable, and not too bad.  Veggie calzone wasn't as good as it sounded.

They sell BOOZE, hey, guys! Booooozzzzeee! Hey  --- oh, you already knew? Oh, I didn't... I only ever went for coffee. It's cool, nah, I should've looked. I know, I walked right by. No, I'm fine!

Well, I'm a fan of the coffee - the ice coffees are americanos. Pissah. The pastries are better than the other local options, and though there is a minimal purchase to put it on a card, it's not hard to hit that limit for one person on a coffee break. I've done it, it's easy, really.

Depending on what time you are driving by here, there can be A VERY LOT of parking, or there can be none. It's the mystical (kidding)... it's the catholic school drop off, mostly that makes it tough to get to. So many minivans, so little time!

The people who work here are really nice, and helpful. I'm gonna go buy a ton of wine next time I go, just cause. Now I know...

i don't really miss atlanta that much - but i do miss willy's.  and this place seriously just reminds me every time of what i'm missing out on.
its number 1 problem is the rice. its gross and they jam pack the burrito with it. 
best to stay away if at all possible and get a quesadilla. i mean you can't mess up a tortilla and cheese.
i'll eat here when i'm starving and broke until the chipolte down the street opens up - which still doesn't compare to willy's
The food is great. The deliver quickly and deliver very late. Very Italian. The bread for subs is a little harder than I care for, but the pizza is excellent.

I went here with 3 friends for lunch today.  The place was all decked out for Halloween and it was awesome.  I especially like the hanging Barbies.  I don't know how to adequately describe the look of the place, but it's great.  The chairs are sort of medieval looking, there's a big suit of armor, and the walls are painted purple.  I got an Ipswich IPA (reasonably priced at $4.50) and ate some of my friend's french fries (Damn good with the aioli they came with.  Also $4.50 for a huge plate of them)  Apparently, they have a garage/punk/rock n roll music night on Sundays at 9 pm that I sadly had to miss since I have work in New York on Monday.  I wish I had known about this place when I lived in Boston.

The coffee is overroasted and underbrewed, the beans for the espresso drinks are shockingly pre-ground. There wasn't even a grinder near the espresso machine. The drink wasn't hot enough, as the 'barista' seemed to believe he could tell the milk was the right temperature solely by looking at it, instead of using a thermometer or feeling the side of the steaming pitcher. Finally, the milk to coffee ratio for my latte was way off - about 8 to 1 instead of something more like 3 to 1. Under all that milk, it was impossible to tell anything about the quality of the beans.

The space is nice, but the drinks need a lot of work. (Note how much the other reviews talk about the ambiance, and how little they talk about the coffee.) Even the atmosphere is suffering from a lack of customers, due to a tough location - it's a long walk from either Harvard or Central, each of which have more convenient options with better coffee. 

I hope Atomic improves, but until they do, I won't be going back.

Substandard burritos.  The wrap is too dry and doesn't cling or hold together like an Anna's burrito.  (conveniently located across the street)  Uninspiring selection of flavors and sauces.  Boring mass-market american sodas.  Too expensive.

In an area with so much casual mexican competition, I'm amazed that this place survives.
No. Just no. So dry so tasteless. So expensive for what you get.... Get it together. This place randomly closes during storms without even making it known. Never again

Pros: Excellent flatbread pizza for a decent value.

Cons: That there's only one location, all the way up in Porter Square!

Balance: PROS

BOTTOM LINE: That I've been able to sample (and I've sampled most), this is the best delivery-pizza in Boston.

Food was great, the rise & shine breakfast is a lot of food for a very reasonable price. Service was good. 
We ate breakfast here numerous times on our trip to Boston.

Last night, after an unsuccessful attempt at Xmas Shopping, I decided to see if I could get something quick and tasty at Zing Pizza.

The place was small, I'd say cozy, but the door was open, probably to keep the kitchen cool, so finding a warm seat was difficult (it was a little cool next to the drink refrigerator, which left only a couple seats as options).

Regardless, service was quick, food came out in a couple minutes, and the slices of pizza were very large...  and tasty.  I had the Blue October and the Greek (can't remember the exact name).  The October had a unique, sweet taste, and I enjoyed it.  The Greek was likewise good.

So apart from the cool interior, the food rocked.  I bet this place is great to hang out any other season, but there's a take-out option anyways.

Also, gluten-free options for the gluten allergy folks as a bonus.

I'll be here again!

Really? REALLY???

You are losing customers at the same pace that Charlie Sheen is gaining Twitter followers.

Since it seems that I'm not the only one with a complaint about the service at this place, one would think management would step in and deal with the situation. I guess they just don't care...much like the people working at this location. I will NEVER go back to this place after the crappy service I received.

I'm here to receive a service, that YOU being in the SERVICE industry are to fulfill. Don't freaking scowl at me because I'm squinting to read your horribly fonted menu written on the wall. Don't worry, I'm not staring at you and what clearly must be your Halloween costume from when you dressed up like Trent from Daria. And when I order food, don't freaking stare blankly at me and then question my choices. 

While it's one thing to have people in the customer service industry lack enthusiasm for their jobs, it's completely another to have people who just really do not give a f%ck. You will find both at this location.

Also, this is for you Mr. Moustache douche canoe crapface ...I'm so very sorry you're life doesn't seem to be going in the direction for which you planned. Please don't take that out on everyone you serve. Maybe you should take your annoying wease-like French-based accent back to Montreal and try at your life again...this time with a smile.

Pros: It's open until 2am. Do you know how many places in Harvard Square are open until 2am? (......silence......crickets......) Oh that's right I'm sorry, IHOP.

Cons: Such crappy food. Inexcusably crappy. And if you're a girl, don't go alone late at night, unless you want to find yourself in a conversation with a 55-year-old inebriated crazy Pete.

Balance: CONS

BOTTOM LINE: I don't care how awesome Harvard Square was in the 60s. In the 00s, it's lame -- and the fact that the only late-night grab-a-bite option here is Au Bon Pain exemplifies why.

This place is GREAT! Super high quality food, the people who work there are sweet down-to-earth friendly people. The owners are awesome!

I love their grilled wraps. The wilted greens, garlicky oil, caramelized onions and tender chicken-- my mouth is watering! 
They also have a delicious chicken caesar salad. 

Go for lunch, brunch or dinner. All the menu items are winners. It's gourmet comfort food with an Italian flare.

It's a warm and cozy little (very little) cafe, and it usually has fun hip-chick music playing. Nice neighborhood feel.

ABP in Harvard Square is a landmark of sorts. If you would like to observe the socioeconomic spectrum that populates the Square, this is probably the best place to do it. You have your typical Harvard wasp-y types, working away on their Mac books. A good mix of international visitors and students. The chess players. And the homeless. All in the same space. 

The food, to be perfectly honest, is underwhelming and over-priced. But this is a fine place to stop for a cup of coffee and a conversation. It's also a good meeting point for other ventures as it stands out prominently in the Square and is in view soon after your step out of the T.

I have finally found the perfect Saturday brunch spot in Cambridge! It's Taco Bell, in the Cambridgeside Galleria!

Tucked into the cutest corner of the mall food court, this Taco Bell really reigns supreme.

Let's start with the service. My cashier was helpful and attentive, fast and efficient, a true master of the art of cashiering. Food prep was just as on point. My food arrived quickly, made perfectly, and it was truly delicious. And the woman who called out my number and handed off my food was just as great as the rest of the staff.

Next, ambiance. The food court itself has a bit too much neon for my own taste, but I'm sure others will enjoy the charm. I chose to eat in the outdoor seating, perfect for a beautiful summer day as it was today. The seats had a great picnic table feel, and the fountain was gorgeous. All in all, well worth the trip.

So your friend needs a hot recommendation for the coolest brunch spot in Cambridge? It's Taco Bell!

Warm white wine.  Warm beer.  Paella was probably the worst that I've ever had (out of maybe 50 restaurants worldwide), with spectacularly mushy/gluey rice sparsely punctuated with dry chicken and overcooked mussels.  Five of us ordered a bunch of different things and we never found anything that we liked.  The sliders were overcooked but edible and maybe the best thing that we had.  Waiter was young and mostly clueless, though well-meaning.  A glass of red wine was served at a reasonably cool temperature.
Is this place still open?  I used to eat here a lot when I lived in Cambridge.  My favorite was the sub sandwiches.  Delish.  And it's open late!

There are many great choices for Mediterranean and tapas food in Cambridge, but few have the kind of pedigree that Kika has, and when i say pedigree i of course mean the ownership.
Many restaurants fail in their first year and having a seasoned captain at the helm to help navigate the perilous waters is the key to success.
Kika has this in spades with their already successful owners.
I only got to try a couple of dishes tonight, but i look forward to heading back. Lets start with the chicken empanada's, this is the empanada that all others should be judged by, delicious flaky wrapper with the most exquisite filing i've ever had, juicy chicken, a little corn, onion, beans and sweet sultana's served with a small side of a rich creamy tomato dipping sauce, the perfect compliment to this outstanding dish.
I also had the lobster crab ravioli, these delicious little pillows cooked perfectly and mixed with a sweet tomato and langostino sauce that's sprinkled with a little smoky paprika to give you a little reminder these were made with care.
Service was outstanding, Maria our server was an absolute delight and extremely knowledgeable, always there when we needed her, never imposing.
Outdoor patio is chic and cool, however the hidden gem is inside. An absolutely beautiful interior that really, my words will not do justice, but i'll try but you have to see it, the words that do spring to mind, gorgeous, classy, artistic, welcoming, what can i say, i love it!!
Prices were reasonable and the drinks menu includes some great Spanish wines and beers as well as some drink staples.
Also a pleasure to meet the hands on owner helping with host duties whilst manning the helm of this wonderful restaurant.

Stopped by here with friends after work last week.  Not really sure it was here, but we saw a new sign and decided to give it a go.

The decor is very interesting and not something that I had seen before.  It works for theme and helps make the place seem more intimate than it might otherwise.  The only issue I had was with the sound level, it was a little loud.  Yes, we contributed to this, but I still like to be able to have a conversation.

The food is very good and in line with their other restaurants.  I just wish that they would have been more open about their lineage and advertised it.  I may have been here on purpose rather than accidentally.  I can understand wanting to make it on its own, but the background is important.

I also like the sangria (okay, who doesn't like sangria) and recommend it to anyone who visits,

Lastly, the restaurant is very close to the red line so you do not need to drive which is important with the sangria.

I will be back in the New Year and am glad to have a new place, related to an old place, near where I work.

After today's brunch at City Girl, I'm tempted to sell my soul in exchange for twins for the express purpose of naming them "Sausage Gravy" and "Asiago Biscuits." Sausage would be the girl, yknow, to throw guessers for a loop.

A mainstay on their weekend brunch, the dish smothered two fragrant and dense biscuits with a meaty, herbalicious gravy, then siding the most lovely marriage with a healthy portion of perfectly scrambled eggs. Couple the madness with a surprisingly savory and ragingly refreshing rosemary lemonade for a balanced, unique breakfast.

With room for only 19, there will be wait. There is no space to wait inside. Because you will inevitably be on the list, don't wait outside the front door, opening it every two minutes to check on progress. It's freezing out and you're personally Jack Frosting the 2-top by the entrance. And please PLEASE don't crowd the wee bit of footspace that I may be graced with your ass in my breakfast. It doesn't jive with the eggs.

Tried a bite of the girl's roasted red bliss potatoes and they ruled the school.

I got the Herc as a plate (11.77$ total). It was pretty mediocre. The quantity was pretty filling, and the pita was decent. However, the meat wasn't that tasty (kind of weirdly dry in certain areas, not very flavorful, and a bit too salty), and the rice was totally a mush. The other vegetables weren't anything special either. If I was passing by and wanted something filling I'd stop by, but I wouldn't go just for the taste.

Just your typical Taco Bell in case you were in the Cambridgeside Galleria food court and were craving Mexican food. Their staff is everything but Mexican but they will satisfy your craving, especially if its for crunchy tacos as they are one of the very few places to carry such a product...

It was recently revamped and it looks much nicer and cleaner.

As usual it's very affordable and quick.

On the whole, the burrito experience here needs some work.  It is a little on the pricey side, but this is Cambridge after all, so what's the extra $1, right?

I tried their new pork burrito.  While the flavor was OK (better than the flavors you'll get at Picantes in Central), I think the staff are all relatively new and have never made burritos before.  What you get for your $10 meal ticket is a short, squat burrito which isn't really filling, and tends to fall apart.

I started the review by giving it only 2 stars, but I've decided to up it to three, since the flavor of the burrito seems to be better than the offerings at other Central Square and Harvard Square joints.

So usually I like this location, the staff is usually friendly and engaging (sometimes a little too engaging when the asst. manager flirts with me) and the food is like all Qdoba food, hits the spot when I'm craving Qdoba (not when I'm craving Mexican or Taco Bell).

I like the rewards program and they give me coupons if I don't come in often enough.

Today they started making my naked burrito and forgot to put the rice on the plate so put it on top of the beans, not that it matters much but I had to remind the kid to put the rice on.  Seriously, there are three ingredients in every burrito, how can you forget one?  He then had to re-ask what kind of meat I wanted.  For real?  You can't remember Rice, Pinto, Pork?  You have to ask after each one?

Also, no one, from tortilla girl to burrito line man to cashier was very friendly and avoided eye contact.  At least sketchy 45 year old gay customer wasn't there to hit on me this time, as he has been at least three other times.

Otherwise it was fine and the food was up to Qdoba standards.  I just wish the lunch staff was a little more with it today.

I walk by this place every day and watched it being built. I am in love with the decor, it's so 2010s and very well done.

Stopped by for lunch with folks from work after they opened. This was my first experience with tapas, and I liked it.  I thought it was cool they had pão de queijo (Brazilian cheese balls), as well as an eclectic mix of all sorts of things, so I brought my husband there for lunch. Kika is a bit upscale, lunch ran about $20 per person.
Abigail's has a friendly staff, great drink menu.  The good is good slightly pricey. It is espically convenient if you work in the area, but I would make a trip across the river from Boston for it.

I have yet to receive my food but I had to write my first review ever, and it's based solely upon customer service. I called Pronto Cafe 20 minutes before closing time from my home which is 3.5 mi away (outside of maximum delivery distance) because I'm 6 and a half months pregnant and craving a cheeseburger sub sooo bad! Even though they were extremely busy the guy taking my order empathized with me and allowed me to place my order despite the time and distance.  So needless to say I'm extremely thankful for the customer service alone!

AMAZING food, friendly service, great ambience, and very reasonably priced .
Tatiana as Jeffery  were  welcoming and friendly.  Will definitely be coming back!

Really dug the decor inside, and the many unique touches that they had to make you feel comfortable and as though you were somewhere special.  The drinks were very well done, and our meals were both very tasty.  I had the BBQ sandwich, and it was VERY well seasoned.  The side salad was a nice touch, and my husband enjoyed his steak sandwich (although the bread was a bit stale).  We'll be back--great place!

First of all, there is no sink in the bathroom. Just a big bottle of hand sanitizer. Gross! My friend had a sandwich that was really good, and I had overpriced fettuccine alfredo that was sub-par. Overall it was fine, but I probably won't go back.

I have to say I am very surprised and disappointed by the mediocre reviews on small plates. I wish the reviewers had the experiences I have had. This has become my boyfriend's and I go-to restaurant when we know we will get an amazing meal and amazing service. And we are picky! The tapas-portion sizes are good sized and they recommend 2-3 per person and, along with their amazing home-made scrumptious bread, is plenty (oh plus their scrumptious dessert and lattes). I love the concept - having a taste of many "plates" - and they do offer half or full size portions of their entrees. The combinations are unusual and the dishes, inventive. The petit filet is amazing, the mushrooms are divine, the desserts are to die-for. This past Saturday we sat on the patio, which is just lovely, and we were amazed to get a peek at how small and efficient the kitchen is. Stephanie, one of the owners, is extremely friendly, accommodating and creative and her smart and chic style is reflected in little touches around the restaurant (a flower arrangement here, a wine box repurposed as an herb planter there - in the garden). The chef, Jerome, hails from New Orleans and is extremely talented in his varied creations and offers so many unusual combinations on the menu it makes it very hard to choose! (Good thing it is recommended to get 2-3 per person.) This place can't be beat for atmosphere, price point, portions, service. Especially with this struggling economy, the concept is brilliant - you can go in for a drink and a few tapas for a light meal without breaking the bank. I just love small plates!

Normally I'm a huge fan of Bagelsaurus but my experience today was not ok. I went for a bagel with cream cheese (which will be around $4 something, the bagels normally are great so I don't complain) I'm not sure if the crew was not paying attention but my bagel order was incorrect and there was barely any cream cheese! I recommend going on the weekends, there will be a wait but the staff is better. The weekday staff for some reason isn't as accurate and consistent.

Saloniki is one of my go-to lunch places.  The line gets long sometimes but it always moves quickly, and the food is affordable, healthy, and tasty.  It's location right by MIT's campus is very convenient, and if you're a college student, don't forget to show them your student ID for a free side of fries with your meal.

This place is totally hit or miss. When you need something carb-y and are in a rush (or it's 1 am), then ABP is a glorious haven in the wildnerness that is a bitter cold windy Harvard Square on a weeknight. Wait, except that all the pastries are stale and empty and the only people left in the building are homeless and unbathed.

Then again, sometimes it's early on Sunday AM and you can only get a hot breakfast....oh wait, Dunkin Donuts would be much faster and cheaper.

Eh. Super eh. If you go here I would never touch the door handle or trust that the coffee would be fresh. If you're desparate and in a rush, then I would go if there's no line. Even then, the surliness of your cashier might put you off.

To be fair, the last time that I went I hadn't eaten in 24 hours and my cashier was very nice, the sandwich was delicious, and it took 7 mins (I was in a rush). Hooray!

Best deal on happy hour in Harvard Square, hands down. Go Mon 5-6p or Tues-Thurs 4-6p and food and drinks off the happy hour menu are only $5!! These drinks are phenomenal. The sangria is the best I have had. It has the most pleasant cinnamon flavor and well balanced sweetness. They also have this other drink, Rebujito Blanco and Rojo. WOW. How delicious and dangerous at $5 a pop. I probably would never go here for a full price meal, but a great deal for some after work drinks and snacks. Happy hour is also very low key. You have the restaurant to yourself and get really great service. Food is pretty good too, just small. Again, great value at $5, but too expensive for a real meal.

Delicious food and good service! We came here for brunch on a weekend and ordered eggs benedict and French toast, along with drinks and were not disappointed! The drinks were yummy and potent, and the food portions were very generous. Everything came out fairly quickly, and we never felt neglected by the wait staff.

I like this place a lot-good coffee, good snacks, lovely environment. 

BUT there is a sign on the door saying "no quarters." I get it- I do, but when a paying customer asks for change so she can park and have a coffee and a snack, and is *declined* (rudely), I find it a bit much.
What a unique cozy quirky restaurant. My daughter and I loved this little gem and the food was terrific and we appreciated all the vegetarian options.
Had the mozzarella sticks and pasta.   Both had watery sauce which ruined it.  This place was not serving what I would call Italian.  Maybe the subs and pizza are better, I won't be back to find out.
One of my favorites. I love the food, drinks and atmosphere here. The outside area is a real gem, recommend going here on a nice warm day.

At first glance, especially from the outside, River Gods appears to be nothing more than your typical dive bar.  But lest we forget, don't judge a book by its cover.

Upon entering, the first thing you will notice is the decor.  The place definitely has a Gothic/Medieval vibe to it--between the suit of armor, old organ, and the cathedral pew/throne-like seats.  It makes for a very cool, not to mention unique, atmosphere.

But bars are not built on atmosphere alone.  I can't speak for the menu as a whole, since I've only been twice; but, the Cheese Burger, with or without bacon, is delicious.  Angus beef, American cheese, and caramelized onions paired with a serving of some of the tastiest fries I've ever had.  Top all that off with beer, wine, or a cocktail, and you've got yourself a tasty, tasty meal.  I recommend the Black Velvet, which is Magners Cider topped off with Guinness in a pint glass.  Liquid awesome.

If you're looking to get some good grub in a bar that provides a change of pace from what you're used to, seek out River Gods.  You'll be glad you did.

I have seriously never had a bad meal here! In fact, the only problem I've ever been faced with is what to order because everything is so good!!! 

Sometimes I'll feel daring and order something I've never tried before and then wonder what took me so long... other times, I go for old favorites like the fettuccine in lemon-shallot cream sauce with prosciutto and peas and wish I could eat it forever. The pasta's good, the pizza's good, paninis - you really can't go wrong. Weekend brunches are worth the wait, if only for the verde omelette alone (although I still need to try their French toast).

Remember to check the blackboard for daily specials and save room for the pumpkin bread and vanilla ice cream sandwich dessert, holy moly!

The staff is awesome, laid-back but efficient, and I love this space - it may seem cramped but they do a lot with it! The coziness is part of the charm. Don't ever change, City Girl! Hearts forever and ever, always.

So, unless you were born with a working knowledge of supply chain management out of the womb or paid large sums of money to a small business consultant that specializes in food/bakery businesses then chances are opening weekend is going to have some kinks.  

When my dad and I donated to this kick starter it was to promote delicious food with an adorable name that needs to be around because there is so much garbage being sold as food that a woman who has a metal rod for one of her bones and then still stays the course of baking amazing bagels is pretty damn impressive.  I really don't care that I waited in line or they took time to spread some cream cheese because in about a month it'll all be second nature and they'll get the supply and demand stuff down.  All I care about is that the bagel is as flipping good as it was when I first had it, and you know what?  It is.

When I started dating my super Polish now-husband his grandma swore by the Jewish Polish bakeries in NYC for the best bagels.  I tried them and the angels sang and I knew what good bagels were supposed to taste like.  When I lived in Europe and made my way to Kraków I was determined to visit a bagel shop in the Old Jewish Quarter because this area is supposedly home to the best bagels in the world.  When I tried them the angels sang and the clouds parted and I knew all other bagels afterward would be measured against these puffy clouds of deliciousness.  

Bagelsaurus bakes bagels that hold their own against the guys from across the pond yet have set themselves apart with a crunch and chewiness and hint of yeastiness that will please your bagel hankering belly.  If patience isn't your virtue come back a long-line-less day, but, do come back cause this shop has got the goods.

I love Saloniki. The food is fresh and flavorful every time. I'm especially fond of the salads, and as a vegetarian it is hard to find a good salad in a fast, casual restaurant. I ask for the lemon preserve sauce, which is just a tad spicy and good on everything. I had the food delivered to me this time, and unfortunately they forgot to include the pita bread. The rest of my order was correct and delicious.

The good part about Qdoba is that they give you tons of food (mostly rice). The bad part is that you feel ill afterwards. I would rather bring a can of spaghetti o's and have them cold than eat at Qdoba. I can't quite decide I why I dislike Qdoba so much but everything tastes processed. Stick with Picante or Felipe's.
Love this place. But they should put a tip jar by the cash register. The employees deserve it!

Oh Cindy's. You are not 3-star material but you do stay open rather late for a restaurant in Cambridge, and I guess that in itself is redemption.

I don't like the pasta here (and I really don't like the wine sauce), but I used to get the spinach calzone. The boyfriend loves the intensely garlicky garlic bread (read: minced garlic and butter on bread). Steak tips are bearable, but ask them to cook it a little rarer than usual. Yeah, I'm glad I've moved on.

My boyfriend and I went for dinner yesterday as part of our Valentine's Day celebration since we were both really busy on Valentine's Day. The decor is really interesting and fun to look at. 
We ordered a few different types of skewers as appetizers and they were all absolutely delicious. Both dipping sauces were great, too. The selection of drinks is really great- not many places have Black Bush Whiskey, but they do at River Gods, along with a nice selection of cocktails and beers. We each got a drink before dinner and they were delicious and generous in size. When my boyfriend finished his drink the waitress stopped by to ask if he would like another, and this kind of attentive but not pushy service lasted throughout the night, despite the restaurant and bar filling up while we ate. 
Our dinner was also excellent- I had the Seafood Cake Sandwich and mashed potatoes and my boyfriend had the barbecue chicken and fries. We shared and each liked everything. After dinner we tried the Hot Whiskey and it's phenomenal. We both love whiskey and enjoy trying different drinks with it, and this was really something special. Not something I'd always be in the mood for, but smooth and warm and soothing. 
I will definitely be going back for dinner, and I'm curious to see what it's like after-hours, too. Couldn't have asked for a better meal.

This place looks really trendy and hip from the outside but on the inside its food is far from perfect. I went here with a few of my co workers for lunch one day and I was thoroughly disappointing. The waiter forgot some of the drinks and had to be reminded to bring the, He also forgot about us once the food was set down...okay so you aren't always going to get good service-I could accept the rotten service if the food was decent but it wasn't! The veggie burger was just OK nothing to rave about and I was left wishing I had gone somewhere else. Maybe I should have gotten something better *sigh*....It gets stars for the ambiance but that's about all.

A friend and I had weeknight drinks and tapas here recently.  I found it to be a pretty standard tapas place.  The ambiance was nice - elegant but nothing stood out as unique.  We had a selection of vegetarian tapas which were a little pricy but were good enough taste-wise... Artichokes, eggplant, and a usual favorite of mine, patatas bravas.  I had a sparkling cava sangria which was refreshing.  I'd go back if they were running some kind of promotion or a friend really wanted to go.

You know that feeling when you've just moved in to a neighborhood and you're itching to find "your spots".  Ben and I felt that way back in June, and we were absolutely thrilled after our first time at City Girl.

We knew it was going to be the place which we could count on for a good sandwich to go on an afternoon in the summer or the place where we could grab the cozy table in the back and talk endlessly if we wanted to dine there.

And its been just that without fail.  

p.s. The fontina sandwich is fantastic, and the sausage on baguette is perfection.

Not bad for one of those Taco bell, not good enough for me to come back. I hate it when you leave and drive away just to open the bag with food to find they made a mistake with the order. Not the first time, hopefully the last time.

Discovered this place on the Off The Beaten Path Food Tours Central Square Food and Mural Arts Tour. We got an incredible green bean dish that's only available on the tour, but I went back after to try other stuff on their extensive menu. It's a DREAM!!!!!! My favorites were the seared avocado and build your own bowl. I'm not vegan, but I do love fresh veggies and feeling better after eating not worse. I'm so pleasantly surprised by this place -- I'll be coming back every week. Thanks for existing, WHP!

I love this place!  This is definitely my favorite Sushi place in the Boston area so far.  On top of the food being delicious the space is very sweet and elegant with the painting on the walls and the soft lighting.  Plus the I always find the service friendly and super efficient.  If you are looking for an amazing roll try the Butterfly Maki.

I usually get the Despena pita wrap, which is so good! I generally like coming to Saloniki, however there have been a couple of occasions when I've ordered take out, and they have gotten my order wrong. Hopefully they work out a better system, because I do like their food!
I love the Zing Gluten Free pizzas! Great GF processes, and they even let you know when there may be a GF issue with an ingredient. No where else can you find such a tasty, moist gluten free pizza.
I've introduced some of my co-workers to this place, and they absolutely love it, thus confirming that cindys is the best!!!!!  I might have to order from them right now.

We came here when it first opened last January for a friend's birthday.  They were very nice about accommodating our large group, though we felt a little noisy in the cute, little intimate space.  It feels like someone's home, and it's very simple and cozy inside.  

Parking around Harvard Square is always about luck, though there is a parking lot across the street (not sure on pricing).  Service wasn't bad, and I really appreciated that they didn't charge us a plating fee or anything for bringing out own birthday cake.

There are a couple of good by the glass wine selections at decent prices.  Everything on the menu is supposed to be as local and sustainable as possible.

Dishes weren't tapas, per se, but they were smaller portions.  Many combinations sound interesting, but in the end, the dishes don't always pan out.  The warm plate is a great selection to start out sharing with friends, and it's a nice balance of different starters, with braised leeks, polenta, dried fruit, etc.  The seared scallops weren't bad, but the black sticky rice didn't do it for me.  The filet is the perfect size for me in the half portion, as I don't eat much red meat, and even at $22 for the full size, the price isn't too bad.  Other dishes weren't as memorable.  Most things are around the $10 range.

I'm a fan of small dishes, where you can try a little of everything.  It's probably best to go with 3-5 people, rather than a large group.  Most of the guys in our group were complaining about the portions, but that's the whole concept of the restaurant, right?

This cafe has a terrific brunch if you are willing to wait. I showed up around 10:45 on a Saturday with a group of four and was seated within about 40 minutes. I had the egg and cheese sandwich on a pressed baguette and my friend had the eggs with sausage gravy over cheddar biscuits. The eggs were deliciously done--light and fluffy. The roasted potatoes were crisp and delicious. The only thing I wished were different were a) the wait, and b)  that the cheddar biscuits had been offered with a vegetarian gravy. I'll admit though that I did sneak a dry-ish bite from my friend's dish and boy it was good!

Last evening just b4 closing, I ordered for delivery the Combo Pasta dinner.
It WAS NOT good - it was SUPERB!!! 
I've tried their steak/pepper/cheese and onions(add on) AND that too was very tasty! 

Try them, you may LIKE it!!!

I just returned here with friends last Saturday for what would be a great evening. The outdoor patio is open and we were lucky to get the last table available.

The food as always was great, the service was really friendly, and the experience was a lot of fun. The tables are spread out enough for privacy but close enough to provide a fun and lively atmosphere.

Small plates is a great summer lunch-time and dinner retreat with friends and family...if you can get a table. Be sure to call ahead and make reservations. The word is out.
Love this place. Way cheaper than a meal plan (for most people it's above $13 per meal compared to $9), and fairly healthy. Fries are free for students, so it's really filling too.

I wish I discovered this place sooner. The wait is a bit long, but the subs are pretty good.

I had the MIT special and really enjoyed it. I wouldnt recommend this place if you are in a rush, but it def. has some good subs and good prices.

I work around the corner and ran here one day to pick up lunch for a co- worker so I just doubled the order for a chicken Caesar wrap.  I can't stand eating salads, but this wrap maybe the best thing since slice bread.  I have never ordered anything else, but after talking to the cook the steak tips are just as good.  The store is very clean and the prices are right on target.  Great local market with a little of everything and a great breakfast too.  Everyone who works here is very friendly and has a smile.  

Chicken wrap with a can of soda like $8.75and maybe the most under-priced roll up east of the Mississippi river or west of the Charles River.

This place is awesome! The food is really fresh and the menu has something for everyone (meat eaters, vegetarians, not so much vegans, but there are a couple of things). I loved their Greek Salad and the grilled Salmon was cooked perfectly. They have a good wine selection and they are a great place to grab coffee/espresso and breakfast any day of the week, especially on the weekend!  They have a great little deli counter where you can get your cold cuts and cheeses, so you don't have to deal with Whole Foods or Shaws when you really only need a couple of things. 

Places like Sarah's aren't around much these days. A charming market, clean and well stocked convenience store (albeit prices are a little high for grocery/personal items), and the cafe is great for take out or to stay.  A super neighborhood place that serves the "village" needs perfectly!

to be fair, this is only a review on the cafe part and only for breakfast.  perhaps i am completely spoiled by all the offerings that somerville has, but i really expected more out of a place serving sit down breakfast in huron village.

the menu is fairly basic, nothing outrageously interesting or creative - but that's okay - it's just breakfast.  a couple of us had blueberry pancakes.   they were made well enough  but here was the surprising thing:  when we ordered the pancakes, we asked if they had real maple syrup.  the woman behind the counter said, "i don't know -  it comes out of a bottle like you get a the grocery store and I am sure it says maple syrup on it." and then she pointed me to the squeeze bottle that has sticky brown goo all over the top that screams flavored maple syrup. we tried a little on our plates and yep, sure enough, thick, high fructose corn syrup with maple flavoring added.  so not a good start.

then the coffee which was fairly bland.  i don't have high expectations for breakfast place coffee so that didn't bother me.  but what did bother me is i ordered a large cup of coffee and the person behind the counter gave me a cup that was literally two thirds full.  i handed the cup back to her and said very politely that i did not need that much room for cream and could she please fill up the up a little further.  she seemed a bit miffed but filled up the cup a little further, giving me still a good 1 1/2" at the top for cream.  i didn't want to bother any more at that point.  shorting your customers on coffee can't be the best way to make money on your customers! 

and for ambiance, yeah, call it low-key.  definitely not cozy or quaint.  maybe it's better for lunch or dinner but for breakfast, there are lot's of better options.

It upsets me to see all the negative comments here. By far the rudest people here are the customers. Take the trouble to say please and thank you and to ask about the coffee and the staff are very pleasant. 

Anyhow, HiRise provides by far the tastiest coffee in Harvard Square. The staff take their coffee very seriously indeed - I would call them coffee geeks, really. And they know that quality is more important than quantity. When did you last find a 16oz latte in Italy? I'm afraid to say I would sooner pay 3.25 for their large cappuccino than I would accept a free drink of the same name from Starbucks or Pete's. It's a real work of art.

The bakery products are, indeed expensive. But they're also very good. Again, size isn't everything.

This place is such a refreshing antidote to the faceless drudgery that is most of the rest of Harvard Square.

Oh, Zing! You changed my conception of pizza, maybe forever. If only I'd known that you wouldn't be around upon my post-summer return, I would have visited you every day (okay, every week). 

What an empty autumn this was without your Blue October butternut squash, caramelized onion goodness. And what a sad January without your Dracula's Dilemma to keep me warm (wherever will I find your tangy pomegranate molasses vinaigrette again? So delightful). 

I'm not sure I can leave Cambridge without bidding you a proper farewell, so can you please re-open before June? 

(One of) Your biggest fan(s).
The soup is mediocre and over-priced (actually, all their food is), but their chai tea lattes hit the spot.

I'm pretty certain this is what the deal is with Cinderella's; they have a pickle jar full of lukewarm fish juice and just before they wrap your order they take a small paint brush, dip it in the jar and baste the food in said disgusting fluid.

If this is not what is going on in the kitchen then I demand to know why everything, everything, tastes like fish.

And watch yourself behind the building, the delivery drivers are NUTS and it is full blown GTA out there...if you're walking I suggest you do Columbus proud and limber up first

Once in awhile I enjoy Taco Bell. I come here because it is the only location near me. I usually order the fresco soft chicken taco or the regular fresco taco. They are really good. I have tried the Doritos Taco and it is really good. That is what I usually order for food and it has always been tasty and the order has been correct every time I have come here. The one thing that I really like is the lady at the register who is very friendly and kind. She is one of the nicest customer service people that I have encountered. She is really sweet.

This is for all Au Bon Pains in general...definitely an okay chain if you must eat at one.  Sandwiches are always decent, if a bit bland...no surprises.  I've never been crazy about the coffee, but it's drinkable.  They do always seem to have really great soup, and alright bagels.  I can think of far better options in HSq, but if you're at the airport or something, and don't have much to choose from, the yellow Au Bon Pain sign can be a welcome sight.

I go here because it is conveniently located next to the T and Harvard. They make a good latte and they have a variety of baked goods, salads, sandwiches and soups. It can get crowded especially in the winter time when everyone is trying got keep warm and it can be hard to find a table.

One thing that bothers me is that in the evenings the cashiers often wander off and the registers are left unattended. I then have to go look for help just so that I can pay for my coffee and leave. It can be annoying if you're in a rush.

Really good!  A little gem in a much-needed area.  Enjoyed the service, the sushi was fresh, pink, and yummy.  They gave us a litle free mochi at the end of the meal.  It was a bit pricey, but I find that good sushi ALWAYS is pricey.  (Nothing is worse than bad sushi.)  This place was totally worth it and we will be back.

I guess as a drive through emergency stop it works well but otherwise no. The staff can't seem to get anything right for anyone ordering. The seating was crowded and the bathroom was extremely small. 

They completely screwed up our order and seemed to have done the same with everyone else's. 

Thankfully this is a chain and there are many many other locations.

I came here again, my first time with my best friend last summer, but this time, taking my Chinese grandparents so that they could try something akin to dim sum in a different light. Dishes seem to be fairly hit or miss. The paella seems to lack character compared to others I've seen, but perhaps it is best to compare it to Dali. It only had shrimp, chicken, and escargot. The rice itself was alright, but there is a lack of further imparted flavors. The skate wing was good for the most part, the fish very tender and flavorful being moist instead of flaking, working well with the bed of spinach it is placed upon, but the chickpeas seem a bit underdone. The jerk chicken is very good for those who enjoy the dish with a lighter spice kick than you would find at a more traditional preparation, placed on top of a salsa of mango, peppers, and red onion, albeit personally, I like a bit more umph. The crabcakes were alright in terms of the level of filling, but they were very, very dry. They're served with a bit of sauce and a lot of diced cucumber, but perhaps instead of the cucumber, a tzatziki could work better. The warm platter has been good both times I went, loaded with a variety of flavors. I'm just surprised my grandmother was downing the sour kumquats. The pisadiere was very good, being soft in its flakiness, pliant to being cut, and loaded with chevre, spinach, and onions. The quail did not seem... out of the ordinary. It was a bit dry compared to ones I've had at other places, the quinoa only enhancing the dryness. The duck breast with apples? pears? (don't know which, my sister grabbed it all) and cranberry stuffing was alright, at least the breast, being juicy and cooked medium-well. The stuffing was... a bit dry. That seems to be a theme... The Japanese eggplant was bordered with a tomato sauce with slivered almonds. The Japanese eggplant was alright, but the uncooked slivered almonds seemed to detract from the dish, the flavor a contrast but far too light and the texture a bit off. The nuts could use some roasting. The beef satay was the only dish that seemed to have a measure of kick, though I am not too sure about the pairing of the hot skewers with lukewarm diced apples. The sauce had flavor, though the beef was very 'well done'. The beef itself seemed to have... a light flavor on its own, relying on the dipping sauce heavily to impart.

The biggest issue I have with the dishes in general is that the food would be so much better if it was served hot instead of somewhat lukewarm. Some may consider a salamander to be cheating, but in the case of needing to mass produce a large array of dishes, it may be almost a necessity for a place like this.

Overall, the food isn't bad, but seems to lack amazing for me. The flavors seem too mild except for the kumquats and apricots. It seems like the food is trying to avoid offending a base palette. The service was very good, however, constantly filling glasses, checking in without overdoing it. The restaurant can be a little bit... tight for those of a larger size. None in my party save myself, but it was a bit tight, even for my smaller frame parents. The massive looming mirror that was half a foot thick behind my parents didn't help comfort level, the base of the mirror butting up against their heads.

Dishes to try: Pisadiere, Skate Wing

What separates this Irish pub from every other Irish pub in Boston (and we have a few) is what they mean by "Irish."

They don't mean quaint, back-country Irish paraphenalia.  They mean ancient, pagan, screw-you-St-Patrick Irish.  Dark and mysterious.  Older than old school.

Decent pub grub, and they pour a good pint of Guinness.  Definitely worth the trip if you're looking for something different.

Pro tip: Monday nights are Weekly Wax - two dueling DJs take turns in the booth upstairs and mix their own favorites.  Always a good time to drop in.

I love the decor and design here. The monotone prints of people on the walls are fun and eclectic. However, I sat outside near the outdoor bar with corn hole on a bright summer day. 

I had a basil blackberry margarita with the mussels; both were yummy and refreshing. My two friends had the pizza and salmon, they both loved them. Our waitress was super friendly and let me order drink and food before my friends arrived. 

I will love coming here again soon!

Service was slow and inattentive. That is the first thing that jumps out to me when I think about my lunch here yesterday. Second thing that jumps out is how dry the brisket sandwich was. The fries with Gouda and bacon had some sort of gravy under the then that was not listed in the description; I think they were trying to make poutine. The blue cheese on the pork sandwich overpowered what could have been a great sandwich. (It had onion rings on it, how can you go wrong with that?)Those things being said Abigail's is promising. I'll check back on it once it matures a bit.
Nice little place, went for lunch yesterday after walking around Fresh Pond, had Sarah's Soup (lentil curry soup), really good and hit the spot.  Will try them on a Sunday morning for breakfast soon.

We've tried probably all the Cambridge/Somerville/and most Boston tapas places, and Kika stands out. We've also been to Madrid, Andalucia, Barcelona, and Central Spain (Caceres). Kika is on par with (and exceeds) the quality we experienced in our travels.  Winners: Berenjena Rellena, Pechuga al Ancho, Empanada de pollo, Albondigas de Cordero, Solomillo con Chimmichurri. Great tapas, excellent service, superb patio atmosphere, moderate prices -- what's not to love!!

Interesting if imperfect boutique neighborhood bar. Nice atmosphere (reminded me a lot of the bars I used to hit occasionally in San Francisco - the Latin American, the Make Out Room). A couple issues: food is spotty - I've had better than good things (the pizzas, the fries), and worse than good (the pasta). The owner is super personable, but have to say the staff could be friendlier. Have been there many times and nary a smile from anyone there, no overt good-feeling neighborhood props or recognition. A shame, because that's what creates longer term loyal customers.
