I agree that it is a little disorganized when it comes to ordering (especially on a busy weekend day), but I trust that this will be fixed in no time. Also, once you've been a couple of times, I don't really think its an issue at all anymore. 

Glad to see lots of families venturing inside - makes it feel like a real neighborhood joint. Hope this place sticks around a while.

Excellent service. The staffs are very knowledgable of every ingredient to enable me select  my order quickly. 
Sandwiches and drinks are tasty

Before coming here I was warned about the poor service so I was prepared ahead of time, but it was a convenient location for breakfast food. It's a typical seat yourself diner with a pretty standard breakfast menu. No complaints about the food, I got scrambled eggs with hash browns, toast and bacon. The service was the bad part because it took a while for someone to take our order and for the food to come. Then we asked to order off the lunch menu for a to go order and were informed lunch started at 12. Then at 12, we went to place an order and they informed us they couldn't start lunch until all the breakfast orders were complete. They were nice about it, but it just seemed like a bad system. Overall, does the trick for a simple breakfast, but service is not great.

Speedy service and good food. Soup dumplings are great and all their Taiwanese and Szechuan dishes are serviceable. I'm not a big fan of their "salt and pepper" coating which is more of a batter than most places. They're always packed but they move people in and out pretty fast so if there's a wait it's usually less than 15 min.

Yummy, affordable and great value subs. The small subs average over 9 inches and the lunch bag (chips and soda) sets you back only $9 which is one of the cheapest lunch in Tech Square. The service moves quickly even during the peak lunch hours.

I thoroughly enjoyed my time at Ryles Sunday Jazz Brunch. I came with the intention of watching/hearing Yoko Miwa Trio who are an amazing bunch of musicians. 

The food was good, and well-priced (portions were generous) and I'd definitely come back again when Yoko Miwa Trio is performing. The only downside was that there was only one waitress for the entire restaurant which got pretty full fast. Very slow to get coffee, water, and the check.

I love the tuna melt here. It makes my mouth water just thinking about it. Hot tuna with melted cheddar cheese slathered on top, in the middle of their fabulous flatbread. Yum-O. My friends and I shared the smores platter once but it was definitely not worth the money. Although the mini fuel pot was cute and nice to play with, we could've bought the graham crackers, marshmallows, and Hershey's chocolate from the market for a fraction of the price (~$8 feeds two). I'll usually stop by this Cosi if I'm hungry and near Kendall station.

It is across from my work and I had thought of trying it for a while and finally did when I was low on time.  The iced coffee I had tasted pretty awful and the price was more than Starbucks for less coffee and I was annoyed that I couldn't pour my own milk or cream to taste.  I may try it again at some point, but with Starbucks and Au Bon Pain close also, I won't ever be making this a regular coffee stop.

Yeah Olivia... I had Werner as my waiter too 10/19/09 and I felt pretty much the same way: snotty attitude and slow service.  I kind of felt unwelcome to be honest.  I kept thinking... aren't these hard times for the restaurant industry?  I mean you would think that in this Titanic of an economy the service would at least somewhat resemble the "Be Our Guest" scene in Disney's Beauty in the Beast.  It was a little more like walking into a stranger's house and asking for a drink and having them look at you like "who the hell are you?"  My favorite was when our party asked for a drink menu so we could pick our second round, never got it, but Werner eventually did come around to ask if we had decided... because we memorized the menu the first time I guess.  Eventually I just got up a got a menu from the front myself.  The jazz was eh.  Not a lot of soul in it.  The drinks were pretty good though and the décor is also cool, especially the piano keys above the bar.  I just wish I had felt like I wasn't imposing.

Sunnny's diner tiny and quaint, but they also have free wireless. The menu is simple.  Soup, salad and sandwich for lunch.  Pancakes, meat and omelets for breakfast.  It's just not exciting.  

Service: Good
Portions: Normal
Taste: Normal
Price: Normal
Overall: zzzzzz ... zzzzzz
what? you guys are closing?  Is it 2pm already?

I'd only go to Sunny's if it's convenient for you.

My granddaughter wanted a carrot cake and she loves unicorns and rainbows.  Although she doesn't usually make carrot cakes, Terri agreed to make one and it was delicious. The design was absolutely beautiful and my granddaughter was beyond excited.  I wish I could give Terri 10 stars.

Stopped in here for lunch and I'm so glad we did.  The first thing that struck me was the great way they were using mobile technology for everything. Iphones for ordering, paying and viewing at every cooking station.  Then there was the food.  Most people here said to try the chickpea fritters (and many people there were ordering just that) but we decided to be rebels and try the BBQ Seitan and Egg & Eggplant with a side of fries ... SO DELICIOUS!  It was great to see them making the food and then it was awesome to eat (of course).

Affordable breakfast in a place that looks like it's right out of a 1980's Miami Vice episode? Yes please!

We walked into the modest looking Sunny's dinner on a chilly Monday morning only to be welcomed by one of the nicest guys ever. We could have sat just about anywhere and picked a table. The counter wasn't deep enough to accomodate our knees.

We promptly glanced at the menu and ordered away: I had chocolate chip pancakes and a Nantucket Nectar Half & Half (Arnold Palmer). My dining companion had coffee, a diet coke and a plate of eggs, meat and homefries.

Everything came out pretty quickly and tasted great. There was nothing outstanding about the food but at under $7 there's no way in hell I could complain. Plus the staff was extremely friendly. They have a very simple menu but it's executed well but don't expect any frills here.

This is a simple, very casual dinner that basically pretty much only does breakfast. There's pancakes, egg plates and little else but that's just fine by me. 

One note however is that the coffee is apparently pretty horrendous according to the girlfriend but that's about the only negative thing we could find about it. My chocolate chip pancakes were great and they didn't skimp on the chocolate which made them even more awesome.

This is the kind of place that you'd often ignore but honestly, now that I've been once, I'll be returning. Hopefully not too long from now.

Clover is my favorite place to stop by for a quick meal. Their options are always creative and the food is super fresh. The employees are super friendly and are always more than willing to answer questions and provide recommendations. Everything--from the classics like the chickpea platter to the impossible meatball sandwich and the zucchini sandwich--is absolutely delicious. I also love how no two meals are the same since the sides are constantly changing. My husband really likes their soda selections, but I find them to be too sweet. I especially like how you can see everything being made at this location!
Very good Chinese food for a very good price. I had the Shandon dumplings along with a bowl of beef noodle soup. Both were excellent and highly recommended.

Called right when they opened at 11a and asked if they had gamjatang today. The lady on the phone said yes, so we pre-ordered it and told them I would be there in 20 minutes. They confirmed it.

Twenty minutes later, we walked in anticipating a steaming hot bowl of stew for lunch, and they informed us they were now out of gamjatang. Not cool.

We ended up going to Sura BBQ instead.

The homefries are barely cooked, the waitstaff is not friendly and the food DOES have a weird flavor. If it's the weekend, it's worth paying a little more and hitting up the Irish breakfast at Spirit, just a few doors down. They don't open til later though, but at least you can get a bloody mary. 

I wish someone would open up a better diner in Porter. We've never had a good one.

Three days in a row "my" baristas have delivered smiles and good, strong, iced, fair trade organic coffee (thanks, ladies)!  The pastries aren't bad either.  Thumbs up to the chocolate chip walnut cookie and thumbs waaaay up to the pain au chocolat.

I was a bit apprehensive about my potential level of enjoyment sitting in this tiny corner of a tinier bookstore.  No need for fear at all.  There seems to always be a seat, even though there's always a line, and a thumbed-through newspaper awaits your perusal.

As to the fresh rolls...I have yet to try them, but I've been there around the time they arrive, and I'm convinced they must have crack in them.  The way people swoop in for them out of nowhere like owls in the night is flabberghasting.  Are they so good that you have to come in at 10:30am to get 10 of them?  Apparently!

Oh, and it's yet another cash only spot.  :-(

Have you looked at the nutrition facts on their Web site? Please do -- even if you aren't a health nut and you don't diet -- it will still shock you. I thought I was eating healthy -- some tasty soup or a grilled chicken sandwich. WRONG. 

Plus, I used to go religiously every Monday for the Winter Garden Veggie soup, and then they discountinued it. I went three Mondays in a row and they said "Oh, we're out." And then they finally told me they had discountinued it (without removing it from the menu). 

On the plus side: They have a much better set-up than Au Bon Pain -- but try not to go between 12:00 and 12:30, it gets crazy.

I have removed my previous review and given an accurate rating based upon my sincere appreciation for Paddy's. I don't know what I was thinking by reviewing it in the first place. 

If you are lucky, you can get one of Dana's Hot Dogs for lunch. He cooks them in a nice old-school rotating hot dog cooker thing and they are pretty tasty. 2 bucks.

Bumped to 5 stars after a particularly yummy hot dog and a fine time hanging out with some of the very nicest people in Cambridge on a hot summer day. OK, morning. I have nothing but good things to say about Paddy's. The best of the best.

Filipe's gets 5 stars because it is delicious. 

Yes, It is usually very crowded around any meal-time, and yes, sometimes you cannot enjoy the roof because it's too full-- but there's a reason people flock to this place so much.

I have tried almost everything, but best of all is getting your favorite burrito made into a chimichanga. Also, the frozen margaritas are wonderful and they pack a punch! 

The staff is always nice, and I've never had anything I wasn't crazy about. Everything is scrumptious!

For those days when you don't want to stand in line at Rosebud, walk half a mile down the street to Andy's Diner.

The home fries were some of the best I've had in the city, the eggs were buttery and delicious, the coffee was refilled at a good pace, and there's enough seating that you shouldn't have to wait too long.  In short, go here for a tasty, no-frills breakfast - the hours may be short, but the flavors are strong!

How about spending some time on the menu and actual food rather than making the restaurant look cool?  I could have made this food during a camping trip, without use of a fire.  I got granola for $5, and my boyfriend ordered the mysterious "breakfast sandwich" - no description on the menu. I got the granola in a plastic cup, even though we were seated in the restaurant. The breakfast sandwich ended up being a stuffed pita bread - anemic cheese, tomato and soft-boiled egg. While we were eating my boyfriend said, "you want to go to Bom (next door cafe) after this and get something to eat?"
A great value.  Pretty much everyone I know goes here for lunch more than any other place in the Square.

Ok Cambridge, so this is what a college bar is supposed to be like. $3.50 tall boys, crowded, awkwardly small bathroom. Pretty fun place. I certainly had a good time and had been looking for an actual college bar in Cambridge.

Maybe I'm spoiled, maybe not. Maybe I'm looking a gift horse in the mouth- this is, after all, a moderately decently interesting chinese restaurant in a high-volume area that suffers from mediocre establishments up and down the street. But, it's called Dumpling House, so I expected damn good dumplings. As I said, perhaps I'm spoiled, because the benchmark I have of soup dumplings is from Joe's Shanghai in NY's Chinatown, and, IMHO, the dumplings here at Dumpling House are a quantum leap below those at Joe's. The dough here is too thick, never absorbs the flavors, the soup is strangely spiced (and much too salty in the opinion of my dining companion), and the filling is sparse and uninviting. So, don't go for the eponymous. That said, the other dishes we had were more than adequate, and there might be some real gems on this menu. Service was hurried and noncommittal, which is not unusual in places where english is a decidedly second (or third) language. Prices seem reasonable, and they aren't afraid to offer spicy. Nothing to drink other than tea at the moment. My other complaint- way too much plastic. Plastic chopsticks. Plastic soup spoons. The overall impression is an atmosphere of unconcern with your satisfaction.

This place is small and crowded, but a great breakfast place with regular breakfast items.  I used to be in love with this place until i went to Brookline Lunch (cheaper+more food) but it's still really cheap, delicious, and quick.

This place has a lot going for it - really delicious, amazing cold brew equal exchange coffee, for one :) They also make drinks with soy, almond or rice milk. It's in a bookstore, there are a lot of cool people around and other neat things in this plaza, including a great toy store and one of the outdoor pianos anyone can play that are now around Boston. 

The only downsides are limited seating and potential crowding, and a pretty slow moving line.

If you're looking to break away from your coffee habit, 1369 is a surprisingly good resource, offering a delicious and refreshing variety of Iced Teas brewed daily.  On the sweltering day it was today, I could not have imagined drinking a coffee (iced or otherwise) and a sign outside of 1369 beckoned to me with their offer of tea. They got me hook, line and sinker. And for less than $3, I left with a giant fresh Iced Peppermint Tea and a whole new outlook on this miserably hot day.

IMHO, I agree with Ivy K and Jeffery L.  Don't understand the other h8ters.  One Ramen is a solid new ramen and sushi place with a tasty selection of appetizers, sushi, and ramen.  3 maki roll dinner is a good value as are the traditional ramen choices - miso, shoyu, shio, and tonkotsu.  No $15 bowls of ramen for this family.  The tonkotsu ramen is definitely the best choice of the 3 we've tried so far.  Some of the Maki rolls could be a bit bigger for the prices, but all are tasty.  Definitely worth a try.

Great first experience here! Moksa beat my expectations. When I walked in, the atmosphere declared that this would be upscale eating: everyone was dressed well, many suit jackets, the mood lighting was warm, sophisticated, and classy. I naturally expected pricy plates and small portions. However, I was pleasantly surprised by how far my money went (5 Crab Rangoons for only $4!); many of my friends even had leftovers. I'd say the front bar area is great for a secretly budget-conscious date.

As others have noted, some reviewers were quite offended that the ramen was not fatty enough for them. I don't really see that as a negative, I wanted to review in hopes that they stick around because I would like to re-visit. I was able to eat the whole bowl and drink the broth without feeling like I wanted to throw up like at other ramen places in the area. They pride themselves on less fatty and less salty ramen broth especially compared to the others in the area. So if that's what you're looking for (which their target clientele may then give them a try. Just because it is different, does not mean it is bad. I had the tonkatsu ramen which they infused flavor with fried shallots which I love. I can't speak for the sushi as I did not have any yet but looking to coming back!

Delicious food and inexpensive with a beautiful rooftop bar!

Pros:
I enjoyed the tequila slushy that is the margarita and it was pretty strong so a good steal at $8 for Boston's usually overpriced drinks.

Delicious food and easy to make healthy if you so wish.

Beautiful rooftop bar with cool people frequenting.

Cons:
Gaining seating at said rooftop bar is a free for all. I had to revert to animalistic tendencies to hunt and gather tables and chairs for a group of 6. We asked a waitress for help, who said she would but then ignored us and sat with her friend at their table for over half an hour.

I ordered the dip trio and they charged me for it before telling me they were out of queso and asked if double guac was okay. I get running out of food, but you should let someone know that before charging!

My friends I were with were all roadtripping through US from New Zealand, and as such had their NZ identification and a photocopy of their passport but not their passports. They haven't had a problem in 20 cities, but Felipe's demanded a passport. They are all clearly in their mid-twenties and I know this is a tricky issue, but it stunk and the way the bartender enforced the policy was not understanding at all (he was kind of a d*ck.) I think this is a policy that should be revisited or clearly posted on the website, it sucks to be expected to walk around with your passport.

Went on a Wednesday night and we took the last empty seats.  The atmosphere of the restaurant could use a little work in terms of table lay out, lighting and to a milder degree decor.  Otherwise, I would give Kimchi Kitchen 5 stars because the food was exceptional!

I had the Kimchi Jigae and a Korean Popcorn chicken special, buddy got the bulgogi.

I tried some of the bulgogi and liked that it was not over sauced like a lot of other Korean bbq.

The popcorn chicken was tasty and no complaints there.

The Kimchi Jigae however.. my god they call it Kimchi Kitchen for a reason.  I haven't had Kimchi Jigae this good since I left Los Angeles!  The broth had a lot of depth instead of just a generic spicy flavor.  The kimchi in the soup was tender and there was a lot of it!  

The Korean friend that I came with actually enjoyed it and he usually does not like Korean food.  In his words: "Thanks for dragging me out here"

Will return and try the other dishes!
Great little dive bar! Beers are cheap and drinks are stiff. Bartenders are attentive.

The people aren't terribly friendly, there is hardly ever a place to park, the cleanliness is questionable, there is never a seat, but the COFFEE is damn good.  Now don't get me wrong, I am speaking solely about the espresso products.  I have never had their brewed coffee as I am not much of a drip drinker!  I like that they can make every latte with soy milk for those of us non-milk drinkers and I like that the pastries aren't usually too bad.  The tables and coffee fixing station are often dirty and the line is usually out the door, but I wait in it if I really wanna good latte.  Hell, if I am driving all the way to Cambridge I mind as well wait for something good.  When I am feeling really rebellious, I get my coffee here and then walk over to Carburys for food!

Tasty Korean comfort food. 

Kimchi Kitchen is best categorized as a bunsik, or a small casual Korean 'snack' cafe. Like at other bunsiks, the best dishes to get at Kimchi Garden are spicy  rice cakes, gimbab, kimchi fried rice, etc.  Kimchi Kitchen is small and comforting and everything I tried was very flavorful, tasted like it came freshly out of the kitchen, and seemed very authentic. The place can get busy which can make service a bit hectic, but the staff is super friendly so it's all good. 

The ddukbokki spicy rice cakes were great and just spicy enough. The japchae was good too. The soondooboo soft tofu stew had an interesting smokiness to it. I also tried the fried chicken special which was not bad. I did not try the kimchi fried rice, but it looked very tasty. Overall I was pretty happy with the food both in terms of taste and value. The menu is limited which is fine, but I would love to see some seafood options sometime, at least as specials. 

I definitely want to come back!

She is so talented and kind!  This is our second cake made by Terri.  It turned out better than I had anticipated!  I was running a little behind schedule to pick up the cake.  She gave me a courtesy called because she was afraid I had forgotten about the cake and she didn't want me to show up at the dinner party empty handed.  I had a wonderful experience working with Terri and the cake she made was delicious and beautiful!

I am a huge meat-lover, and even I really enjoyed the vegan menu.  The soy BLT is especially delicious. Decent price ($6-7) per sandwich. Also platter combos available. Free hot water, which kept me warm in the cold. Overall, a great way to grab a quick bite or catch up with a friend!

5 stars for the food (could not have tastier food) 3 stars for the interior ( a little dated could use a refresh).  Everything I've had there has been scratch made, the soups, roasted turkey, that is they roast the turkeys for the Turkey club...delicious.  Had some cabbage/chorizo soup...delicious.  My son who hits all the 'fab' burger joints, Shake Shake, Boston Burger, Tasty Burger said the burger here was 'awesome'.  He had his with american, sauteed onion, lettuce, tomato, came with Steak Fries, so they have different fries depending on the meal...what? well all right.  I did not ask if they made shakes or pies, 2 big ingredients for my diner fix, but honestly everything is good, whoever is cooking is skilled and cares about what they are putting out there, straight forward nice waitresses.  Andy's is super solid.  stop by for anything you'll be glad you did.

Found Terri while looking for bakeries on Yelp and I'm so glad that I did! 

I've had 3 cakes made by Terri. She is a lovely lady who is very talented at baking and especially decorating. You give her a picture and she'll re-create it on a cake using her piping skills. Her carrot cake is divine, filled with nuts and raisins with a cream cheese frosting that isn't too sweet. I'd love to try her other cakes in the future. Her prices are very fair considering how much bakeries charge for custom cakes nowadays. 

I'd give her a 5 but I've had some issues getting a hold of her. However, understandable since this does seem like a business that she runs out of her own house so there aren't set business hours.

Amazing food. Great service by very friendly staff who patiently explain dishes for those unfamiliar with Korean food. The food was very fresh and delicious. Will be back.. Pricing was very reasonable too.

Dish rec: bibimbop

This bar RULES in each and every way. Really and truly.
'Nuff said.

Cosi in Kendall needs to take a few lessons in efficiency management. The first time I went with a friend (in October or so), we had to wait more than half an hour for sandwiches, and only then to be told that our shakes weren't available, and would we like a refund? Couple this with the sheer number of workers in the back milling around who have no idea what's going on... I'm surprised they're still in business. 

I was back recently in December, hoping that management had ironed out the kinks in their system, but no dice. The bread is dry and tasteless, their chicken cutlet sandwich is served with cubed chicken. What?Just... say no.

We eat here often, and I really like Kelly, the waitress.  So, maybe I'm not the best reviewer...

Food's pretty good!  Wish they'd spend the time and money it would take to update the place and the menu.  Porter Square *needs* a diner presence, and Andy's is the only contender for now.

Great place to read a good book or catch up on some studying with some ginger lemonade!  Service is consistently fantastic.  Their rewards programs (buy 12? 14? and get 1 free) is nice because your name is saved in their computer and you don't need to carry a card around.

We were a group of 6 (including one precocious 2 year old) and the place was perfect.  If you are looking for a nice, robust cup of joe, an excellently fluffy omelette with delicious fillings and an imaginative menu, this place is NOT for you.

Damien S's review  from 2/8/13 is spot on. 

I got an omelet with onions, peppers, and mushrooms; salsa on top. The mushrooms were canned, and I suspect that the peppers were from a jar since they were incredibly salty, pickled, and faded in color. The onions I think were legit. Would not go that route again, but the others at the table got carb-based stuff which looked great. 

It's a no frills place with excellent service.

Some trivial things to add to the Gots Nots list:

- no splenda
- not substitution-friendly; what you see is what you get
- no french fries, much to the chagrin of our 2 year old breakfast mate

I love diner dives (naturally, I'm from Jersey) and this one definitely makes my A-ok list :-)

My advice for this place is to beat the lunch rush.  I tend to go right at noon or after 1:30 so I have a table to myself.

I love the free wireless and their Bacon, Turkey, Cheddar melt with carrots.

I eat here all the time. Whether I get the salads or the subs, I am yet to have something that I don't like. They recently changed up the brand of their roast beef because the old stuff was so-so. Whoever picked the new brand of roast beef knows what their doing. The staff is always friendly and the owner is up front making sure everyone is getting what they need. From portion to taste this place is on point and a welcome addition to Tech Square.

I spent my St. Patty's Day here yesterday with friends, and had a most awesome time! We had many many Guinnesses and great food.

I love the fact that the waitress was Irish. She was incredibly nice and very attentive to our large group.

The food here was superb. I had the yummiest fish & chips I've had anywhere. The corned beef and cabbage & shepherd's pie also looked really good.

They played good Irish music all day, which got louder as the place got more crowded. The only thing that made this place a less than perfect was that there wasn't live music.
Very good food. I'm veggie and took the salad which was nothing more than vegetables, nothing particular but my friends had various dishes (sorry I don't remember) which were all delicious!

I would actually give this place 5 stars for the sandwiches, but ever since Al's was sadly forced to leave Harvard's campus I've been disappointed more times than not by the fact that Al's closes way earlier than their posted business hours. I got to the store today, June 30, at 2:35pm to find the store completely closed up and it looks like it had been that way for a while. Their sign says they close at 3 so it makes me super sad that they randomly close half an hour early, especially since I'm walking all the way from Harvard just to get these subs.

Really though the sandwiches are awesome and huge, I legitimately spend $40+ every time getting a subs to eat and then have again for later.

Please stay open when you're supposed be open, I'm just trying to give you my moneyyyy!

So I've been in town for almost 24 hours, but I can tell that this will be my second home.  The bartender Chris introduced himself and kept our glasses full, the locals included us in their conversations and invited us to the dance floor, and one guy even gave up his seat so we didn't have to stand.  It's divey, but I love them like that!!!   I'm a fan.

This is my second time ordering from Kimchi Kitchen, and I'm currently writing as I eat my delivered kimchi fried rice and bibimbap. As a Korean, I know Korean food and I have to say that this place is subpar. I wasn't able to snap a pic of the kimchi fried rice before consuming, but it is hugely disappointing that there are barely any visible chunks of chopped kimchi and I don't get why it's seasoned to be EXTREMELY SPICY and has the weird blend of frozen veggies you might get in a Chinese-style fried rice (edamame, green peas, corn, carrot chunks). Not the familiar spice from residual kimchi juice, or from gochujang. But a burning, red-hot unknown spice that is like chili flakes on crack. I had to resort to mixing the kimchi fried rice with the plain rice from my bibimbap to reduce the level of heat. The only redeemable piece in this dish is the well-cooked fried egg over easy.

Also disappointed that no side of the usual miso soup or any banchans were included in the delivery. Instead, at the bottom of the bag are a sad two pieces of Asian candy. 

The first time I came here I ordered the korean fried chicken togo and man, that was a downer also. It tasted like some semblance of Korean-style Orange chicken with another unnamed bright-red sauce unfamiliar to my Korean taste buds and the tiny bits of chicken were hard to find under the heavy breading. 

If you're looking for good authentic Korean food, I wouldn't recommend coming here. If you're looking for decent, "somewhat similar to Korean and this should pass to satisfy my craving" food, I still wouldn't recommend coming here. To conclude, I just crunched on a small rock, proving the poor quality of the meal.

I want you to know that the potatoes I had here were the best breakfast potatoes I have ever had in my life. In. My. Life. Not only did I watch the guy younger than me make them, he put them on my plate seconds after they were done. I think I blacked out at some point because I don't remember what he put in them, but I suddenly looked down and they were gone.
I had the Sante Fe omelet (maybe the Mexican, can't remember) basically it was green peppers, tomatoes and onions sans cheese. Very fluffy, fresh ingredients and overall just super tasty. Prices are amazing as well. I think my dish was like $5.95.
I wish I could go back every week.

Paddy's is one of a kind....it's small, dark (some would say intimate) and everyone there seems to know each other since their childhood days. A good place to go for beers...and only that I must say....

After reading a review about Paddy's being a townie bar, where hot-dogs were the only food option, I decided to check it out. However, much to my disappointment, i quickly found out that there are NO hotdogs...and no food at all....

Nonetheless, a very intriguing place...

Bar review: the bar literally had the worst possible service I've ever encountered anywhere. Ice had better service at places much much busier. It's highly unlikely that I'll be anywhere near this place again and will make sure to tell my friends to avoid this place like the plague. The are places with much better drinks and much much better service.

I attended a family function at the Hacienda Restaurant. My cousin did not want a " house cake". She specifically wanted to order from Cakes by Terri. Terri dropped off the cake herself. 
The cake was appropriately decorated for this particular function. Not only did it look great, it was delicious.
When I found out what it cost, was very surprized. It was significantly lower than any bakery I know.
This woman  genuinely likes her job and it shows by the way she treats her customers.
Their clam chowder is very tasty, but the turkey chili was kind of boring. I'll go on Fridays in the future to get the clam chowder, otherwise this is a bit of a miss.
Tried the sundubu jjigae here and it tasted okay, there wasn't anything particularly special about it!

The burritos are good. The problem is that they overcharge me 90% of the time. I have been coming here for about 6 weeks now, about 3 times a week. I always order the same thing, burrito without cheese and guacamole. Yet, 90% of the time they try to overcharge me for cheese and guacamole. It's dishonest and frustrating to have to argue with the cashiers, even when it's obvious they have made a mistake.
Rumor has it that Ryles is closing its doors.  That's too bad.  I thought it was a good place to see live music and take the occasional Salsa Dance lesson.

It could have been perfect if they:

1. did not put loud and ugly music
2. were able to control a comfortable room temperature
3. do something about the smell

Went for early dinner for my husbands birthday.  The place wasn't at all busy but after waiting over 15 minutes to be asked if we would like to order a drink, we left.  This place is just for show.  I can't speak for the food but based on the short time we were there, I wouldn't recommend it.

1369 is the perfect place to come early in the morning, get a latte, a muffin, and hunker down to study at one of the back tables. Something about this place always makes me feel smarter just by doing my work there. There's just enough people around to feel like you're in good (scholarly) company, but its usually a quiet place.

Al's is known for the best lunch deal in Boston, with high quality sandwich ingredients, fresh baked bread, fast service, and a chips and a drink for a bargain. Now you no longer need to go into Boston to get it! The Kendall area had a sore lack of affordable quick lunch options, and almost nowhere to get a good sub, now Al's has saved the day.

I eat lunch at Al's several days a week, and now that I've been to this location over 50 times I thought I would update my review.

Subs are large or small, but the small is larger than anyone else's large and will fill any big appetite. Order hot subs in the line to the right, and cold subs to the left. They also serve soups, salads, and chicken kabob meals at the far left line.

Here are some of my favorites:
1. Roast Beef with Mayo and BBQ (and provolone, lettuce, and basil)
2. Turkey & cheese add bacon (a ton of tasty crispy bacon makes it well worth the extra cost)
3. Chicken salad add bacon (with lettuce, cheese, and oregano). The best chicken salad sandwich you will ever have.
4. Steak Bellino with american cheese. A decent steak and cheese that melts right into the fresh bread.
5. Hot Pastrami ("swiss and spicy" with swiss cheese and mustard)
6. Chicken Eggplant Parmesan. As the chicken is not breaded, the breaded eggplant adds that needed flavor
7. Pesto Chicken Kabobs. When I'm not in the mood for a sandwich. Awesome homemade pesto chicken breasts on a bed of rice, plus a salad and a loaf of fresh bread. 
8. Meatball parm sub. Highlighted by their excellent homemade sauce.

The chili is also that perfect balance of flavor and spice, meat and beans, and can be added on the side for a big meal or good enough on its own.

Service is still very quick without the insanity and yelling of the downtown locations. There is just enough seating inside, and tables on the sidewalk on nice days.

Cafe zing is a small but bustling cafe located inside one of my favorite local book stores. Browsing piles of books and cards while drinking a fresh cold-brew is great for a hot humid day. The espresso drinks are great and so are he scones. Try a bag of their day old pastries for $1 each!
The cafe doesn't have a huge amount of indoor seating but the there is almost always a seat at the outdoor tables with umbrellas or at one of the work bars. With the free wifi - it is a wonderful place to st and do a little work or read the new book you just bought. 
I recommend the aztec hot chocolate and their Vietnamese soft rolls with peanut sauce. Be sure to try their ginger lemonade if you are looking for something sans caffeine.

I'd eat there every day if I could. The Kimchi jigae is really good, their free tea (barley and green is awesome) and it's extremely reasonable. My family of 3 is usually very full and has leftovers for ~$30. 

It's no frills and is also available via Caviar.

Ok, I know I should not eat to many subs.  But these are so good.  First, some of the best bread around.  Warm and fresh.  The size is 'OMG'; a small gives me two meals!  I have tried the steak sub (shared with my boss), a sub with turkey and cheese and the chicken kabob salad.  All were very good although the steak sub could of used more cheese.

Most of colleagues like the place; some are looking for more meat in the subs.  They seem fine to me but perhaps not to you!  Anywho, a nice refreshing addition to 600 Tech Square.  A affordable lunch meal in the Kendal Square area.

This place reminds me of a bunch of old-time haunts in places like Jersey City.  You cannot come into Paddy's and be anonymous, whether alone or in your group -- it is too small, too bright, and that's just not the vibe here.  You are there in someone's barroom, and you have a certain obligation to be polite and respectful to them and your fellow guests.  You get that down, and this is one of the friendliest places around.  Leave your pretentions at the door and stay a while.

Terrible place. I ordered a steak salad from this place. The steak was not tender--very tough texture and not juicy at all. The amount of salad dressing given for the salad was very small in amount and the flatbread was not fresh. The roof of my mouth was cut by the hard texture of the bread. Try to avoid. Not recommended

Old school no frills diner that hasn't been updated for some modern "ambiance" and that's part of its charm. Basic diner food done well and friendly staff. No wait but we went close to opening. I got the omelette and it was great, home fries could use a bit more seasoning. They have a delicious sounding pancake and waffle selection that we'll be coming back to try.

Super burrito never disappoints! Great size especially for the price and always filling. Not unexpected but they aren't as generous with the guacamole as they are with the rice and beans. The lines can get pretty long, sometimes out the door, usually around 7 or 8 but they move quickly. The Jamaica (Hibiscus Tea) is delicious and would highly recommend as a refreshing drink to add to your meal!

Love the 1369 espresso! Would be a cherry on the top if this location got some nice ceramic coffee cups. This is one of the reasons why I prefer the Inman sq location. 

The interior is nice, lots of tables, outlets, no free Wifi (unless you can use xfinity). The place fills up really quickly, it's a popular coffee shop.

Before going further, I want to acknowledge that the Dumpling House offers great food, the complimentary tea is delicious and the service is quite fast. When I went to the Chinatown location, although it was busy, my husband and I were treated well and everything went smoothly. But the low rating goes to the service my friends and I received at the Cambridge branch. We went there on a Sunday night that was not too busy. After waiting in line for our turn, we were placed at a table and that's when problems began. Our waitress was the rudest. She kept rushing us to order by asking questions like "are you ready yet?" with a very harsh tone. Once we ordered she almost threw the dishes on our table, and towards the end kept walking back to our table to try and yank the plates off us if they looked like we might have finished eating. To top it off, when it was time to pay for the bill, one of us went to the restroom and while we were paying and waiting for our friend, the waitress came back, said thank you and while doing so, literally nodded us away towards the door! I would definitely go back to the Chinatown branch but I encourage the Cambridge one to train their employees better!

Go-to place for college students in Harvard Square. The concept is similar to chipotle - build your own burrito or nacho bowl as you go - but the food is much, much better for the same price point. They also recently moved to a new location and added a bar and a rooftop deck.

I went to Moksa on opening night and it's now my new favorite dining spot! 

The bar has a wonderful ambience, with subdued blue-tinged lighting illuminating the bar. There are two large screens at the bar to appease Boston's ubiquitous sports fans but they're unobtrusive enough to allow for free-flowing conversation amongst those of us who are oblivious to the game. The friendly staff of bartenders encourages a convivial atmosphere (and since it was opening night, they shouted "Cha-ching!" whenever someone ordered one of their "Cha-ching" drink specials, which made the atmosphere fun and festive). 

The small plates allow you to sample several dishes - perfect for sharing or simply those who are indecisive and want to try a little of everything (and the prices make sampling several dishes very manageable - the dishes I tried ranged from $6-$8 dollars each, though some of their noodle and rice dishes were in the $10-$15 dollar range).  I would highly recommend the Roti topped with succulent shortrib braised in mint and the Shao Bing, which is a wonderful blend of steamed bun and savory pork belly. The grilled brussels sprouts have a wonderful, smoky flavor and the orange ginger kimchi served on the side is a great complement. 

The cocktail menu features a good selection of creative cocktails. I had a Silver Peony, which blends sake with cucumber and lavender. The drink is normally served straight up but after another bar-goer warned me that it was quite strong, the bartender offered to make it for me on the rocks. Her suggestion made the drink perfect and I appreciated that she checked back a little later to see how it was and if she could make any adjustments. 

Moksa is definitely off to a great start - check it out before it gets popular and more crowded!

Made a reservation on-line and never heard from them.  A friend called to do the same, never got a call back.  Finally, I called them and got our reservation for a party of 10 for Sunday jazz brunch.  We get there and they have no idea about our reservation.  Luckily, at 10:30 AM we were their first patrons so it was no biggie to pull tables together.  We got our drink orders right away while waiting for the others to show up.   Had to ask for menus to be brought over.   20 min. later our party was complete.  20 min. later the others are still waiting for coffee (what gives?  there were only a half dozen tables there at that point).  The food was prompt and average, it's just the service that was painfully slow.  The music was fab, though.

Love the chickpea fritter sandwich. If you have never been to clover they are awesome! They use a lot of fresh ingredients that are in season and they also have a couple of local beers on tap. They make their own sodas which are pretty unique and they are eco friendly. Nothing but great things to say about them. Keep it up guys.

This place has fallen. So much. Used to come here all the time for two years while working at Harvard. Went back today. So salty. No flavor. So disappointed. I got a chicken burrito with some add-one like guacamole, corn, and sour cream. Customer service was terrible too. Everyone looks and acts miserable and with the worst attitude. Not a place you want to be. Definitely will only ever come back for the rooftop and drinks. Don't waste your time on food. Chipotle makes a better burrito. And that's saying something.

yay boston for having a nice chill jazz brunch!
the vibe in there is awesome, and quite the food selection for brunch. prices are standard, and you are not too squished in the tables!

i would have given 5 stars because the jazz was really chill, but there were only 2 waiters for the entire place. 2!!! they were extremely busy....and definitely made loooots of tip money. but it took a while to get our orders taken. also the food is nothing special.

but the ambiance and jazz brunch thing make it a unique and chill place to come. i called in at 11:45 for a ressie for 12:30 for 2, and got it. yay! brunch for 2 is the way to go.

I come here very often, I always have margaritas. Today I came in and was given the smallest cup I've  ever seen so, I asked " How much is this drink?" ( Knowing it's usually around $6-$7) bartender tells me it's $7. The is two times smaller than what I'm usually given but they're charging the same exact price. RIP OFF!!!!! I truly like coming here but it's the last time I'll drive 20mins out of my way to come to my USED TO BE favorite margarita place. Legit robbery!!! ALSO... if you'd like any type of flavoring it will be an up charge of $1.50.

Great food.  I work all over the Boston area, and eat at many breakfast, lunch establishments, and I have to say this IS the best place in Cambridge, and I love the free parking out back!   Getting hungry writing this review.. Enjoy.

"You really get your money's worth because we use real crab!" I should have known then that something was about to go terribly wrong. Sigh. I'm getting ahead of myself. Let me back up a little bit.

Full disclosure: my friend and I showed up (1) with her LivingSocial voucher and (2) at 5 pm on a Monday. Now, in a perfect world, neither of those things would have any impact on the quality of our dining experience. However, I suspect that issue #2 had a negative effect on the meal that we received.

So, the positives: Moksa is beautiful on the inside. It's very cool and modern. I assume that it's a great place to see and be seen, with tons of trendy young urbanites sipping on wittily named drinks. However, because my friend and I are old, shamelessly hungry working stiffs, we were there at the crack of dinner. The place was dead. No matter -- like I said, shamelessly hungry over here. We started with the chicken lettuce wraps, twice-cooked green beans, and ginger crab fried rice. The wraps were definitely tasty, and the green beans were outstanding. Outstanding, I tell you! I could have happily eaten like three orders of those crispy, oystery, amazingly delicious beans.

Tragedy struck when we dug into our ginger crab fried rice. There was ginger aplenty, and the fried rice galore, but there was a deeply troubling lack of crab. When I think about the fact that we paid $14 for that crabless crab fried rice, bad things start to happen to my blood pressure. It completely tainted the rest of our meal. Yearning something more satisfying to round out our dinner, we got the pork and veggie gyoza. They were good (porky as promised), but nothing could get the bad taste of that non-crab out of my mouth. Also? I feel like everything would have been better if it had come out at a higher temp. Lukewarm food is not on my list of favorite things.

Our server was sweet and attentive, and like I said, the food was generally tasty. That fried rice just made me profoundly unhappy. We'll be back again because I still have a LivingSocial voucher of my own to use. But if I didn't have this voucher? Not so sure I would come back. I'll cross my fingers for a better experience next time.
Decent pub! Centrally located and nice staff! A local feel and the patrons diverse. Prices were pretty average, not dirt cheap but definitely not expensive.

UGH. I have to say that I have had good food here, but the last two times were rock bottom for a simple order of oatmeal.  The first experience gone bad were peaches rock hard BUT the second was a live worm squiggling around the oatmeal and after horrifyingly eating one bite and seeing it, further inspection found that the peach cut up was rotten on one side.  How do you not see that when cutting it up for a customer?
I took cell pix and had my colleagues look at it- it was a major gross out. I think I will save you the disgust and not attach the pix.

The Field is a nice neighborhood Irish pub. It can get busy on Friday and Saturday nights, but I've found it's a great place to hang out at off-times to grab a beer and hang out with friends. The food is also very good, definitely better than most bar food. The bartender is always very friendly.

Please don't think about going anywhere else!! Terri was unbelievable from beginning to end. She was very helpful with designing the cake. She prepared a perfect cake and it tasted delicious! I will be going back to her for many more cakes!

Went here on the recommendation of Boston Magazine. I was very disappointed. The espresso was very weak in flavor. I was told they're trying a new roast. 1369 should go back to what ever they had first or something else. The girl who toke my order was very pleasant. I won't be back.

I LOVE THIS PLACE! So homely and warm, not a huge place but very neat and welcoming. 

Their entrees come in a set, along with a yogurt drink, a box of seaweed and 2 side dishes.  Great value and variety.  Love the bento set style food.

I've had the kimchi rice here as well as the tofu chigae, both were great and authentic!  Seems to be run by a Korean family, professional and polite.

Thanks yelpers!  After a failed plan to go to Craigie on Main (no brunch on Saturdays!) I was in need of a quick back-up breakfast plan.  This place is right around the corner and definitely hit the spot.

This is a small two-person diner, with the food and prices to match.  Be warned, the stools for the counter are set really close so you might want to wait for one of the tables if you or your companion are tall.  

I got two eggs, over medium, with white toast and home fries.  The eggs were a bit overcooked, I would order over easy next time, but the home fries were soooo gooood.   Honestly, some of the best I've ever had.  I also got to try some of my husband's french toast, and that was really good as well.  

I would definitely try this place again, if only to have the home fries.

Espresso is only ok, and there's no cream available to mask the bitterness (in all senses of the word ;-).  Pastries seem nice enough, and the counter workers are sort of friendly.  Ambience is stark.  Ok, stark-ish, though the space is actually quite cool in a retro way--high ceilings and a great storefront.  

On the up side, they do accept LevelUp, which gave me the extra push to try the place out.  After a few visits, I'm a little disappointed that I can find no compelling reason for me to come here from the other side of Harvard Square.

Cambridge needs more independent coffeehouses; this one's just not my speed.

Terri is amazing!  I called her with only a week's notice for my parents surprise 60th birthday party and I needed a cake.  I didn't have a great idea, but just a general concept of a landscape picture I wanted.  I left the artistic vision up to her, and I am so glad I did.  She did an AMAZING job.  I don't think I could even do with pen and paper what she did with frosting.  I'll try to post a picture here.  She is great, and it was only $30!  I gave her 40 because I was so satisfied and other places quoted me around $80 for a similar sized cake completely unpersonalized.  Terri is a true artist, great to work with and I will definitely be back again soon.

What a gem! Al's has the BEST chicken salad I've ever had, just delicious. The prices are extremely reasonable and the service is speedy and friendly.
The shoppe is clean with nice seating. I'll definitely be back.

Update: I actually work at this cafe now, and I can still safely say that I love my fellow staff and our awesome customers to bits. Still kicking myself for only having given us 4 stars in my 2010 review when I was able to un-biasedly give 5 stars.
Came here on a Friday night and it was very empty. Only a few couples dancing. I'd heard great things about this place, but Ryles didn't quite live up to the hype. I'm sticking to Habana Club.

My first day in Boston my friends took me to Ryle's for the jazz brunch.  I really liked the atmosphere and listening to live music was cool.  Apparently it gets really crowded there, so it was nice that we didn't have to wait since it wasn't busy last Sunday.  I had the eggs benedict which were okay.  I'd definitely be down for going again and trying one of their omelettes which looked better.  Our waiter was really nice, though it sometimes took awhile to get his attention to refill our coffee cups.  Overall, I'd recommend!
The staff is not friendly at all.  The place smells and looks old and not taken care of.  If they cleaned up their act perhaps they could compete with the zero competitors in the neighborhood!

Good coffee. Weird layout but kind of cool because it's unique. Pretentious servers. If you ask for a large coffee, expect to be made to feel like you just asked for popcorn and soda at a gourmet steak restaurant. I did, got an explanation about how this wasn't Dunkin donuts coffee, and was told I was part of their customer base that "complains" about this. Grin and bear it as I did. Or just go elsewhere.
It is great to finally have a Korean restaurant in East Cambridge that serves home cooking. The service is fast and friendly. The food is on point. The japchae and kimchi jigae were delicious.

I came all the way from NYC and my friend took me here ! It's wonderful.  Best Chinese restaurant in Boston. I ordered spicy fish, lobster, fish dumplings, Nanjing duck and etc. All are perfect.  The bosses used to be professional chefs.        The environment is nice too.

some secrets!

tuesday: $1 tea day!

last friday of the month: 10% off (sponsored by gogreenstreets.org)

oh, and free wi-fi and hooray for vietnamese fresh rolls!

Barismo is one of my favorite cafés. It's comfortable & friendly. The food (small menu) is really good & unusual, and their coffee is unusually delicious. They use an espresso + hot water method and it just tastes really good. It would be a 5-star review if they had public wifi. But, I can pick up an xfinity hotspot, also it's a good place to sit & work.

What's great about this place is that you can turn on the puke faucet outside, they'll reprimand you a bit, ask you a whole bunch of "are you sure you're all right?"s and "are you okay?"s but STILL let you in and serve you.

Every time I smoke outside, I feel like I'm thisclose to getting into a tussle with one of my friends and we'll be carrying on and they'll playfully push me and I'm tumble backwards with my arms at my sides doing little circles, trying to maintain balance and I'll get bumped onto oncoming traffic, and I'll be constantly rolled back and forth after car upon car comes rolling on top of me.

I almost always get a Black Velvet here which is usually more velvet than black, but I guess they see my face and know I'm one of the two non-Irish people in the bar and I probably don't even notice. And the customers are nice, but I feel like there are a bunch of eager and earnest people there waiting to talk to you and THEY WILL if you look at them once.

I'm beginning to think that this bar was started before they let dames into the place, because the men's room door is right inside the bar. The woman's room, you have to go past a few buckets and mops to this strange hut-type place that you have to walk sideways to. THAT'S CLASS.

Clover is the best! Blue Bottle coffee alone makes this place a must...but wait, there's more!

I had the egg plant and egg sandwich and it was so good. The sandwich was taking a while and everyone was so nice, they gave me all kinds of free food while I waited (and the wait wasn't bad at all). The service can't be beat!

I love that Clover is doing so well in the Boston area, I wish nothing but success to this outstanding establishment.

Oh, I've heard rumors that the owner is a BU grad as well!!

Pleasantly surprised by the value at Al's.  Finishing the "small" in one sitting is really pushing it, unless you want to be in a food coma for the rest of the day. 

The bread is fluffy and chewy.  They call it French bread but it reminds me of the dough they use for rolls at Bertucci's (which are always a favorite).  

In the hot sandwiches, the ingredients are hot.  The sandwich is not toasted.  In the Sicilian Steak and the Pastrami sandwiches, the meat is shaved like the meat in a standard Philly cheese steak.  They are also heavier sandwiches that will satisfy even the most hangry of folks.  

Even at peak lunch rush, when all of the restaurants in Kendall are flooded, I spent less than 10 minutes there.  The line moves quickly and they churn out sandwiches like no other. 

Pro tip: if you're craving a tuna melt, you can order the tuna salad sandwich (usually cold) in the HOT sandwich line, and ask for it with cheese and toasted/melted.

The service was fast and friendly. The food was exactly what I expect and want from a diner: a little greasy, filling, and cheap. The atmosphere was quiet and clean. 

If you want to go out for breakfast on a Sunday morning and not have to wait in line with a bunch of hipsters for overpriced eggs and bacon, this is the place for you.

Damn if they don't have the worst coffee I've had in a long time. It's like three-day-old-gas-station-coffee bad. It tastes like battery acid, and not in a good way.

Or, to put it another way: ABP's coffee blows Cosi's out of the water. And ABP coffee is fucking terrible.
This is a straight up no frills blue collar diner type of place w/ a really cool looking clock. So that's it. Lots of Pats & Celtics photos on the wall.
Love this place, great neighborhood bar to get a beer, love their 5K run also, i make sure i do it every year

My father grew up with Bubby and Peggy Ryan - two of the greatest people ever.  I'm sure Ruth will take great care of the place now and it will continue to be someplace anyone with a smile and a beating heart can feel welcome.
Despite what Yelp says, this place most definitely does not have wifi. Nice place otherwise.

One star for food, three stars for the drinks.  I've been here three times:  Once for a great guerilla gay bar night; once for drinks; and once for dinner.  The first two were fine; the last was terrible.  This place unfortunately needs to get it together if they really want to succeed as a restaurant.  The space is great, and the idea is nice, but the execution is terrible.  The wait staff was an absolute disaster.  We had two waitresses serving our table of four and neither knew what they were doing.  We waited ages for every piece of our meal. One said it was her first day, but after asking for the third time for water and about the dessert, I had lost patience.  They also kept randomly blasting music out onto the patio as though someone couldn't figure out the sound system.  This is really not acceptable when aiming for the swankier atmosphere.  Please Moksa - step it up, I want to see you succeed.

It's a fine bar but I found that if the staff gets in the weeds it's hard to receive good service. I think that with a little bit of a trek from Central Square, there are a few better places with a warmer, laid back feel.

*Bar review only*

Pretty solid spot for drinks with friendly service, a spacious interior and very reasonable noise levels even when it got busy on a Saturday night.  The signature cocktail menu with drinks based on each animal in the lunar calendar was a cool touch and my Red Viper was pretty good.  My gimlet, on the other hand, was mediocre...maybe I shouldn't have ordered one at Drink a little while back as that serves as my baseline =P

Might be worth a shot to try some of the small plates next time.

Yes, it is a swanky vibe--very "hipster." BUT AT WHAT COST?! Granted, the coffee and pastries were tasty. I got a gf corn & blueberry loaf, a pineapple turnover, and two dirty chais. But we waited for an excessive amount time for the chais which were lukewarm and not all the way full. I was missing easily an inch from the top of the cup and when I say lukewarm I mean a few degrees above room temp. The whole excursion cost me $30 (I tipped 20%) and it was most definitely not worth it. Also: warning NO WIFI

It's not that the food isn't good, it is, but what I appreciate most is ordering a large sandwich and knowing it's going to be two meals. Food is expensive around here. I mostly get a grilled chicken with buffalo/red-hot sauce, if not a steak and cheese, both are very good.

the atmosphere is very casual. the place overall is pretty small and I would recommend this place for people who just want to eat alone or with friends too! i ordered their special- cold spicy noodle with bulgogi which was $12 and I was overall satisfied with the food. 

they also sell Korean icecream there and drinks too for dessert lovers!

To start, I don't drink coffee, so I really can't say anything about 1369's bean juice besides that my co-worker says it's "strong like crack."  I do, however, drink iced tea in rather large quantities so I tend to frequent this place in the morning before work.

Frankly, I am disappointed.  It looks like everything I could want in a coffee shop: clever name, sweet mosaic over the door, simple yet cozy interior...  The service is fast and the prices are pretty cheap.  There are some good salads, breads, and scones.  They even play indie music with altogether too much cursing for it's sweet melodies, on occasion.

Unfortunately, the iced tea is bitter.  Stop overbrewing people.  White tea is a delicate thing.  Maybe I will like 1369 better in the winter when I opt for a chai latte or a hot chocolate...  It's harder to mess that up.

The food is organic, fresh and delicious! The customer service is outstanding.  Jonathan took my order, was patient and explained the menu. 

I ordered an eggplant & egg sandwich. He said it would take a few minutes and I told him I'd go wait outside with my girlfriend and dog. When my order was done, Jonathan ran across the street to give me my food. A rare customer service gesture in today's world. 

I highly recommend Clover for anyone who's a vegetarian or looking for something lighter to eat.

Came here to see a band at the first floor jazz club on a Saturday night. The place wasn't the busy when we got in then quickly filled up around 10pm. The band advertised the show starting at 9 but did not take the stage till 10pm. We had rushed from our dinner to make it on time but ended up waiting almost an hour for music. There was an opening singer and the sound of the mic was screeching at points. It was probably a fluke on the part of the sound crew not the acoustics of the place. However when the band finally got going it was worth the wait. 

We had had dinner elsewhere so just got dessert and drinks. The mixed drinks are reasonably priced at $6 but are tiny. Martinis are $8 and are in tiny glasses also. The service was very slow. As the place got full our server was all over the place and we to wave him down to get more drinks. We ordered a chess pie dessert I thought was good. Around 11pm they ran out of Stella then it took another while for another beer to come over. 

The seats themselves were horrible. If you are going to be sitting down for a few hours the seats should at least be comfortable. They were hard as a rock with a paper thin cushion. We didn't even stay till the end of the show cause it was so uncomfortable. I did like the jazz club atmosphere. I would go back to see some random jazz but probably not for a long show.

The coffee was decent, freshly dripped right into my cup. It was flavorful, although a bit bitter. Not a lot of milk options.

The falafel (calling it a chickpea fritter is probably the most obnoxious thing I've seen this week--although they did explain it in response to my review, and their explanation is reasonable) is very tasty. The pita could be better, fresher, so it tastes less stale and also soaks up the sauces more so the sandwich leaks less. But the falafel are made to order, scooped and fried while you wait. The fixings are very fresh. There are pickles and a few other picked things, Israeli salad, and a bunch of hummus. The falafel could have been more evenly distributed. But overall a very tasty, refreshing sandwich with toppings that were a little out of the ordinary (in a good way).

The lavendar lemonade was nice and lavendarey, but the lemonade part of the equation was wanting. It could have been countrytime for all I know. There was no pulp, no tartness, and not even an abundance of lemon flavor, and it had that uniformly cloudy, translucent look. I'd pass on it. The fresh soda syrups are cool, but all they've had for days is beet--clearly no one wants to drink that, since it's just been sitting there unordered.

The service is friendly. They take cards. They respond appropriately and promptly to customer criticism and seem to really care about the customer experience

Welcome to Dwelltime, where procuring coffee is a baffling ordeal! 

I get it. They brew exclusively pour over style coffee with high quality beans and it is a tasty brew. The problem is that my typical visit usually goes something like so: I enter the shop and can't recall if I'm meant to order from the hip barista nearest to the door working the pour over apparatus or the one working the register area. After thoroughly mispronouncing the name of the coffee I've chosen, I am shunted forward into the line to pay.

At this point, inevitably, the person in front of you will be debating whether to get the ginger lime macaroon  or the gluten free cookie (they both look soooooo amazing, you know?). Eventually, I will pay and sidle around the counter to wait for my barista to lovingly, and excruciatingly slowly, finish pouring water over the coffee. I would normally add a splash of dairy, but feel great shame to adulterate this artisanal product.

The coffee really is delicious, but I sometimes (most times) I prefer the convenience of drip coffee without the hip atmosphere.

Overall I loved the food!! the buns and dumplings were amazing. When I went it was packed but the wait didn't last long. The only thing I didn't like was the pan fried noodles, they were very soggy and the edges were too dry. overall the potions were good and the staff is friendly.

After my initial review, folks from Clover reached out to me via Yelp with a very sincere apology and the offer of a couple free breakfast sandwiches plus invitation to a customer breakfast event.  I was impressed not only with the response and care I received but with the breakfast sandwiches as well (and have been back on my dime as well). Tasty, healthy, fresh.  The coffee is good as well, although every cup i've had has had a unique tartness.  I'm not sure this is a critique, but worth noting.  In any case - I think it's safe to say that my initial experience was a fluke and not the norm.   I've enjoyed every experience at Clover since the first and will be back again.

Passed by Dwelltime over the past couple weeks since they opened but went for the first time today. 

As others have noted, the table/chair set-up is a puzzling use of space as they await special permission from the city to have more chairs added. In the meantime, it may not be the best place if you are counting on a table for your laptop, schoolbooks, etc. One thing I love about the space is the large windows, which let in the breeze. There are chairs facing out to the street with the windows, and it's almost like sitting outside. 

Coffee is very good, pastries are also good. I'm not crazy about the price, though - on a second round after finishing our coffee, we purchased two lemonades and two chocolate chip cookies and couldn't believe that it came to $8. 

Overall, this is a great addition to the neighborhood. They've done a lovely job with the interior design (exposed brick, sign from the original auction house on the site, window to the kitchen), and once they get the table/chair set-up worked out it will be even better.

Cosi is a great sandwich chain.  The fresh bread is yummy. Yes, this one in particular is crowded, and yes it's doesn't have mind blowing atmosphere, but it has free wifi and constant freshness in the bread department.  All in all a great choice for lunch, and  great place to fly solo with your computer.

What a cool way to be introduced to a community! 

A friend and I are in Boston for a week, and we wanted to grab some breakfast after coming into Boston last night. We walked around and didn't find much, until we stumbled upon Ryles. What a cool place! On the first Sunday of every month they have the Patricia Adams Ensemble (upright bass, piano, drums and vocal quartet) playing some awesome jazz tunes. 

The food was awesome - just like you would get at home. Try the croissants if you can - delicious! 

A definite place to try if you're in the area!

We are fortunate to work in Cambridge near a wide variety of restaurants. Nearby is Clover, a vegan restaurant. I'm not vegan, but every once in a while, it sounds like a good option - today was one of those days. 

The staff is happy to explain the menu to anyone not in-the-know about vegan options. The menu has some standards but also changes to a degree regularly. 

What we discovered today was that it was difficult to get a dish that wasn't fried or that had soy... The counter person was happy to suggest substitutions and we ended up with a pretty decent lunch. 

My falafel was overdone but, otherwise the food was fresh and tasty. 

Oh yeah - get the Ginger Soda!

If you're looking for something different for lunch give Clover a try.
Pretty mediocre service. Placing my order was like pulling teeth and they were very slow.

Cosi is really just Meh.
Granted, it has a great location right next to the eastern half of MIT's campus. But the food is nothing to write home about.
I've tried most of the sandwiches, and they're all just mediocre.  The personal pizzas (thin crust!) are decent, but they feel premade and really commercial.  The staff is friendly, but the atmosphere feels so cheesy.  They try to be fancy with the "artwork" on the walls, but it really misses the mark.
I'll avoid Cosi when I can, unless I'm forced to eat it if their food gets catered.

Dear Cosi,

In a way I wanted to like you.
I generally prefer the mom & pop places, but I thought I'd give you a chance. I'm sorry to say I almost wish I hadn't. While your chairs may be comfy, the food did nothing for me, at least not on this visit. 

I just stopped in for a snack, and your free wireless drew me in. I ordered a caramel apple cider (which took three employees to ring up) and a brownie bar. The caramel apple cider smelled of chemicals (definitely wasn't all natural), and made me nauseous (no lie).

I do like the fact that you bring the food to the table when its ready. It's nice to not have to worry about leaving your laptop and bags at the table to grab your food and weave through the tables. So that's definitely a plus.

Maybe I'll give you a second chance if I find myself in the area in need of wireless and more food than just a snack. Maybe a nice sandwich will change my outlook, as some of them do sound good. I would like to thank you for giving the vegetarians more than one option, it is greatly appreciated.

Sincerely,
Carrie E.

Have you ever had an espresso so bad that you had to return it? I never have, until Dwelltime. I have had many espressos in my lifetime, but am not an espresso snob whatsoever. The espressos here are disgustingly sour. At first I thought it was a fluke, so had the barista repull the espresso (where we also got some attitude) - but, to no avail. It was still horrid. For the first time in my life, I would not even chug it for the caffeine. I returned the espresso. 

I then got a dirty chai latte, which tasted like water. I felt bad and did not want to cause a scene and return that as well. When I met up with a group of friends, I had them all try the chai latte, and they were all thoroughly disgusted. One of them described it perfectly - "tastes like water with a flavoring of chai"

On top of that, this place is expensive. Don't waste your money here. Plenty of other places around.
Very good Korean popcorn chicken, extremely spicy kimchi fried rice. Low prices, and dishes are large enough to be a full (albeit a little small) meal.

If you have the gift of gab and want to share it, come in and make friends. Plan on coming back over and over! This excellent little establishment will win over those who want good company, laughter, conversation, and a little blarney.

I heard about this place ages ago and would drive by all the time. A few years back I was riding my bike and seriously needed a beer so i went in. I was a little scared at first as everyone in there is a regular and I was not (at that point at least). 
I got a cheap beer and made some friends real quick. I go back on a regular basis and am now one of the regulars, but I will say I tend to be the youngest person in there, and usually the only chick (not that I mind that what so ever)!
It's still a favorite of mine to bike to and drink, but I live a little further away now and it's getting harder to make it home after so many beers.

Burger. Burger. Burger.
The Field is famous for its burger. With a cold German Radeberger this can make your evening. It is better than Bartley's given it has beer. Burgers and beer. It's as easy as that. So when you crave for exactly this combination - well, go to The Field.

It took me a while to try out Clover, almost a year after moving into the Cambridge/Somerville area. Partly, I think it's because there are a decent number of fully vegan eateries in the area. But also, there seems to be some weird gripes about Clover that put me off a little.

However, I tried the Cambridge St location out recently on the way back to Union Square from Kendall. When you walk in, it's a bit confusing what the actual arrangement for ordering/paying is. After a greeting from an employee behind the counter, I discovered ordering is basically anywhere behind the counter, as he just entered it into a phone-like device (along with my payment).

I read a lot about their falafel ahead of time, and where that's more or less their flagship item, I got it in platter form (my partner in sandwich form). We also ordered a side of fries to split. The order took surprisingly long (the menu board actually tells you average wait time, and noted after our orders were placed that it had gone up). However, I wasn't in too much of a rush so not the end of the world by any means.

We took our food back home and I was a little surprised that the platter did not come with pita! Their pita is house made (and popular), so I was bummed it did not come up with the platter. Given that most falafel/Mediterranean platters I've had anywhere else would include that, I think this is something that should be called out a little more. After all, their menu doesn't even cite ingredients (which is pretty unusual for a vegetarian/vegan restaurant). 

While I did not get to have pita with my platter, I had a bite of my partner's and can see what all the fuss is about. It was certainly some of the most pillow-y pita I've had. As for my food, it included a few different types of pickled veggies, a cucumber salad, hummus, tahini, chickpeas, and falafel. Everything was quite good, and surprisingly filling.

My partner really liked her falafel sandwich too, and we were both impressed by the quantity of fries that came with our order. It was definitely share-able.

Next time I check out Clover I'll make sure I'm representing that pita life (whether on platter or sandwich).

Arbitrary restrictions and attitude abound at dwellcrime. Want a half-caf coffee? Nope can't do that. It is apparently not possible to put half a scoop of full-caffeine and half a scoop of decaf in the pour over filter without charging double. Want internet? Not between 9am and 230pm. You are encouraged to make time to dwell at dwelltime, just not too much time. The open windows are great, but without a counter, your legs have no place to dwell.

I thought that the Dumpling House location in Cambridge would be better than the Gourmet Dumpling House in Chinatown. Dumplings were pretty bad. Weird shape, and mostly dough. Not much filling. We also ordered sauteed spinach, sichuan peppercorn chicken, and fried tofu. The peppercorn chicken tasted okay, but the way that they fried it didn't seem right. The spinach was obviously good because it's hard to mess up very simple dishes. Tofu didn't have much taste. Maybe living in China spoiled me and gave me unrealistic expectations. Menu definitely is authentic, but quality isn't that great.

It's 2015 and we've come a long way to elevate coffee in this country.  For an independent coffeehouse to not brew coffee to order (no hand pour, no French press, no vac pot, clever or aero press) is odd and disappointing.  This place even buys good-quality beans (Barringtons and SquareOne) that mostly can't be tasted in the shop save one that sits in a pot for an unknown amount of time however good the intentions of those who run the shop. They know brewed coffee degrades even 10-15 minutes after brewing given the several hundred flavor compounds it has.

For a restaurant or other, non-coffee-centric business, pre-brewed coffee is more typical.

A 45-minute limit on WiFi isn't very customer friendly or sensible (doesn't prevent camping) but different shops will have different policies on that.  More unusual is a coffee shop that doesn't treat good coffee with enough respect to even brew it freshly to order.

My friend and I were looking for a place to grab a quick bite to eat in the area and found ourselves in front of Clover Food Lab... we had never heard of it and weren't even sure it was a restaurant, but it was pouring rain so we stepped inside and the girl at the counter explained the concept. We both got the soy BLT which was amazing. The next day, we went back and I got the chickpea fritter sandwich, also amazing. I have a feeling we'll be back there soon..

I'm hooked on the free bread samples.. I don't usually buy lunch while at work, but I'll buy coffee on a regular basis and theirs is great.  Kendall Square has 2 AuBonPains, 2 Starbucks, a Rebeccas Cafe and a Dunkin Donuts, but I keep returning to Cosi for coffee because it's a rich dark roast and the bread is addictive. The staff is great and it's a nice place to just chill. (There's a nice and quiet little back room.) The menu change often some of it's very good, some of it is just eh.  Do try the coffee and snack bars and hang around for the bread to come out of the oven.

Well to start off the atmosphere is very nice. My friends and I have been here 3 times and the food is still horrible.  The sushi is okay, you can find better tasting sushi at shaws. I don't know how someone can screw up fried rice but I was not impressed. To make matters worse the service is crap and it was slow.  I really hope they will hire  new chef's that cook with love and a GM that can control the FOH and BOH. Once that happens this restaurant has a good opportunity to be great. So please owner take consideration of rebuilding the FOH and BOH and controlling the line.  Start by improving the food and services.  The night club is probably the only thing I enjoy when coming here which shouldn't be the reason Moksa should strive themselves on.
I owe Al's this review. I spent a summer in Cambridge and I tried Al's hot sandwiches many times and made a lot of memories with my friends. Absolutely delicious and I wish they were in DC too!

We just had breakfast here and waited 45 minutes for our food. The service was terrible and the owner refused to talk to us when we asked for the manager.
